source.summary: Preflight safety check requests are being billed against tenant quota and causing 429s for interactive traffic
source.description:
Issue summary: Customer runs a mixed workload where each interactive chat request is preceded by a lightweight 'preflight' safety/scoring check. After enabling a preflight microservice, FinSight started seeing persistent 429 throttles for low-latency interactive chat traffic. Impact: end-users get immediate 429s for short chat requests despite overall tenant usage being within expected bounds. The customer reports that each chat interaction produces two quota decrements in the console: one for preflight and one for the actual generation.

Expected behavior: preflight checks are lightweight and should either be exempt from tenant token accounting (internal preflight) or billed at a small fixed cost, not double-counting the real generation request. Observed behavior: preflight + generation both decrement tenant token buckets as full requests, leading to tenant quota exhaustion and 429s that affect production chat traffic.
source.impact: Customer-facing interactive experiences are intermittently blocked with 429s during normal usage peaks. Several paying enterprise customers have reported degraded UX and one internal partner escalated to SRE due to SLA concerns.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Customer has preflight service calling /v1/preflight followed immediately by /v1/chat.completions
2) Send 1000 paired preflight+chat requests over a sustained 2 minute window from a single API key, mixed concurrency (50 concurrent connections),
3) Watch tenant token ledger and rate-limit counters in Console, observe two decrements per end-user interaction,
4) Continue until tenant hits quota and subsequent chat requests receive 429s with X-RateLimit-* headers showing depleted tokens
logs: 2026-03-10T14:05:02Z EDGE route=/v1/preflight key=acct-FIN-01 inc=token_count:512
2026-03-10T14:05:02Z EDGE forwarded /v1/preflight -> origin cluster=preflight-us-east (status=200)
2026-03-10T14:05:02Z ORIGIN route=/v1/chat.completions key=acct-FIN-01 inc=token_count:512
2026-03-10T14:05:03Z RATE-LIMITER tenant=FIN-01 tokens_remaining=0 reason=window-exhausted
2026-03-10T14:05:03Z CLIENT 429 response returned to api-key acct-FIN-01 request_id=ff12ab34
investigation: Initial triage (Support): Confirmed client is issuing a preflight request prior to each chat call and both show up in customer Console usage charts as full requests.
SRE check: Rate-limiter metrics show identical per-request accounting for /v1/preflight and /v1/chat.completions (both increment tenant token counters by the estimated token count).

Edge routing hypothesis: /v1/preflight should have been marked as an internal/exempt path but the recent edge routing change (edge config v22.4) introduced a path-level header rewrite that removed X-Redwood-Internal, causing preflight to be treated as a normal billed request.
Correlation: the timing of customer enablement of preflight + our edge config rollout matches the start of reports (2026-03-09 22:00 UTC).

Repro on staging: Reproduced paired accounting in staging by sending preflight+chat and watching ledger increments. Adding X-Redwood-Internal:true on the preflight request causes it to be ignored by tenant accounting (workaround).
Risk assessment: High customer impact (enterprise chat UX), quota exhaustion can cascade to other tenants if shared pool misconfiguration present.
comments: Ava Chen (Support) 2026-03-10: Raised by FinSight support engineer. Asked for request samples, API keys, and timestamps. Provided initial guidance to throttle preflight frequency.
Diego Marquez (SRE) 2026-03-10: Confirmed double-counting in rate-limiter metrics. Noted edge config change v22.4 as likely root cause. Requested edge logs for affected time range.
FinSight-Eng (customer) 2026-03-10: Provided sample request pair and said disabling preflight reduces 429s immediately but is not an acceptable long-term solution.

Diego Marquez (SRE) 2026-03-11: Workaround validated — adding header 'X-Redwood-Internal: true' to preflight requests prevents billing. Preparing patch to restore internal-header preservation in edge routing.
resolution: Workaround (prod): Customer instructed to set X-Redwood-Internal:true for preflight requests until server-side patch is deployed. Support provided a curated nginx sidecar snippet for their SDK to add the header.

Short-term fix: Reverted the edge config change that stripped internal header from preflight paths in us-east canary (edge rollback v22.4->v22.3.1). Canary observed for 6 hours with no new double-counting reports.

Permanent fix: Edge PR github.com/redwood-inference/edge/pull/7821 includes tests to ensure /v1/preflight and /internal/* paths preserve internal headers and are exempt by default; target merge and staged rollout 2026-03-13.

Post-action: Added automated e2e test to CI that simulates preflight+generation flow and asserts only generation increments tenant ledger. Added a console note explaining preflight accounting behavior and a recommended SDK header for internal calls.
Follow-up: Postmortem scheduled and linked; SRE to review edge routing change process to add a gate for internal-header preservation.
next_steps: Merge and roll out PR to edge (priority): Diego assigned to coordinate canary rollout and monitor rate-limiter metrics in us-east.
Customer comms: Support to send an incident summary and expected timeline for the fix to FinSight by 2026-03-12.
Billing check: Finance to validate whether any overbilling occurred due to the double-counting and if credits are required (cc: billing@redwood).

source.summary: SDK retry storm + small connection pool causing serialized streaming requests and intermittent inference timeouts
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a recent regression in perceived inference latency and intermittent request timeouts for streaming responses. The customer is on a Dedicated deployment and uses our official Go and Python SDKs behind an internal gateway. Impact: API calls that previously returned streaming tokens within ~120–200ms now see long tails up to 6–10s or outright 504/ETIMEDOUT for some requests.

Observed behavior:
- Increased end-to-end latency for streaming calls from a subset of customer hosts beginning 2026-03-09 02:00 UTC.
- Spikes of 429/502/504 correlated with client-side retries and connection churn.
- Host-level connection pools show active connections ~= configured max_connections_per_host, while requests queue on the client.

Expected behavior:
- Streaming responses begin delivering tokens within expected latency envelope; client retries/backoff should not serialize requests behind a small connection pool.

Environment:
- Customer: FluxHealth (dedicated)
- Region: us-east (customer Dedicated capacity pool)
- SDKs: go-sdk v1.6.3, python-sdk v2.0.1 (custom wrapper used in production)
- Gateway: Envoy sidecar with connection pooling to Redwood API

Steps to reproduce (customer repro):
1) From a customer host with default go-sdk config (max_connections_per_host=8, max_retries=6, backoff_initial_ms=50, backoff_multiplier=2, no jitter) start 100 concurrent streaming requests to redwood-open-7b-v2.
2) Observe client-side queueing and a subset of requests timing out after 10s.
3) Reduce max_connections_per_host to 4 and repeat — serialization worsens and tail latency increases.

Logs / traces (redacted):
- Client logs show sequences: "request queued (pool exhausted) -> retry immediate -> socket closed -> retry -> request queued" repeatedly.
- Edge gateway (Envoy) logs show connection churn to the API and many HTTP/1.1 connection resets.
- Serving runtime traces show some requests arriving in bursts but no host-side CPU/GPU saturation during observed windows.

Initial hypothesis:
- Customer SDK configuration (high retry count with aggressive backoff multiplier but NO jitter + small connection pool) is leading to synchronized retry attempts that serialize behind the small connection pool. This effectively causes head-of-line blocking and perceived inference timeouts even though the backend is healthy. Envoy TCP connection churn exacerbates the problem by reducing available idle connections.

Customer impact and business context:
- FluxHealth runs interactive clinical-assistant features; long tail latency and timeouts are causing user-facing failures and a degraded product experience. They are on a committed Dedicated plan and have SLA expectations for latency and availability.
source.investigation_notes:
2026-03-10 09:15 UTC - Support triage: verified customer stack and observed synthetic reproduction with customer's wrapper.
2026-03-10 11:40 UTC - SRE validated that dedicated pool GPUs show normal utilization and no infra-level throttling during the window.
2026-03-10 14:05 UTC - Reproduced locally by running 200 concurrent streaming requests with SDK config mirroring customer; observed client-side queues and serialized request delivery.
2026-03-11 08:30 UTC - Confirmed Envoy upstream connection_reuse settings were default; connection resets were frequent when client created/destroyed persistent connections due to retry behavior.
Hypothesis refined: synchronous retries without jitter + short TCP keepalive causing newly retried requests to open/close sockets -> temporary connection starvation.
source.temporary_workaround: Advise customer to: 1) lower max_retries (e.g., to 2) 2) increase max_connections_per_host to 32 3) enable jitter on SDK backoff (randomized +/- 25%) 4) enable HTTP/2 persistent connections through Envoy to reduce connection churn. Provided sample Go config and Python snippet. Customer applied changes and saw immediate reduction in tail latency in trials.
source.resolution: In-progress: Provided recommended SDK configuration changes and an Envoy config snippet to enable HTTP/2 upstream and longer keepalive. Customer reporting improvement after applying client-side changes (reduced retries and larger pool). SRE to work with infra team to add a server-side detection/alert for excessive client-side retry storms and add guidance to SDK docs about recommended defaults for dedicated deployments. Planned follow-ups: 1) PR to SDK to enable jitter by default in v1.6.4 (linked) 2) Confluence runbook update for "Mitigating client-side retry storms" 3) Postmortem if we observe recurrence once changes roll out.
source.resolution_notes: Temporary mitigation successful for customer's traffic pattern; final SDK fix and docs scheduled. No code change required on Redwood serving for now. Recommend formalizing recommended SDK defaults for Dedicated customers in onboarding checklist.
comments: 2026-03-10 10:05 - Maya Chen (Support): Opened ticket after customer reported elevated streaming tails and timeouts. Requested configs and logs.
2026-03-10 11:12 - FluxHealth (Customer): Provided SDK configs and sample request traces; noted increase began after traffic spike on 2026-03-09.
2026-03-10 11:45 - Ethan Park (SRE): Verified backend hosts healthy; suspect client-side pooling/retry behavior. Asking customer to run repro with lowered retries.
2026-03-11 09:00 - FluxHealth: Applied recommended changes (max_retries=2, max_connections_per_host=32, enable jitter) in staging and observed tail latency improve from ~6-9s to 200-300ms.
2026-03-12 12:20 - Ethan Park: Filed SDK PR to enable jitter by default and to add docs for dedicated deployment recommendations (PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/4123).
2026-03-12 15:30 - Maya Chen: Marking ticket In Progress while SDK PR and runbook work continue; customer remains available for follow-up testing.

source.summary: High attach latency and throughput collapse from small-chunk inference stream CQ saturation
source.description:
Issue summary: BrightWrite (dedicated pool) reported a mid-morning collapse in steady-state throughput for multiple batched routes. Impact: p50/p95 token throughput drop ~40-70% for routed models and p99 attach latency spiking to 1.2s from baseline ~80ms. Customer-facing inference requests saw elevated 5xx attach timeouts and increased fallbacks to smaller model variants. Environment: prod us-west dedicated pool, heterogeneous GPU hosts (A100 80GB + A10 mixed), customer traffic characterized by many short inference streams (~12-32 tokens) at high qps.

We observed a pattern inconsistent with normal autoscaler signals: autoscaler did not trigger additional hosts despite attach latency rising. Support triaged as potential noisy-tenant + kernel-level IO backpressure (PCIe completion queue saturation) affecting attach path and the serving scheduler fairness.

Impact details: Customer reported 30% drop in successful completions during business-hour window, increased errors correlated with sudden bursts of thousands of small requests per second.

Runbook links above for quick reference.
steps_to_reproduce: Warm up a dedicated pool with mixed A100/A10 hosts in us-west-2 and pin modelos redwood-1.2-vicuna-13b to the pool.
From a single tenant client, flood the endpoint with small inference requests (payloads 8-32 tokens) at sustained 3k-5k rps for 10+ minutes.
Observe host-level pciutil and iostat for stalls, and check per-host /proc/interrupts and perf events for completion queue depth.
Measure attach latency and scheduler queue depth on the router and edge-proxy; correlate with GPU attach failures and autoscaler metrics.
logs: 2026-03-10T09:42:03Z host-gpu-17 kernel: pci 0000:65:00.0: Completion queue depth exceeded threshold, cq_depth=512, max=256
2026-03-10T09:42:05Z serving-runtime: attach request timeout (2000ms) for tenant=brightwrite, route=/v1/generate, model=redwood-1.2-vicuna-13b
2026-03-10T09:42:07Z scheduler: enqueue latency spike: p99=1.05s, pending_batches=46
2026-03-10T09:42:08Z autoscaler: host_free_slots=0, observed_attach_latency=1.1s, decision=hold (no-scale) due to short-term qps volatility
investigation_notes: Found correlation between high rate of very short requests (<=32 tokens) from a single tenant and elevated PCIe completion queue (CQ) metrics on A100 hosts.

Host kernel reports show CQ depth growth leading to longer interrupt handling and increased attach syscall latency; NVLINK not primary culprit — PCIe CQ path saturated by many micro I/O completions from repeated small memcpy ops.
Serving scheduler fairness algorithm gave equal weight to many tiny streams causing frequent context switching and inefficient batch formation; combined effect produced long tail attach waits.

Autoscaler logic uses short-window spike suppression and requires sustained increase in queued work to add hosts; because each request was short-lived, autoscaler judged noise and did not scale until latency already degraded.
We reproduced locally with a generator and observed same CQ behavior within 8-12 minutes at sustained small-request QPS >3k on similar host mix.
comments: 2026-03-10 10:05 - Hector (SRE): Noticed kernel CQ warnings across 3 hosts in the pool. Opening host-level debug snapshots and increasing pci driver logging.
2026-03-10 11:12 - Mateo (Eng): Reproduced in staging with small-chunk generator. Confirmed CQ growth pattern and CPU interrupt saturation. Filed PR to add aggregation at sidecar (PR#4721).
2026-03-10 14:22 - CS (Lina): Customer reports partial recovery after temporary rate limit applied from BrightWrite. They need ETA for permanent fix and ask whether re-routing to other pool helps.

2026-03-11 09:30 - Mateo (Eng): Deployed sidecar coalescing patch to canary hosts in eu-west. Early telemetry shows 22% reduction in host interrupts and improved p50 attach latency. Planning prod rollout today after validation.

2026-03-12 08:18 - Ava (Support): Informed customer of canary results and scheduled maintenance window for prod mitigation. Customer accepted temporary backpressure configuration until full rollout completes.
resolution: Mitigation applied (2026-03-11): Enabled request coalescing at sidecar for small inference requests (50ms aggregation window) for BrightWrite tenant and set soft per-tenant concurrency cap to 1200 rps to reduce CQ pressure.
Patch rolled (2026-03-12): Kernel-level pci driver tuning (PR#988) deployed to prod hosts in us-west-2 to raise safe CQ watermark and reduce interrupt storms under micro-IO workloads.

Autoscaler changes (pending): Proposal to add CQ_depth and attach_latency as autoscaler signals, and relax short-window noise suppression for dedicated pools with consistent short-request patterns. Follow-up ticket created.
Customer impact: Throughput recovered to ~90% of baseline and p99 attach latency dropped to ~180ms after mitigations. Customer agreed to staged rollback tests and telemetry monitoring for 48h.

Follow-up items: (1) Instrument CQ_depth metric across all hosts and add alert at 70% watermark, (2) add tenant-level policy to enable coalescing for other customers with similar patterns, (3) update runbook with reproduction steps and recommended tenant-side batching guidance.

source.summary: Post-migration edge proxy: intermittent 429s for mixed API keys and signed-url callbacks
source.description:
Issue summary:
After a scheduled edge proxy migration (blue->green) on 2026-03-09, the customer reports intermittent 429 responses for a subset of traffic that uses a mix of persistent API keys and signed URL callbacks. Impact: several ETL workers and webhook callbacks saw periodic failures for ~20 minutes during a heavy ingest window, causing backlog and retries downstream.

Impact:
- Acme Analytics batch ingest pipeline experienced ~18% failed request rate during the window, causing requeues and delayed downstream processing.
- Customer reported billing and operational disruption; they are on a Dedicated plan and requested immediate assistance.

Environment:
- Customer Dedicated pool in us-west-2, routed through new edge-proxy cluster (ep-usw-3).
- Mix of long-lived API keys (service accounts) and short-lived signed URLs generated by their ingestion service.
- HTTP/2 multiplexing between edge and origin observed in traces.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Generate a signed-upload URL from the customer app.
2) Start 40 parallel uploads using both signed URLs and API-key-authenticated PUTs to the same tenant.
3) Observe intermittent 429s on ~1 in 20 requests during high concurrency.

Observed behavior:
- 429s return with x-rate-limit headers showing token window at 0 for tenant-level bucket.
- Many 429s appear correlated with short bursts of TCP connection resets during proxy cutover.
- Some successful requests share identical Authorization header value but different socket metadata (client IP/port).

Expected behavior:
- Mixed authentication flows should be accounted properly and not double-count into the same short-window burst unless tenant capacity is exhausted.

Logs/headers attached by customer (truncated):
- x-rate-limit-tenant: 0/300
- x-rate-limit-window: 10s
- Authorization: Bearer sk-prod-ACME-xxx
- Signed-URL: X-Amz-SignedHeaders...

Notes on severity:
- P1/Sev1 because production ingestion is degraded and customer is on Dedicated pool with SLA expectations.
source.investigation_notes:
Initial triage by support (Lina) and SRE (Mateo):
- Correlated spike exactly with the edge proxy cutover timestamp (2026-03-09T23:42Z).
- Edge telemetry shows short-lived connection resets (~TCP RST) across a subset of upstreams during shifting routing. Reset pattern caused client reconnections to land on different proxy worker with new ephemeral socket metadata.
- Rate-limiter calculates token usage partially keyed by (tenant_id, connection_fd) in the current hotfix branch; reconnections caused duplicate accounting in the short refill window for signed-url callbacks that re-auth immediately after reconnect.
- Signed URLs are stateless and reused in customer flow; when reconnection happened rapidly, the origin observed parallel in-flight operations that the rate-accounting path treated as multiple unique actors rather than deduping by tenant+api-key identity.
- No evidence of tenant quota being legitimately exceeded by sustained traffic; this looks like transient misattribution triggered by simultaneous reconnects + mixed auth types.

Repro attempts by SRE in staging:
- Simulated reconnect storm with combined API-key and signed-url traffic reproduced 429 spike when ephemeral-connection sharding is enabled.
- Disabling connection-level sharding eliminated duplicate counting in the test.

Temporary mitigation provided to customer:
- Rolled back edge-proxy routing flag to previous stable pool for Acme tenant.
- Increased tenant burst window from 10s to 30s as stop-gap to reduce sensitivity to short reconnect storms.

Proposed remediation:
1) Fix rate-accounting to canonicalize by tenant_id + effective_auth_identity for short windows, treating signed URL callback requests as the same tenant identity when signed URL maps to the tenant. (PR proposed: github.com/redwood-inference/edge-proxy#765)
2) Add a small dedupe cache (100ms) for rapid reconnects to avoid double-counting.
3) Improve telemetry to emit reconnect-origin metadata in rate-limiter metrics for easier future triage.

Next steps:
- Eng to land PR for canonicalization and dedupe cache (owner: Mateo).
- Post-deploy smoke test: run mixed-auth reconnect simulation in staging and validate no artificial 429s.
- Schedule a follow-up with Acme to confirm backlog cleared and no further throttles observed.
- Update runbook to include edge-proxy cutover checklist item to disable connection-shard flag for tenants undergoing large concurrent signed-url traffic during migration.
comments: 2026-03-10 09:12 - Lina Chen (Support): Received initial report from Acme. Customer provided curl traces, sample headers, and request ids. Escalating to SRE due to Dedicated SLA.
2026-03-10 10:05 - Mateo Alvarez (SRE): Correlated with our proxy rollout window. Starting staging repro to validate whether reconnection pattern causes duplicate accounting.

2026-03-10 13:22 - Mateo Alvarez (SRE): Confirmed repro in staging with reconnect storm. Temporary mitigation: revert routing flag for Acme tenant and increase burst window. Notifying customer of rollback.
2026-03-11 08:40 - Support (Lina Chen): Customer confirmed errors dropped to <0.5% after rollback and burst-window increase. They are still seeing increased latency for some callbacks but no 429s.
2026-03-12 14:55 - Eng (Mateo Alvarez): Draft PR for accounting fix created; running unit tests and additional integration checks. Target deploy in next canary wave.
2026-03-12 15:10 - Lina Chen (Support): Scheduling follow-up call with Acme for 2026-03-13 to validate backlog drain and post-fix behavior.
source.resolution: Workaround applied (routing flag rollback and burst-window increase). Fix in progress (rate-accounting canonicalization + reconnect dedupe). Pending deploy and customer validation.

2) Start 40 parallel uploads using both signed URLs and API-key-authenticated PUTs to the same tenant.
3) Observe intermittent 429s on ~1 in 20 requests during high concurrency.

Observed behavior:
- 429s return with x-rate-limit headers showing token window at 0 for tenant-level bucket.
- Many 429s appear correlated with short bursts of TCP connection resets during proxy cutover.
- Some successful requests share identical Authorization header value but different socket metadata (client IP/port).

Expected behavior:
- Mixed authentication flows should be accounted properly and not double-count into the same short-window burst unless tenant capacity is exhausted.

Logs/headers attached by customer (truncated):
- x-rate-limit-tenant: 0/300
- x-rate-limit-window: 10s
- Authorization: Bearer sk-prod-ACME-xxx
- Signed-URL: X-Amz-SignedHeaders...

Notes on severity:
- P1/Sev1 because production ingestion is degraded and customer is on Dedicated pool with SLA expectations.
text:
Issue summary:
After a scheduled edge proxy migration (blue->green) on 2026-03-09, the customer reports intermittent 429 responses for a subset of traffic that uses a mix of persistent API keys and signed URL callbacks. Impact: several ETL workers and webhook callbacks saw periodic failures for ~20 minutes during a heavy ingest window, causing backlog and retries downstream.

Impact:
- Acme Analytics batch ingest pipeline experienced ~18% failed request rate during the window, causing requeues and delayed downstream processing.
- Customer reported billing and operational disruption; they are on a Dedicated plan and requested immediate assistance.

Environment:
- Customer Dedicated pool in us-west-2, routed through new edge-proxy cluster (ep-usw-3).
- Mix of long-lived API keys (service accounts) and short-lived signed URLs generated by their ingestion service.
- HTTP/2 multiplexing between edge and origin observed in traces.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Generate a signed-upload URL from the customer app.
2) Start 40 parallel uploads using both signed URLs and API-key-authenticated PUTs to the same tenant.
3) Observe intermittent 429s on ~1 in 20 requests during high concurrency.

Observed behavior:
- 429s return with x-rate-limit headers showing token window at 0 for tenant-level bucket.
- Many 429s appear correlated with short bursts of TCP connection resets during proxy cutover.
- Some successful requests share identical Authorization header value but different socket metadata (client IP/port).

Expected behavior:
- Mixed authentication flows should be accounted properly and not double-count into the same short-window burst unless tenant capacity is exhausted.

Logs/headers attached by customer (truncated):
- x-rate-limit-tenant: 0/300
- x-rate-limit-window: 10s
- Authorization: Bearer sk-prod-ACME-xxx
- Signed-URL: X-Amz-SignedHeaders...

Notes on severity:
- P1/Sev1 because production ingestion is degraded and customer is on Dedicated pool with SLA expectations.

source.summary: Batcher synchronization glitch causing periodic p95/p99 latency surges and intermittent 504s for short streaming requests
source.description: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours NimbleDocs (dedicated customer) has reported recurring p95/p99 latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts affecting short streaming requests. These occur in bursts approximately every 5-8 minutes and correlate with a rise in batcher flush latency on a subset of nodes. Impact: Customer reports degraded UX for their chat widget during peak hours; some requests hit 504 and are retried by clients. Frequency: spikes observed ~6–12x per hour during the incident window. Initial hypothesis: a synchronization edge-case in the continuous batcher interacting with KV cache warmup/prefetch locks under low-concurrency short-stream traffic.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use a short-streaming workload (average 12–30 tokens) pinned to redwood/opt-2.7b-quant on a dedicated pool in us-east
2) Generate steady low-QPS connections from ~50 concurrent connections that alternate short bursts (simulate chat idle-resume behavior)
3) Observe p95/p99 latency and 504s in request logs and serving-runtime dashboards over ~30 minutes
4) Reproduce more reliably by toggling KV cache prefetch feature flag on the node to true (observed correlation in production)
5) Compare node-level batcher flush latencies before and during spike events
source.logs: Excerpt from serving-runtime logs (2026-03-11T15:22:05Z): [node-id=us-east-4b] WARN batcher: flush_delayed duration=254ms reason=sync_lock_wait; [node-id=us-east-4b] ERROR stream: upstream_send_timeout id=abc123 status=504; Correlated metric: batcher.flush_latency.p99 rose from 18ms to 420ms at 15:22:05Z. KV cache prefetch latency spike seen: kv_cache.prefetch_ms.p99 410ms.
source.investigation_notes:
Observations:
- The latency bursts line up with increases in batcher.flush_lock_waits and kv_cache.prefetch_ms on a subset (~8%) of dedicated pool nodes.
- Short-stream requests are disproportionately affected because they rely on fast flushes; a delayed flush pushes them into the next micro-batch causing high tail latency and occasional client-side timeouts.
- Nodes with the issue report intermittent 'gc pauses' unrelated to application GC (kernel-level scheduling pauses tied to synchronous prefetch IO path).
Hypotheses considered:
- Lock convoy caused by synchronous KV prefetch on batch flush path when cache miss rate spikes for certain tenant prefixes.
- Timer skew in the continuous-batcher loop causing bunching of flush operations across shards.
- A recent micro-optimization (PR #5213) changed prefetch concurrency and may have surfaced this under dedicated low-QPS traffic.
Investigations run:
- Correlated Prometheus metrics (batcher.flush_latency.*, kv_cache.*) across nodes; flagged nodes show correlated spikes.
- Reproduced locally by running the prefetch path synchronously and forcing ephemeral cache misses; observed similar p99 jumps.
- Reviewed related PRs and found PR #5213 (prefetch concurrency change) merged 2026-03-02 onto serving-runtime branch.
comments: 2026-03-10 10:12 - Support (Arielle Kim): Customer reported increased p99 latency in the chat widget with a few 504s. Collected sample traces and logs; escalated to SRE.

2026-03-10 13:40 - SRE (Mateo Alvarez): Identified pattern on ~3 nodes in NimbleDocs dedicated pool. Batcher flush latency spikes precede 504s. Tentative hypothesis: synchronous kv prefetch holds flush lock longer.
2026-03-11 09:02 - Eng (Priya Nair): Reproduced locally with prefetch concurrency forced to 1; p99 jumps reproduced. PR #5213 noted; rolling back prefetch concurrency as a canary in staging.

2026-03-11 14:30 - Support (Arielle Kim): Informed customer about ongoing investigation and temporary mitigation plan (node drain + staged rollback). Customer acknowledged; chasing acceptance for immediate node drain.
2026-03-12 02:05 - SRE (Mateo Alvarez): Started rolling drain of 4 affected nodes; replaced nodes show stable batcher.flush_latency.p99 ~20ms. Monitoring for recurrence.
source.resolution: Workaround: Drained and reprovisioned the small subset of affected dedicated nodes and temporarily reverted the prefetch concurrency change in the staging build. Long-term fix: Eng to land a patch (PR #5299) that moves KV prefetch off the batcher flush critical path and adds jittered flush timers to avoid synchronized flush bunching. Follow-ups: schedule a canary rollout to dedicated pools and add an alert (batcher.flush_lock_waits > 100ms persistent) to pager duty. Customer impact mitigated; awaiting full rollout and postmortem.
source.notes: Postmortem to be created after full rollout. Observability tasks: add a per-tenant prefix metric for cache-miss-triggered-prefetch to help root-cause similar issues in future.

source.summary: Intermittent 5xx wave from TLS-resume-ticket exhaustion combined with HTTP/2 negotiation mismatch between API gateway and runtime
source.description: Issue summary: Starting 2026-03-10 09:12 UTC NimbusHealth observed repeated 5xx errors for short periods (~2-8 minutes) across multiple chat and embedding routes. The API gateway returned 502/503 and upstream serving runtimes reported rapid connection resets and ephemeral worker evictions. Impact: customer-facing requests intermittently failed (up to 18% error rate during the peak window) causing degraded application experience for their enterprise workflows. Environment: production (us-east), mixed-hosted and dedicated tenant; incident coincided with a tenant priority reconfiguration from NimbusHealth account team.
source.impact: External: NimbusHealth experienced elevated 5xx rates across chat and embeddings endpoints, occasional streaming disconnects. Internal: apigw saw accept_queue growth and increased TLS handshake latency; several runtime pods restarted due to connection churn leading to transient capacity reduction.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use a high-concurrency client to open many short-lived streaming connections (keepalive disabled) to chat route for NimbusHealth tenant. 2) Trigger a tenant priority reshuffle via admin console (simulate same weight/priority change). 3) Observe TLS handshake errors in apigw and intermittent 502/503 responses. Note: reproduces reliably when TLS ticket rate is high and client keepalives are low.
source.logs: apigw logs: repeated 'tls: session ticket cache exhausted' and 'http2: stream aborted before response received' entries. Runtime logs: 'connection reset by peer' followed by 'ephemeral-worker eviction: rapid reconnects' and short crashes of the runtime proxy. Metrics: accept_queue length spiked to 85% of capacity; TLS handshake p95 increased from 30ms to 420ms during bursts. Traces: distributed tracing shows HTTP/2 GOAWAYs issued from apigw immediately followed by runtime side aborts.
source.investigation_notes: Timeline: 09:12 first error spike, 09:15 escalation to SRE, 09:20 mitigation rollouts started. Hypothesis 1: TLS session-ticket exhaustion combined with apigw HTTP/2 SETTINGS window tweaks caused protocol negotiation mismatch and connection churn. Evidence: ticket-rotation metrics show burst of ticket invalidations at 09:10; apigw telemetry shows aggressive SETTINGS frame changes pushed during tenant reshuffle (window size and max_concurrent_streams altered). Hypothesis 2: Clients opening many short streams without keepalive force apigw to issue new TLS handshakes, exhausting the ticket cache and preventing fast resume, which amplifies handshake latency and triggers hedged-retries and hedged-retry loops. Partial findings: reproducer in staging confirmed ticket-cache exhaustion reproduces error pattern; disabling SETTINGS window change removed the GOAWAYs in test. Cross-references: related to earlier incidents about handshake-throttle and keepalive-cull (see linked issues).
comments: 2026-03-10 09:23 - Support (Ava): Customer reported error spikes and shared initial request ids and timestamps. Asked for logs and whether they had changed client-side keepalive settings.

2026-03-10 09:37 - SRE (Diego): Observed TLS ticket rotation metrics matching spike; created temporary alert to track ticket cache saturation. Started investigating apigw HTTP/2 settings pushed in the last config roll.

2026-03-10 10:02 - Eng (Priya S.): Confirmed apigw config change that nudges SETTINGS initial_window_size and max_concurrent_streams during tenant priority reshuffle. Reverted the SETTINGS tweak in Canary, seeing immediate reduction in GOAWAYs.

2026-03-10 10:25 - Support (Ava): Updated NimbusHealth that we are rolling a targeted mitigation and requested they test. Customer reported partial improvement but still small blips for heavy short-lived streams.

2026-03-10 11:00 - SRE (Diego): Deployed a two-part mitigation: 1) temporarily increase TLS session-ticket cache, 2) pin HTTP/2 SETTINGS for apigw-to-runtime connections to stable values during tenant reshuffles. Monitoring shows 5xx rate dropped below 1%.
source.resolution: Mitigation applied: increased apigw session-ticket cache size and pinned HTTP/2 SETTINGS for gateway->runtime control plane to prevent dynamic window resizing during tenant priority operations. Hotfix PRs merged: PR/7842 pinned SETTINGS and added a guard to skip SETTINGS updates during ticket-rotation windows; PR/1911 increased ticket cache and added metrics to alert earlier. Customer was informed and confirmed errors fell back to baseline. Triage: incident classified Sev1/P1; no customer data exposure.
source.resolution_notes: Hotfix rollout timeline: 2026-03-10 10:45 canary change; 2026-03-10 11:15 global rollout completed. Post-mitigation monitoring for 48 hours; no recurrence in the observed window. Root cause: interplay of intensive TLS ticket rotation (due to high short-lived connections and low keepalive) + dynamic HTTP/2 SETTINGS adjustments during tenant priority reshuffle causing frequent GOAWAYs and runtime evictions.
follow_up_tasks: Create a dedicated e2e test that simulates tenant priority reshuffles under high short-lived stream load (owner: SRE, due: 2026-03-24)
Add alerting for TLS session-ticket cache utilization crossing 70% (owner: infra-monitoring, due: 2026-03-18)
Add a configuration guard to avoid SETTINGS frame changes when ticket-rotation or high handshake latency is detected (owner: api-gateway team, due: 2026-03-20)
Document client guidance for enterprise customers: recommend keepalive configuration to reduce full TLS handshakes (owner: Solutions, due: 2026-03-22)

source.summary: Intermittent console 504 / aborted uploads: gRPC-web upload stream resets behind customer ADC that coalesces HTTP/2 SETTINGS frames
source.summary_detail: Customers report 504s from Redwood Console when uploading large prompt bundles (~30–120MB). Failures only occur for users routing through their corporate ADC (F5/Big-IP) that applies TCP optimizations. Browser shows gRPC-web stream reset / PROTOCOL_ERROR; server-side logs show aborted streams and SETTINGS frame sequence anomalies.
source.description: Issue summary: Intermittent upload failures and 504s observed in Redwood Console for a Private deployment. Failures reproduce only when the user's traffic flows through their on-prem ADC which performs HTTP/2/TCP optimizations. Impact: uploads fail to complete, leading to customer inability to persist prompt datasets and occasional console timeouts; affects a subset of users behind the ADC, not all tenants.
source.impact: Multiple engineering users at Nimbus Security cannot upload prompt bundles via Console; some retry loops create duplicate upload attempts and token churn. Estimated 10–20% of uploads from within the corporate network fail during nightly ingestion batches.
source.environment_context: Private deployment (customer-managed VPC, VPCE to Redwood control plane). Customer frontends traffic through an on-prem F5 BIG-IP ADC which terminates TLS and proxies upstream to the VPCE. ADC configured with HTTP/2 multiplexing, TCP coalescing, and an application profile that rewrites some SETTINGS behavior to minimize handshake RTTs.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Login to Redwood Console from a client inside Nimbus Security network.
2) Start a large prompt bundle upload (~30–120MB) via the Console 'Dataset > Upload' flow (uses gRPC-web over HTTP/2).
3) Observe upload progress; in failing cases upload stalls and Console eventually shows a 504 or 'Upload failed: stream reset'.
4) Reproduce is consistent when client is on the corp network and routing through ADC; bypassing the ADC (direct internet or mobile tether) completes upload successfully.
5) Packet captures and ADC access logs were captured by the customer and attached below.
source.observed_behavior: gRPC-web stream resets mid-upload, Console logs show 'STREAM_RESET' or 'PROTOCOL_ERROR'. Server logs report aborted streams, and HTTP/2 trace shows SETTINGS frames arriving out of expected order or with coalesced payloads. ADC logs show an optimization labeled 'http2-settings-coalesce' and TCP coalescing across small writes.
source.expected_behavior: Uploads should complete without stream resets; HTTP/2 SETTINGS and WINDOW_UPDATE frames should arrive intact and in-order so the gRPC runtime can maintain the stream. Console should return success and persist the dataset.
logs_and_artifacts:
Customer-provided tcpdump (snippet): frame 12345: TLS record: Handshake
    frame 12346: HTTP/2 SETTINGS (initial)
    frame 12348: HTTP/2 SETTINGS (ack) coalesced-with WINDOW_UPDATE
    frame 12350: DATA (partial) -> stream reset
ADC syslog excerpt: Mar 10 10:12:34 bigip1 INFO http2:settings-coalesce enabled profile=low-latency; coalesced_frames=12
Console client error (browser console): gRPC-Web: transport error; code=2; message=Stream reset (code=PROTOCOL_ERROR)
Server side trace id: trace-redwood-98231 (attached request ids across aborted uploads)
Customer-provided ADC config excerpt (redacted): http2 profile with 'coalesce-settings' and 'tcp-latency-opt' enabled
investigation_notes: 2026-03-10 15:00 - Ethan Park: Triaging; reproduced a failing upload when routing through customer's VPN endpoint (confirmed success from mobile tether).

2026-03-10 16:10 - SRE: Captured server-side HTTP/2 frame trace. Observed SETTINGS frames grouped and a WINDOW_UPDATE inserted before SETTINGS_ACK which triggers our gRPC runtime to treat the stream as out-of-order leading to PROTOCOL_ERROR.

2026-03-10 17:45 - Eng: Reviewed gRPC-web client behavior; client library expects SETTINGS/ACK handshake before application DATA on a new stream. ADC coalescing merges control frames and packets such that ordering is not preserved from server perspective.
2026-03-11 09:30 - Support: Requested customer to temporarily disable 'http2 settings coalesce' and 'tcp-latency-opt' on ADC; they agreed to test during a maintenance window.

2026-03-11 14:05 - Customer: Re-tested with optimizations disabled; upload success rate returned to baseline. ADC vendor notes indicate an optional patch that alters SETTINGS handling for legacy backend stacks.

2026-03-12 11:00 - Eng: Determined a safe client-side retry/backoff change can make uploads more robust to short control-frame reordering, but fix is non-trivial and may mask real protocol violations.
customer_communication: 2026-03-10 - Maya S: Acknowledged receipt and requested tcpdump + ADC config. Asked for maintenance window to test config changes.
2026-03-11 - Maya S: Coordinated maintenance window 2026-03-11 13:00–15:00 ET with customer network team.
2026-03-11 - Customer (Nimbus): Disabled 'http2-settings-coalesce' during window and reported successful uploads. Customer will engage ADC vendor for a permanent patch.
2026-03-12 - Ethan P: Provided recommended mitigations and a temporary console-side workaround (retry on PROTOCOL_ERROR for uploads up to 2x with exponential backoff).
source.hypothesis: On-prem ADC is rewriting/coalescing HTTP/2 control frames (SETTINGS/ACK/WINDOW_UPDATE) and delivering them to the backend in a combined TCP segment. The gRPC-web client and our HTTP/2 stack interpret the reordered/coalesced frames as a protocol error, leading to stream resets. Bypassing ADC shows success, disabling coalescing in ADC resolves the issue.
short_term_workaround: 1) Ask customer to disable 'http2-settings-coalesce' and any TCP coalescing/latency optimizations on their ADC for paths to the VPCE.
2) If disabling is not possible, recommend bypassing ADC for uploads (split upload endpoint via direct egress subnet) during their migration window.
3) Console-side: implement a guarded retry for uploads that fail with PROTOCOL_ERROR (limited retries to avoid duplication).
long_term_actions: - Engineering to evaluate robust handling of unexpected control-frame ordering in the HTTP/2 stack; prototype safe retry/backoff logic for gRPC-web uploads (Q2 work item).
- Docs: Add ADC/ADC-profile guidance to Private deploy runbook (confluence page) describing HTTP/2 profiles known to break gRPC-web.
- Support: Add a diagnostic check to onboarding checklist that flags 'http2-settings-coalesce' or similar ADC optimizations.
- Follow-up with customer: Confirm ADC vendor patch schedule and re-open if patch doesn't remediate.
- Add unit test in runtime to simulate SETTINGS coalescing to observe client behavior.
source.resolution: In Progress - temporary mitigation verified. Customer disabled ADC coalescing and reported upload success during maintenance window. Engineering to scope a resilient client-side mitigation and add runbook guidance. Pending verification after customer's vendor patch.
next_steps: 1) Eng to produce a small RFC for safe upload retry behavior and run it by security/rate-limiting teams (owner: Lara Chen, due 2026-03-19).
2) Support to update onboarding checklist and confluence guidance (owner: Maya Singh, due 2026-03-16).
3) Customer to confirm ADC vendor patch and provide test date; Support to revalidate.
4) Keep ticket open until customer's patched ADC validated in prod for 48h of normal ingestion cycles.

source.summary: Long-tail prompt length burst causes KV-cache fragmentation, runtime OOMs and elevated 5xx (apigw 503)
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer CurrentsAI reported a sudden spike of 503/5xx responses starting 2026-03-10 02:10 UTC for a subset of their requests. Error rate peaked ~18% for the tenant for ~45 minutes.

Impact:
- Tenant-facing latency increased and many streaming sessions were terminated with 503.
- Several serving-runtime pods experienced OOM kills and restarted; autoscaler reacted by adding nodes which increased churn.
- Customer reports multiple failed inference calls and degraded UX for production users.

Timeline (high-level):
- 02:08 UTC: tenant traffic shows a shift: 90th/99th prompt length jumped from ~400 tokens to ~6k tokens (long-tail burst from batch import job).
- 02:10 UTC: API Gateway backlog increases, 503s observed and routed to support alerts.
- 02:15 - 02:40 UTC: multiple runtime pods report GPU memory fragmentation and OOM; KV cache evictions spike.
- 02:45 UTC: mitigations applied (per-tenant throttling) and customer backoff; error rates return to baseline by 03:00 UTC.

Environment and context:
- Dedicated tenant on us-west-2, pinned capacity to a dedicated GPU pool.
- Autoscaler configured with reactive scale-up; no hard per-tenant concurrency limit before incident.
- Runtime version: 2026.02.14-release (includes prefetching + KV cache compaction).

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Replay tenant "large import" job that issues many long prompts (median 4k tokens, tail to 12k).
2) Allow concurrent submission at existing customer concurrency (50 concurrent streaming sessions).
3) Observe serving-runtime logs and apigw backlog.

Observed behavior / logs (selected snippets):
- apigw: "upstream response status 503 -- upstream reset while waiting for connection"
- runtime stdout: "OOM on GPU 0: failed to allocate 284 MB; fragmentation detected"
- kvcache metrics: eviction_rate spike from 0.2/s to 38/s; cache_hit_ratio dropped 83% -> 27%
- autoscaler: scaling_event: added 3 nodes in 2 minutes; pod_restarts increased by 4

Customer impact note:
Customer reported failed imports and UI errors for paying prod users; business impact moderate-high. Customer requested root cause and mitigation; asked for rollback if necessary.

Immediate actions taken by Support (timeline):
- 02:22 UTC: Applied temporary tenant-level concurrency cap at gateway (20 concurrent) and limited max_tokens to 4096 for new streaming sessions.
- 02:30 UTC: Opened internal incident channel (#support, #sre, #runtime).
- 02:40 UTC: Requested emergency patch to prefetch settings on the dedicated pool (reduce prefetch window).

Additional notes:
- This looks different from prior kv-cache thrash incidents: root cause appears to be extreme prompt-length skew rather than constant high qps.
- Prefetch combined with long-tail prompts appears to oversubscribe GPU memory and cause fragmentation rather than a simple allocation spike.
comments: 2026-03-10 02:25 UTC - Maya (Support): Notified customer of mitigation (concurrency cap). Asked for replay artifacts and timestamps.

2026-03-10 02:33 UTC - SRE (Liam O'Neill): Observed rapid eviction pattern in KV cache and multiple pods reporting small alloc failures then full OOM. Opened a tracing span to capture per-request token lengths.

2026-03-10 02:38 UTC - Runtime Eng (Ravi Patel): Hypothesis: prefetch_buffer * max_seq_len combination with long-tail prompts triggers memory fragmentation. Suggest reducing prefetch multiplier and adding min-eviction-age to KV cache.
2026-03-10 02:50 UTC - Customer (CurrentsAI - ops@currents.ai): Confirmed we started a bulk import at 02:05 UTC; can provide manifests. Asking for whether we should retry imports now or wait for fix.
2026-03-11 09:12 UTC - Ravi Patel: Pushed a hotfix (canary) to cap per-request prefetch to 2048 tokens and reduce background compaction frequency. Monitoring telemetry.
2026-03-11 10:00 UTC - Maya (Support): Customer confirmed retry succeeded with capped settings and no 503s observed post-fix.
investigation_notes: Correlation: spikes in apigw 503s line up exactly with shift in request token-length distribution for the tenant.
KV cache behavior: high eviction rate reduced effective cache hit rate, causing more prefetch work and higher asynchronous memory churn.
GPU OOM pattern: fragmentation OOMs (failed small allocations) preceding full OOM and container restart rather than single huge allocation; indicates allocation pattern churn.
Autoscaler interaction: reactive node add increased pod churn which temporarily worsened memory pressure during warmup.
Reproduction notes: replaying the customer's bulk job on staging with live runtime and prefetch enabled reproduced the OOM and 503 wave within 10-15 minutes.
Related config: prefetch_multiplier was set to 4 for this pool (default), which multiplies worst-case sequence memory reservation. For long sequences this multiplies into overcommitment.
resolution: Short-term workaround (applied 2026-03-10): per-tenant concurrency cap (20), enforce max_tokens=4096 for new streaming sessions, throttle replay jobs until patch applied.
Hotfix (canary 2026-03-11): cap per-request prefetch to 2048 tokens and reduce prefetch_multiplier from 4 -> 2 for dedicated pools while we validate.
Post-fix monitoring: KV cache eviction rate returned to baseline and GPU OOMs stopped for the tenant after hotfix canary (observed 24 hours).

Planned follow-ups:
- Runtime change to make prefetch multiplier tenant-aware and to expose per-tenant max_prefetch via tenant config UI.
- Autoscaler tuning: add hold-time before scaling to avoid churn from immediate reactive scale-ups during bursty long-tail traffic.
- Add telemetry dashboard: per-tenant token-length histogram and KV eviction heatmap.
- Update runbook with steps to apply tenant-level token caps and emergency prefetch rollbacks.
postmortem_action_items: ENG-11234: Implement tenant-aware prefetch cap and expose via console (owner: Ravi) - ETA 2026-04-01
SRE-9876: Tune autoscaler hold-time and integrate memory-fragmentation alerting (owner: Liam) - ETA 2026-03-25
DX-4512: Add per-tenant token-length histogram to Console metrics (owner: Priya) - ETA 2026-03-30
SUP-358991: Follow-up with CurrentsAI on billing adjustments for impacted requests (owner: Maya) - ETA 2026-03-17

- Autoscaler configured with reactive scale-up; no hard per-tenant concurrency limit before incident.
- Runtime version: 2026.02.14-release (includes prefetching + KV cache compaction).

Steps to reproduce (approx):
1) Replay tenant \"large import\" job that issues many long prompts (median 4k tokens, tail to 12k).
2) Allow concurrent submission at existing customer concurrency (50 concurrent streaming sessions).
3) Observe serving-runtime logs and apigw backlog.

Observed behavior / logs (selected snippets):
- apigw: \"upstream response status 503 -- upstream reset while waiting for connection\"
- runtime stdout: \"OOM on GPU 0: failed to allocate 284 MB; fragmentation detected\"
- kvcache metrics: eviction_rate spike from 0.2/s to 38/s; cache_hit_ratio dropped 83% -> 27%
- autoscaler: scaling_event: added 3 nodes in 2 minutes; pod_restarts increased by 4

Customer impact note:
Customer reported failed imports and UI errors for paying prod users; business impact moderate-high. Customer requested root cause and mitigation; asked for rollback if necessary.

Immediate actions taken by Support (timeline):
- 02:22 UTC: Applied temporary tenant-level concurrency cap at gateway (20 concurrent) and limited max_tokens to 4096 for new streaming sessions.
- 02:30 UTC: Opened internal incident channel (#support, #sre, #runtime).
- 02:40 UTC: Requested emergency patch to prefetch settings on the dedicated pool (reduce prefetch window).

Additional notes:
- This looks different from prior kv-cache thrash incidents: root cause appears to be extreme prompt-length skew rather than constant high qps.
- Prefetch combined with long-tail prompts appears to oversubscribe GPU memory and cause fragmentation rather than a simple allocation spike.

source.summary: Clarify accounting for sustained vs short-burst tokens with HTTP/2 connection reuse and signed-url multipath
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports repeated 429s during a sustained ingest job that uses multiplexed HTTP/2 clients and short-lived signed URLs. They see higher-than-expected throttle counts when reusing the same underlying connection across several ephemeral API keys and signed callbacks.

Impact:
- Intermittent 429s on 3 of 5 ingest workers, causing job retries and delayed backfill.
- Customer on a Dedicated pool; affects SLA and daily throughput.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool (prod-us-west) pinned to redwood/open-llama-13b; autoscaling disabled.
- Ingress: client uses HTTP/2 connection reuse with multiple multiplexed streams, each stream attaching a freshly requested signed URL token per upload chunk.

Steps to reproduce (provided by customer):
1) Open a single persistent HTTP/2 connection from an orchestrator to our edge.
2) Spawn 20 concurrent streams over that connection.
3) For each stream, request a signed URL (short-lived key) and immediately POST chunked content to the signed path.
4) Repeat every ~30s for ~15 minutes.

Logs / Observations:
- Customer-provided logs show 429 responses clustered after ~7 minutes. 429 response headers include X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0 and X-RateLimit-Window: short-burst.
- Edge proxy logs show the same client IP mapping to a single connection with many active streams; signed-URL tokens are unique per request.
- Console quota view shows tenant tokens draining faster than expected; console displays per-route burst pool being consumed.

Customer hypothesis:
They believe token accounting is happening per-connection rather than per API key/signed-token and that connection reuse is causing multipath counting into a single short-burst pool. They ask whether signed URL callbacks or ephemeral tokens should be isolated from per-connection smoothing windows.

Investigation notes (support):
- Reproduced a simplified load pattern in staging: multiplexed HTTP/2 streams with ephemeral auth. Observed elevated short-burst consumption on the route layer.
- Initial tracing shows request attribution occurs at the ingress handler which binds rate accounting keys by (tenant_id, route, connection_fingerprint) when stream reuse and signedurl tokens are present. This aggregation was an optimization to reduce lookup overhead for long-lived sessions.
- Confirmed that in current config a single connection_fingerprint can cause burst credits to be shared across ephemeral tokens presented on separate streams.

Hypothesis and risk assessment:
- The aggregation behavior is intentional in high-throughput paths to avoid per-request auth lookups, but it can lead to surprising tenant-burst exhaustion when many short-lived tokens are fanned over a single connection.
- Risk: dedicated customers running multiplexed orchestrators (like serverless uploader pools) may hit short-burst exhaustion even if logical API keys are within limits.

Workaround provided to customer:
- Recommend switching the orchestrator to open multiple shorter-lived connections (reduce stream fanout per connection) to ensure connection_fingerprint separation.
- Alternative: request signed URLs with slightly longer TTL and reduce frequency of refreshes so requests fall into sustained refill accounting rather than short-burst.
- Temporary console change: increased per-route short-burst cap for their dedicated pool while we investigate (change applied at 2026-03-10T18:40:00Z).

Proposed permanent fixes / next steps:
1) Product: add configuration flag on Dedicated pools to prefer per-token accounting over connection-aggregation for routes flagged as "ephemeral-signedurl".
2) Engineering: evaluate trade-offs and implement an opt-in mode that performs a fast token bind (cache per-token) when a signed-url header is detected, avoiding connection-based aggregation.
3) SRE: run a canary in staging that flips the flag for one edge shard and run load tests to verify performance and quota accounting correctness.

Resolution timeline request by customer:
- Customer requests a low-risk fix or a documented recommended architecture within 2 weeks to avoid re-architecting their ingestion pipeline.

Additional notes:
- Related internal discussion in #support and #infra-edge about connection-level optimizations.
- Billing/usage shows consistent token burn matching the described pattern.
comments: 2026-03-10 17:05 - Support (Samir Patel): Thanks for the report, Ava. We reproduced the pattern in staging and confirmed aggregation at the ingress. We're enabling a temporary per-route burst increase while we scope a fix.

2026-03-10 20:18 - Customer (StreamFlare - Maya R.): Thanks Samir — we applied the multi-connection workaround and saw fewer 429s during a 30-minute run. However, opening more connections increases NAT and egress costs for us; we'd prefer a config change on your side.

2026-03-11 09:12 - Engineering (Diego Morales): Noted. Proposal: add "ephemeral-token-isolation" toggle for dedicated pools so we can bind accounting to token id when signed-url headers detected. We'll scope perf impact and a canary window.
2026-03-11 14:30 - Support (Samir Patel): Drafting product request and will link to infra PR and staging canary plan. Targeting RFC by 2026-03-17.
2026-03-11 15:02 - Customer (Maya R.): Appreciate the timeline. Please include guidance about TTL and recommended stream-per-connection limits until a config is available.

source.summary: ScribeHQ: streaming responses sometimes end early (partial output, no error)
source.description:
Customer reports streaming outputs occasionally terminate early with no explicit error. They receive an apparently clean end-of-stream but the assistant text is truncated relative to expected completion length.

Initial signal came via email thread in Ben Carter mailbox (20260303-scribehq-stream-ends-early.json).

Customer details:
- Region: us-east
- Client uses HTTP/2
- Provided HAR capture (har-capture-sse.json) and request_id req_c41a...

Hypotheses:
- upstream connection reset or GOAWAY causing client to treat stream as complete
- gateway incorrectly emitting [DONE] on backend cancellation

Asks to customer:
- raw response headers
- confirm whether they ever see incomplete JSON/event framing
- confirm whether they are using any proxy (e.g., Cloudflare)
comments: Ava Chen (2026-03-04): Created from escalation email. Customer says stream 'ends early' but no error. Attached HAR referenced in email.
Ethan Park (2026-03-05): Need to compare with stall reports (SUP-1842, SUP-1901). Might be same class of flush/backpressure leading to premature termination.
Ethan Park (2026-03-06): Waiting on customer for raw headers + whether they see HTTP/2 GOAWAY/RST.

source.summary: SDK exponential backoff jitter misconfiguration causing serialized retries and apparent inference timeouts for bulk requests
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a spike in end-to-end inference latency and client-side timeouts when sending large batched inference requests (bulk job of ~25 concurrent requests per client). The SDK appears to serialize retries and produces long head-of-line delays rather than distributing retry attempts across healthy connections.

Impact:
- Several customers on Dedicated capacity experienced perceived timeouts and increased median latency for batched/parallel inference.
- Client-side metrics show heavy retry activity and a single active TCP connection per SDK process during peak bursts.
- Affects production workloads for PromptCraft (dedicated allocation).

Environment:
- Redwood Dedicated (prod us-east-1)
- Customer SDK: redwood-sdk-js v2.3.0 (custom config)
- Load pattern: ~25 parallel connections from a single process batching 16 prompts per request

Observed behavior:
- Burst of requests -> SDK initiates retries but retry jitter misconfiguration causes uniform backoff windows (no randomness), leading to synchronized retry waves.
- SDK default maxConnections overridden to 1 in customer's init script (corporate template), leading to serialized HTTP requests.
- API gateway reports many concurrent queued requests from the same tenant but only one open upstream connection, producing head-of-line blocking and increased latency.
- Clients observe socket-level timeouts and then exponential retries in lockstep, which appear as repeated long latencies rather than occasional failed calls.

Expected behavior:
- SDK should spread retries across connections with randomized jitter so retries are staggered, and clients should use a higher connection pool for parallel workloads.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Start a single-node test client using redwood-sdk-js v2.3.0 with maxConnections set to 1 and backoff config: initial=200ms, multiplier=2, jitter=0 (explicitly disabled).
2) Fire 25 parallel request tasks, each sending a batch payload of 12 prompts to redwood-7b-v1 on prod us-east-1.
3) Induce a transient upstream 503 on serving-runtime (simulate slow node or canary drain) for ~3s.
4) Observe client-side request queueing, repeated retries all happening in same time-window, and increased p95/p99 latencies.

Logs / traces collected:
- API gateway trace id: agw-us-east-20260301-7f9a2c (attached in ticket)
- Client logs show repeated "Retrying request (attempt 3) after backoff=1200ms" with identical timestamps across multiple tasks.
- Serving-runtime logs show spikes of queued requests from tenant PromptCraft and repeated 502/503s during the window.

Notes:
- Customer applied an internal SDK wrapper that set maxConnections=1 for connection pinning to their telemetry proxy. That policy combined with disabled jitter caused serialized retry storms.
- Not a server-only regression: reproduces locally with the customer's SDK config.
steps_to_reproduce: Install redwood-sdk-js v2.3.0 with custom config (maxConnections=1, jitterDisabled=true)
Run a client that submits 25 parallel tasks each sending batched payloads of 12 prompts
Simulate a transient upstream 503 for ~3s and observe retry timing and connection counts
logs: [client] 2026-03-01T14:22:10Z Retry attempt=2 backoff=800ms task=17
[client] 2026-03-01T14:22:10Z Retry attempt=2 backoff=800ms task=19
[agw] 2026-03-01T14:22:10Z queuedRequests=42 tenant=PromptCraft upstreamConnections=1
investigation_notes: Initial hypothesis: server-side throttling or scheduling causing timeouts. Ruled out after reproducing locally using customer's SDK config.
Observed SDK config disables jitter and pinches connection pool to 1 — causes serialized head-of-line blocking under concurrency.
Network captures show repeated SYN/ACK on same connection and long HTTP/1.1 keepalive queues, not parallel connections.
Serving-runtime shows normal healthy kernel; error spikes align with synchronized client retry waves, not underlying model slowness.
comments: Ava Chen (Support): Customer says this began after they rolled out a wrapper change to standardize HTTP settings.
Jordan Patel (Eng): Confirmed reproduction in staging using the customer's config. Will add a safety check in SDK to warn on maxConnections <= concurrency.
SRE (Maya Singh): Gateway shows per-tenant queued counts spiking; will add temporary rate-throttle rule to protect shared resources.
Customer (PromptCraft CS): We've reverted wrapper to default connection pool and are rerunning load test; will report back.
resolution_notes: Workaround provided 2026-03-02: set maxConnections>=16 for this workload and enable randomized exponential jitter (enableJitter=true) in SDK config.
Temporary mitigations: SRE added a per-tenant soft concurrency cap to avoid cascading queueing while customer rolled back the wrapper.
Root cause: customer-side SDK configuration that disabled jitter combined with an overly small connection pool (maxConnections=1) which serialized retries and created apparent service timeouts.

Fix planned: SDK change in v2.4.1 will emit a startup warning when maxConnections < recommended for detected concurrency, and will default to enabling jitter unless explicitly set to false. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk/pull/842
Follow-up: create a support doc (KB) about safe SDK connection/backoff defaults and recommend templates for high-parallelism bulk workloads.
status_update_timeline: 2026-03-02: Workaround recommended and applied by customer; SRE applied temporary gateway cap.
2026-03-03: Engineering reproduced locally and opened SDK PR for warning behavior.
2026-03-05: Customer confirms restored latency after rollback to defaults. Preparing closure notes.
source.final_resolution: Mitigated — customer rolled back the custom wrapper and enabled jitter; SDK warning landed in PR for v2.4.1. Recommend closing after follow-up KB published.
follow_up_actions: Publish KB: 'SDK connection pool and backoff best practices' with code examples for Node and Python clients
Add SDK telemetry metric for effective maxConnections and jitterEnabled to alert on risky configs
Review Dedicated onboarding template to ensure recommended pool defaults for bulk workloads are prominent

- Customer applied an internal SDK wrapper that set maxConnections=1 for connection pinning to their telemetry proxy. That policy combined with disabled jitter caused serialized retry storms.
- Not a server-only regression: reproduces locally with the customer's SDK config.

source.summary: Resume subprotocol mismatch causes session rebind and drops terminal attachment on reconnect
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports that after a brief network blip their websocket stream reconnects but the final embedded attachment (base64 PNG) is missing and the assistant sends duplicated system messages. The application sees a successful 101 upgrade on reconnect yet the streamed session does not resume semantics and the last 'attachment' chunk is never emitted.

Impact:
- ParcelFlow's invoice-generation flow fails to attach generated receipt images to the final response for ~2% of streamed requests during the reconnect window.
- End users see duplicated system messages ("System: Session resumed") and a truncated output (missing base64 attachment).

Environment:
- Customer on Dedicated cluster (prod, us-east) behind corporate edge proxy (vendor: Korunner WAF) and an internal ALB.
- SDK: redwood-js v1.4.2 using websocket with sec-websocket-protocol: "redwood-stream-v1" set by client.
- Model: gpt-open-3b pinned for route.

Steps to reproduce (reported by customer):
1) Start a websocket streaming request that yields an image embed near the end (attachment as base64 in final chunk).
2) Induce a short TCP path migration (client wifi -> cellular).
3) Client reconnects and re-sends the original session token (resume_token) but the websocket handshake goes through a proxy that rewrites/normalizes the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol header to "redwood-stream".
4) Stream resumes successfully at the network layer but no final embed chunk is received; instead two "System: resumed" messages appear and the stream terminates.

Observed behavior:
- Server logs show a new session created for the reconnect instead of a resume: log line: "session_resume: failed -> subprotocol_mismatch, new_session_created=1".
- Edge proxy (ALB -> Korunner) dropped or normalized the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol during the upgrade, causing token-to-subprotocol mismatch in resume routing.
- The final attachment generation completed on the worker (worker logs show glue-attach emitted) but the transport-layer mapping to the original session id was lost; client never receives the terminal base64 chunk.

Expected behavior:
- Resume should be keyed primarily by session resume token and allow subprotocol variant normalization without creating a new session.
- If a true new session is required, server should stream a clear terminal event and not duplicate system messages.

Attachments/Logs:
- Customer supplied capture: websocket upgrade requests (pre/post) and a HAR showing sec-websocket-protocol header values.
- Edge logs: "ALB: removed header Sec-WebSocket-Protocol for sticky path 10.20.30.40".
- Worker log snippet: "[worker-7] emit: attachment(base64) id=attach-0312 status=ready -> queued_transport(session_map_lookup=miss)".
comments: Support (Maya R.) - 2026-03-01: Acknowledged report and requested full websocket upgrade traces and a copied server-side request-id. Asked customer to reproduce with SDK debug logging on and to collect ALB logs.

Customer (ParcelFlow - Samir Patel) - 2026-03-02: Provided ALB access logs and a HAR. Confirms internal proxy (Korunner) normalizes sec-websocket-protocol to 'redwood-stream' on reconnect paths. Able to reproduce reliably by toggling mobile interface.

SRE (Diego Morales) - 2026-03-03: Reproduced in staging by simulating header normalization at the edge. Observed session lookup keyed on (resume_token + subprotocol). When subprotocol differs, server treated as new session. Filed hypothesis in investigation notes.

Eng (Ruth Lin) - 2026-03-04: Noted that past design used (resume_token, subprotocol) tuple to avoid cross-protocol resume attacks. Concern: relaxing tuple may open replay surface; propose short-term workaround and long-term change with cryptographic bind.

SRE (Diego Morales) - 2026-03-05: Implemented and tested a patch in staging that prefers resume_token for session rebind and logs a subprotocol-mismatch metric. Patch referenced in PR #412. Asking for rollout to canary Dedicated cluster with feature flag.
investigation_notes: 2026-03-03 - Repro steps run in staging: injected header normalization -> session lookup failed when server expected exact sec-websocket-protocol match.
Log evidence: 'session_router.lookup(resume_token=tok_abc123, subprotocol="redwood-stream-v1") -> miss; fallback lookup(resume_token=tok_abc123) -> hit but marked as 'subprotocol_mismatch'.'

Edge trace: ALB stripped Sec-WebSocket-Protocol for connections routed through legacy pool (pool-legacy-3). Korunner WAF also normalizes the value on reconnects where it heuristically rewrites headers for caching reasons.

Risk analysis: Current behavior prevents some resume attacks by binding subprotocol to resume, but creates availability issue when middleboxes normalize headers. The safest immediate step is to attempt resume based on token if client IP/agent matches and then emit an audit metric when subprotocol differs.
Customer impact estimate: ~2% of reconnects in customer's traffic pattern; correlated with clients that do wifi->cellular switches behind enterprise proxies.
Related incidents: SUP-214159 (stream-handoff-trailing-garbage) shows similar edge-handshake path issues but not subprotocol-specific.
source.resolution:
Workaround and next steps:
1) Short-term workaround (applied via customer guidance): ParcelFlow to configure Korunner to preserve Sec-WebSocket-Protocol on upgrades or disable header normalization for our endpoint; alternately, use the SDK option to fallback to SSE/longpoll during unstable networks. This resolved customer reproductions when tested.
2) Hotfix (deployed to staging for canary on 2026-03-05): server will attempt a token-first resume lookup when resume_token is presented. If resume succeeds despite subprotocol mismatch, server emits metric 'resume.subprotocol_mismatch' and continues the stream. This is gated behind feature flag resume.flexible_lookup.
3) Long-term (design): Engineering to design a cryptographic bind between resume_token and transport attributes in a way that allows safe normalization by trusted middleboxes while preventing token replay by third parties. Tracked as ENG-PLAT-2276.
4) Post-deploy monitoring: add alert on resume.subprotocol_mismatch rate > 0.5% for a dedicated customer during canary.

Customer status: ParcelFlow validated the workaround and confirmed missing attachment rate dropped to 0.0% in their canary after applying the configuration change. Ongoing: we plan a controlled rollout of resume.flexible_lookup to 10% of dedicated clusters next week and will follow with 100% after 72h of stable metrics.
follow_up_actions: Deploy resume.flexible_lookup feature-flag to 10% canary dedicated clusters (SRE) - target 2026-03-08
Create ENG task ENG-PLAT-2276 for cryptographic resume bind design (Product/Eng) - due 2026-04-01
Add monitoring dashboard for resume.subprotocol_mismatch metric and alert above threshold (Observability)

source.summary: Rivermark: streamed completions stop delivering chunks (chunked transfer stalls)
source.description:
Customer reports that streaming responses intermittently stop sending chunks mid-generation. They see partial output and then no more bytes; client times out at ~75s.

They describe it as: "chunked transfer stalls" / "stream stops sending".

Notes:
- Dedicated cluster in eu-west
- Happens more often during peak hours

Repro (customer):
- /v1/chat/completions stream=true
- Long prompts (>12k tokens) + high concurrency

Cross-refs:
- Slack thread in #support about 'streaming freeze' (timestamp 1770582660)
comments: Aisha Rahman (2026-02-28): Customer call follow-up. They have evidence at the load balancer showing the upstream connection remains open but no more body bytes.
Ethan Park (2026-03-01): Looking for correlation with gateway backpressure and flush intervals. Might be hitting per-conn write buffer limits when token rate drops.
Connor O'Brien (2026-03-02): We should confirm whether keepalive comments are enabled on this dedicated environment; fix from SUP-1842 may not be deployed there.
Ethan Park (2026-03-04): Found dedicated gateway is one minor release behind. Planning patch rollout + add metric for 'seconds since last stream chunk'.

source.summary: Cross-proxy normalization of idempotency header causing duplicate tool/function executions
source.description: Customer reports repeated duplicate side-effecting tool/function invocations when using function-calling with retries. The customer is on a dedicated plan behind a corporate reverse proxy that normalizes headers (lowercasing, truncation of long header values, and replacing spaces with hyphens). NimbusAI sees duplicate webhook/tool calls on retry even though they supply stable idempotency keys in the SDK. Hypothesis: the proxy is rewriting/persistently normalizing the Idempotency header in-flight, so Redwood's dedupe layer receives a different header value on the retry path and treats it as a new request.
steps_to_reproduce: 1) Use Redwood Go SDK to create a function-calling request with an explicit idempotency header 'Idempotency-Key' set to a 64+ char UUID-like token.
2) Send request from a host behind an enterprise reverse proxy that enforces header normalization (lowercase, trims >64 chars, replaces spaces).
3) Force a network timeout on the client to trigger an SDK retry (retry logic enabled at default backoff).
4) Observe that Redwood service receives first request and executes tool call; on retry the gateway receives a mutated Idempotency-Key and executes the tool again (duplicate side-effect).
5) Validate on customer side: two webhook payloads delivered to downstream service with different idempotency header values seen by Redwood logs.
logs: customer-proxy.log: 2026-02-24T15:03:22Z INFO incoming request headers: idempotency-key: abcdef1234567890-ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWX--(truncated)
redwood-gateway.log: 2026-02-24T15:03:22Z DEBUG recv headers: Idempotency-Key: abcdef1234567890-ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWX
redwood-gateway.log: 2026-02-24T15:03:25Z INFO request processed idempotency_key=abcdef1234567890-ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWX action=tool_call tool_id=extract-entities
redwood-gateway.log: 2026-02-24T15:03:28Z DEBUG recv headers on retry: Idempotency-Key: abcdef1234567890-abcdefghijklmnop (normalized/lowercased/truncated)
redwood-serving.log: 2026-02-24T15:03:28Z INFO executed tool call id=extract-entities request_id=xyz-998 duplicate_check=miss
investigation_notes: SRE reproduced in staging by inserting a header-normalizing proxy that lowercases and truncates header values >48 chars; observed duplicate execution when idempotency key length exceeded truncation limit.
Go SDK by default sends Idempotency-Key with length ~72 bytes when using UUID+context suffix; short keys (~32 bytes) dedup correctly across retries.
Customer proxy team confirmed corporate WAF normalizes headers for policy reasons and will not preserve raw header values for long tokens.
Confirmed Redwood dedupe uses exact match on the received idempotency header value at gateway level; no canonicalization tolerance currently exists.
source.root_cause: Idempotency keys mutated in transit by a corporate reverse proxy (lowercasing/truncation/character replacement). Redwood's dedupe mechanism requires exact-match on header value, so mutated retries are treated as new requests leading to duplicate tool executions.
workarounds: 1) Short-term: Use a compact idempotency key under 48 characters (e.g., SHA256-truncated hex or base64url of request payload) so the proxy does not truncate or mutate it.
2) Use request-body idempotency: include a stable 'request_id' field in the JSON body and perform application-level de-dup on your webhook receiver until gateway fix is released.
3) Configure corporate proxy (preferred): whitelist/preserve 'Idempotency-Key' header without normalization for Redwood endpoints. Proxy team can map header passthrough by host pattern.
4) For function-calling with side-effects, enable a server-side confirmation pattern: include a confirmation token in the tool payload and have downstream acknowledge (idempotent design).
source.resolution: Temporary mitigations agreed: NimbusAI will switch to generated 32-char hex idempotency keys and apply a receiver-side dedupe for 72 hours. Internally created INT-4820 to implement header canonicalization strategy and logging to detect mutated idempotency headers at ingress (PR referenced). Engineering will add a warning to SDK docs recommending short idempotency tokens for environments that may normalize headers. Planned follow-up: implement tolerant dedupe that compares a normalized fingerprint (SHA256 of header value) as a fallback within 30 days.
next_steps: 1) INT-4820: Add normalized-fingerprint fallback in ingress dedupe (owner: ingress team) - ETA 2026-03-18.
2) Docs task: update SDK idempotency guidance for proxy environments (owner: devx) - ETA 2026-03-05.
3) Customer follow-up: NimbusAI to confirm proxy whitelist change or confirm short-key rollout in 72h.
4) Monitoring: add detection alert when same request path executes >1 tool call within 10s with different idempotency header values (owner: observability) - ETA 2026-03-10.
comments: 2026-02-25 09:12 - Ava Chen (Support): Initial report received from NimbusAI CS. Customer attached delivery timestamps for duplicate webhook calls.
2026-02-25 12:40 - Mateo Ruiz (SRE): Reproduced in staging with header-normalizing proxy; filing INT-4820 to track gateway change.
2026-02-26 08:22 - NimbusAI (Customer): Confirmed they can switch to shorter keys for now; proxy team prioritized a whitelist check but requested a workaround asap.
2026-02-27 10:05 - Mateo Ruiz (SRE): Deployed temporary log capture to show raw vs received idempotency header and added alert rule as discussed.
2026-02-27 14:30 - Ava Chen (Support): Marking customer on-boarded with workaround; awaiting proxy whitelist confirmation.
