source.summary: Canary rollout latency regression in prod (no auto-mitigation); request SLO-based auto-rollback for model rollouts
source.description:
Issue summary
Northstar HR reported elevated tail latency on their Dedicated endpoint during a model canary rollout. The customer observed p95 and p99 latency increases and intermittent client timeouts during peak traffic. The rollout did not automatically pause/rollback, requiring manual intervention by on-call.

Customer impact
- Customer-facing latency degradation for chat completions.
- Intermittent client-side timeouts (HTTP 499s observed from the gateway; no increase in 5xx at the platform edge initially).
- The customer explicitly requested an automated safety rail: SLO-based auto-rollback (or auto-pause) for canary/A-B rollouts.

Environment
- Dedicated deployment
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- Route: /v1/chat/completions (tenant: northstar-hr)
- Rollout type: canary traffic shift (10% -> 25% planned)

Timeline (all times PT)
- 2026-02-17 09:42: Rollout step increased candidate traffic from 10% to 25% in us-east.
- 09:49: First internal alert observed on rollout dashboard: p99 latency +38% vs baseline (us-east) for candidate.
- 09:52: Customer email to TAM (Liam) + support@ noting “timeouts + slow responses”.
- 09:57: #incidents thread opened; SRE begins triage.
- 10:05: Rollout manually paused at 25% in us-east; traffic held.
- 10:11: Manual rollback executed to baseline model version; traffic shifted back to 100% baseline.
- 10:18: Latency returns to normal ranges; customer confirms improvement.

What happened / initial findings
- Regression appears isolated to candidate model build/config in us-east under real traffic mix.
- Affected metrics were primarily tail latency (p99) with some p95 increase; error-rate SLO did not breach, and the existing monitor was not rollout-scoped (could not attribute to candidate vs baseline quickly).
- We did not have auto-mitigation wired: rollout controller did not evaluate burn-rate windows against rollout-scoped SLOs, so no automatic pause/rollback occurred.

Customer request
Please provide:
1) Auto-pause/auto-rollback when latency or error-rate SLOs breach during a rollout.
2) Ability to configure thresholds and windows per route/model/region/tier.
3) Audit trail explaining why a rollback happened (metrics snapshot + policy used).

Links
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1770705405.json
- Additional on-call notes on monitor staleness/rollout scoping: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770246692.json
- Internal postmortem/notes: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/2026-02-17-rollout-latency-regression-auto-rollback-gaps.json

Requested next steps (support-facing)
- Provide customer with a clear explanation of current rollout safety behavior and near-term mitigation.
- Share that SLO-driven auto-rollback is in-flight (project: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration) and offer to include Northstar HR in early access once available.

Workaround (current)
- Keep candidate traffic capped at <=10% for this model family in us-east until regression root cause is addressed.
- Require manual monitoring + explicit SRE sign-off for step increases during business hours.

Resolution (support ticket)
Manual rollback performed; performance stabilized. Follow-up actions tracked in Eng/PM systems for SLO-scoped monitors and auto-rollback integration.
comments: 2026-02-17 10:02 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Customer reports 'responses are ~2x slower and some requests time out' starting ~09:45 PT. Confirmed they were on the canary route. Asked for immediate rollback and requested longer-term auto rollback based on latency SLOs.

2026-02-17 10:07 PT - Sean Gallagher (SRE): Acknowledged. We see p99 jump on candidate in us-east after 25% step. Pausing rollout now; will rollback to baseline. Not seeing corresponding edge 5xx, mostly elevated service latency + some 499s.

2026-02-17 10:14 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Rollback executed. Watching for recovery. Note: current SLO monitors are not rollout-scoped; hard to attribute quickly in dashboards without manual filtering. This is a known gap being addressed in the auto-rollback project.

2026-02-17 10:22 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Customer confirms latency is back to normal. They’re asking for: (a) configurable thresholds, (b) audit log of rollouts/auto actions, (c) ability to suppress during known incidents but otherwise auto rollback by default.

2026-02-18 08:41 PT - Allison Grant (Observability): Adding note for follow-up: need canonical labels for rollout_id/model_version/region to make rollout-scoped SLO evaluation reliable without high-cardinality blowups. Pointing to standards work + dashboard PR in observability-pack.

2026-02-19 16:10 PT - Sean Gallagher (SRE): Support update to share externally: Today, rollouts rely on alerts and manual intervention; we’re implementing SLO-driven auto-pause/rollback with burn-rate windows + minimum sample sizes to avoid flapping. Target: staged enablement after internal canary validation.

2026-02-21 11:05 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Sent customer summary + path forward. Offered early access once auto-rollback is available for Dedicated (us-east first). Marking ticket resolved; leaving linked engineering follow-ups in postmortem + rollout auto-rollback initiative.
source.resolution: Manual rollback to baseline model version in us-east; rollout paused. Latency recovered within ~7 minutes. Customer informed; follow-up tracked for SLO-scoped monitors + auto-rollback integration and auditability.
source.support_next_action: Offer early access for SLO-based auto-rollback once enabled for Dedicated us-east; ensure rollout-scoped latency/error monitors exist for the customer route before enrollment.

Issue summary
Northstar HR reported elevated tail latency on their Dedicated endpoint during a model canary rollout. The customer observed p95 and p99 latency increases and intermittent client timeouts during peak traffic. The rollout did not automatically pause/rollback, requiring manual intervention by on-call.

Customer impact
- Customer-facing latency degradation for chat completions.
- Intermittent client-side timeouts (HTTP 499s observed from the gateway; no increase in 5xx at the platform edge initially).
- The customer explicitly requested an automated safety rail: SLO-based auto-rollback (or auto-pause) for canary/A-B rollouts.

Environment
- Dedicated deployment
- Regions: us-east (primary), eu-west (secondary)
- Route: /v1/chat/completions (tenant: northstar-hr)
- Rollout type: canary traffic shift (10% -> 25% planned)

Timeline (all times PT)
- 2026-02-17 09:42: Rollout step increased candidate traffic from 10% to 25% in us-east.
- 09:49: First internal alert observed on rollout dashboard: p99 latency +38% vs baseline (us-east) for candidate.
- 09:52: Customer email to TAM (Liam) + support@ noting “timeouts + slow responses”.
- 09:57: #incidents thread opened; SRE begins triage.
- 10:05: Rollout manually paused at 25% in us-east; traffic held.
- 10:11: Manual rollback executed to baseline model version; traffic shifted back to 100% baseline.
- 10:18: Latency returns to normal ranges; customer confirms improvement.

What happened / initial findings
- Regression appears isolated to candidate model build/config in us-east under real traffic mix.
- Affected metrics were primarily tail latency (p99) with some p95 increase; error-rate SLO did not breach, and the existing monitor was not rollout-scoped (could not attribute to candidate vs baseline quickly).
- We did not have auto-mitigation wired: rollout controller did not evaluate burn-rate windows against rollout-scoped SLOs, so no automatic pause/rollback occurred.

Customer request
Please provide:
1) Auto-pause/auto-rollback when latency or error-rate SLOs breach during a rollout.
2) Ability to configure thresholds and windows per route/model/region/tier.
3) Audit trail explaining why a rollback happened (metrics snapshot + policy used).

Links
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1770705405.json
- Additional on-call notes on monitor staleness/rollout scoping: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770246692.json
- Internal postmortem/notes: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/2026-02-17-rollout-latency-regression-auto-rollback-gaps.json

Requested next steps (support-facing)
- Provide customer with a clear explanation of current rollout safety behavior and near-term mitigation.
- Share that SLO-driven auto-rollback is in-flight (project: Auto-rollback on SLO breach integration) and offer to include Northstar HR in early access once available.

Workaround (current)
- Keep candidate traffic capped at <=10% for this model family in us-east until regression root cause is addressed.
- Require manual monitoring + explicit SRE sign-off for step increases during business hours.

Resolution (support ticket)
Manual rollback performed; performance stabilized. Follow-up actions tracked in Eng/PM systems for SLO-scoped monitors and auto-rollback integration.

source.summary: Perf canary alerting false positive on small sample (FP8/INT8 rollout) — add min-sample + improve metric tagging/aggregation
source.description:
Issue summary:
Perf canary fired TTFT/TPOT regression alerts for FP8/INT8 canary cohorts that were later determined to be false positives caused by small sample sizes and aggregation gaps (missing/partial dimensions caused baseline + canary to mix in queries).

Impact:
- Triggered an automated rollback in one region cohort and created follow-up noise in #eng-sre / #incidents.
- Slowed down kernel rollout decision-making (teams did extra triage to prove it was noise).
- Risk: could mask real regressions if alert fatigue sets in.

Context:
Ongoing FP8/INT8 kernel benchmarking + staged rollout. Canary cohorts are low-volume by design, but current alert rules assume a stable sample size and consistent metric tagging.

Current behavior observed:
- Alert triggered on p95 TTFT when canary cohort had <10 requests in the lookback window.
- A single cold-start / cache-miss request dominated the p95.
- Some Grafana panels/alerts were aggregating across missing dimensions (kernel_mode not present on a subset of metrics, and model tag occasionally absent in decode-only histograms), leading to mixed series.

Expected behavior:
- Alerts should require a minimum sample size before evaluating percent-change thresholds.
- All canary-critical metrics should be tagged consistently: model, gpu_sku, region, kernel_mode (baseline|fp8|int8), and scheduler_mode where relevant.
- Aggregation should compare like-for-like cohorts (baseline vs canary) with the same dimensions.

References:
- Slack: sources/slack/eng-sre/1776453320-perf-canary-alerts-too-noisy.json
- Slack: sources/slack/incidents/1776469205-perf-canary-sev2-ttft-spike.json
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/perf-canary-rollout-for-new-kernels.json
comments: 2026-02-16 09:12 Rafael Mendes (SRE): Creating ticket to track fixes for canary alert noise. We had a rollback yesterday that looks like a small-sample p95 spike + mixed aggregation. Assigning to Allison for alert rule + dashboard changes; looping in Julian/Sahana for metric tags if needed.

2026-02-16 10:03 Allison Grant (Observability): Confirmed. The current alert query uses p95(TTFT) over 5m with no min-sample guard. Also seeing that some series are missing kernel_mode so the query collapses baseline+canary. Proposed fixes:
1) Gate on request_count >= N (per model/region),
2) Switch to longer lookback for low-volume cohorts (15m) + burn-in suppression,
3) Ensure runtime emits kernel_mode tag on TTFT/TPOT histograms.
Will draft updated rules + coordinate runtime tagging PR.

2026-02-16 11:40 Julian Moore (Serving Runtime - Scheduling): We can add/confirm tagging in the scheduler instrumentation path for mixed workloads. There was a recent change adding more histograms; I suspect the decode-only histogram is missing model. I’ll verify with Marcus and point to the right metric names.

2026-02-16 13:25 Sahana Iyer (CI/Perf Gates): Adding a min-sample constraint also helps our CI perf gate logic (same failure mode when sample size is tiny). If we standardize on min_count=50 requests OR min_tokens=500k tokens for cohort evaluation, we can reuse logic.

2026-02-17 08:18 Allison Grant (Observability): Quick analysis from Grafana:
- The false positive window had 6 total canary requests for eu-west + model X; 1 request had TTFT ~2.1s (cold start). p95 becomes ~2.1s.
- Baseline series had ~120 requests; since kernel_mode was missing on some histograms, the alert query pulled mixed data.
Action items:
A) Update alerts to require min_count, and compute percent-change only when both baseline and canary meet min_count.
B) Update dashboards to show sample size alongside TTFT/TPOT.
C) Runtime tagging PR: add kernel_mode + gpu_sku + model to all canary-critical histograms.

2026-02-17 12:02 Rafael Mendes (SRE): +1. For rollout safety we also want: do not auto-rollback on single-window breach unless sustained for 2/3 windows and min_count met. If min_count not met, route to warning-only alert (no rollback).

2026-02-18 09:37 Allison Grant (Observability): Implemented changes in perf-canary threshold profiles (PR-611) to include:
- min_sample_count per cohort
- sustained breach requirement (2/3)
- separate WARNING vs ROLLBACK actions
Pending runtime metric tagging PR (pr-19934) and Grafana changes (pr-377).

2026-02-18 15:21 Marcus Reed (Serving Runtime): Confirmed missing tag issue: TTFT histogram had kernel_mode but TPOT histogram from decode-only path did not. Fix is in pr-19934; also added gpu_sku to both to avoid cross-SKU aggregation in shared dashboards.

2026-02-19 10:11 Allison Grant (Observability): Grafana alert rules updated and deployed. Dashboards now include request_count and token_count panels next to TTFT/TPOT. Verified with replay query that baseline vs canary are cleanly separated by kernel_mode. Monitoring in us-east + eu-west canary cohorts looks stable (no spurious pages overnight).
2026-02-19 16:04 Rafael Mendes (SRE): Closing as resolved. Next time we stand up a low-volume canary, we should default to warning-only until min_count achieved (documenting in runbook as follow-up).
source.resolution:
Resolved by tightening perf-canary alerting to be statistically robust for low-volume cohorts and fixing metric tagging/aggregation:
- Added minimum sample size gating (baseline and canary must both meet min_count before percent-change evaluation).
- Required sustained breach (2 of 3 evaluation windows) before triggering rollback actions.
- Split alert actions into WARNING vs ROLLBACK based on min_count + sustained breach.
- Added/standardized metric tags (kernel_mode, model, gpu_sku, region) to prevent mixed-series aggregation.
- Updated dashboards to surface request_count/token_count alongside TTFT/TPOT so operators can quickly identify small-sample artifacts.

Verification:
- Re-ran alert queries for the incident window and confirmed the old false positive would be suppressed due to min_count.
- Observed stable canary monitoring overnight with no noisy pages.

Follow-ups:
- Update perf-canary rollout runbook to explicitly note warning-only mode for new/low-volume cohorts until min_count threshold is met.

source.summary: Dedicated H100 pool throughput drop during FP8/INT8 benchmark window (capacity contention)
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Customer on Dedicated (Summit AI) reported a sudden throughput drop (tokens/sec) and elevated queueing during a scheduled window.
- We correlated the drop with internal FP8/INT8 benchmark jobs that inadvertently consumed capacity in the same H100 node pool used for the customer’s Dedicated deployment.

Customer impact:
- Time window: 2026-02-16 02:10–03:05 UTC
- Symptoms: lower sustained throughput, increased p95 latency, intermittent request timeouts at peak concurrency.
- Scope: Summit AI Dedicated H100 pool in us-east.

Environment:
- Dedicated cluster/pool: dedicated-prod-use1-h100
- Region: us-east

Detection:
- Customer email to Support + Console screenshots showing throughput reduction.
- SRE saw increased scheduler queue depth for the dedicated namespace and elevated GPU utilization from non-dedicated workloads.

Initial hypothesis:
- Noisy-neighbor / capacity contention due to benchmark runner jobs hitting the wrong cluster/pool.

Steps to reproduce (internal):
1) Submit benchmark-lab matrix runner jobs with misconfigured --cluster (or missing dedicated-pool exclusion).
2) Observe increased queue depth and reduced effective throughput in dedicated scheduler metrics.

Diagnostics / evidence:
- Grafana: Scheduler queue depth spikes for dedicated-prod-use1-h100
- Cluster events: benchmark-lab jobs scheduled onto dedicated node pool
- Audit trail: benchmark runner job spec lacked required nodeSelector/taint tolerations to force bench pool

Requested outcome:
- Immediate mitigation to restore Dedicated performance.
- Prevent recurrence via infra guardrails (hard separation + scheduling constraints + preflight checks).
comments: 2026-02-16 02:24 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Created ticket after on-call page from Dedicated throughput alarm + customer report. Seeing queue depth rising in dedicated-prod-use1-h100 while GPU util ~98–100%. Error rate stable; this looks like contention rather than crash.

2026-02-16 02:31 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra): Confirmed: benchmark-lab jobs are present in the dedicated-prod-use1-h100 cluster. These should have been pinned to bench-use1-h100. Suspect runner config regression / missing guardrail.

2026-02-16 02:38 UTC — Irene Choi (Fleet): Capacity reservation for sprint was approved, but the isolation relied on correct cluster targeting. There’s no hard admission control preventing non-dedicated namespaces from landing on dedicated node pools right now.

2026-02-16 02:42 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra): Immediate mitigation started: cordon + drain nodes running benchmark jobs; deleting benchmark-lab workloads in dedicated cluster; re-submitting to bench cluster. ETA 10–15 mins for perf to recover.
2026-02-16 02:55 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Metrics improving. Queue depth returning to baseline; p95 latency trending down. No further timeouts in the last 5 mins.

2026-02-16 03:07 UTC — Support (internal note, owner Rafael Mendes): Sent customer update: identified internal capacity contention, stopped conflicting jobs, performance recovering; offered to provide incident summary and discuss safeguards.

2026-02-16 09:18 UTC — Talia Benson (Program Mgmt): Flagging as sprint-impacting: benchmark runners must not target Dedicated pools. Adding action item for infra scheduling guardrails + runner preflight validation before continuing full matrix runs.

2026-02-17 11:04 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra): Proposed guardrails:
1) Dedicated node pools tainted + only dedicated namespaces have tolerations.
2) Admission controller policy: deny benchmark-lab namespace in dedicated-prod clusters.
3) Bench runner preflight: require explicit --cluster=bench-* and validate against allowlist.
4) Reservation calendar enforcement: automated check to avoid collisions with Dedicated autoscaling windows.
Owners: Infra + SRE. Target: within 48 hrs.

2026-02-18 16:22 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Customer confirmed performance normalized. Marking incident resolved; keeping follow-ups tracked under internal infra tasking (see linked INT-9923 for access/quota + guardrail changes).
source.resolution:
Root cause:
- Benchmark-lab matrix runner jobs for the FP8/INT8 benchmarking sprint were scheduled onto the us-east Dedicated H100 node pool (dedicated-prod-use1-h100) due to a mis-targeted cluster configuration and insufficient isolation controls.

Immediate remediation:
- Stopped/evicted benchmark-lab workloads from the Dedicated cluster and re-ran them on the intended bench cluster.
- Temporarily tightened scheduling by cordoning nodes used by the conflicting benchmark jobs.

Preventative actions (guardrails):
- Implemented hard separation between Dedicated pools and benchmark workloads via node taints/tolerations and namespace-based admission control.
- Added benchmark runner preflight validation to require bench cluster allowlist and block dedicated-prod targets.
- Updated capacity reservation process to include an automated “Dedicated collision check” (time window + cluster allowlist).

Customer-facing outcome:
- Dedicated throughput returned to baseline within ~30 minutes of mitigation. Customer notified with a concise incident summary and prevention plan.

source.summary: Quality gate runs timing out in eu-west (Optimize gating) causing config changes to remain blocked
source.description:
Issue summary:
Northstar reports Optimize config changes in eu-west are getting stuck in "Gate running" and then failing with a timeout. Because gating is required for apply, changes remain blocked.

Customer impact:
- Optimize recommendations cannot be applied for eu-west routes (both one-click and auto-apply policies).
- Customer is unable to progress planned cost reduction work; sees repeated "Gate run timed out" failures.
- No production traffic regression reported; this is a control-plane / eval execution reliability issue.

Environment:
- Hosted API workloads
- Region: eu-west
- Routes: northstar-prod-search (primary), northstar-prod-support-chat (secondary)

What the customer sees:
- Console shows Gate status: Running → Failed (Timeout)
- Gate run duration reported ~45-60 min before failing

Steps to reproduce (support repro):
1) In Console → Optimize → Recommendations, select a recommendation that changes batching policy ("increase max_batch_tokens" / enable continuous batching profile).
2) Click "Run quality gates".
3) Gate transitions to Running.
4) After extended period, gate fails with "Timed out waiting for eval-harness completion".

Observed error text (Console):
"GateRunFailed: timed_out. The eval run did not complete within the configured deadline. gate_run_id=gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1"

Triage notes:
Initial suspicion is eu-west GPU job queue delays in eval-harness (capacity constraints) rather than test flakiness (no metrics returned; job never completes).

Artifacts / identifiers:
- Example gate_run_id: gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1
- Example eval_run_id (from Optimize logs): eh_01JMBE9M7W3F0QJ7D3Y2VQ0YQD
- Correlation IDs: rw_req_2f3c1b2d3b1a, rw_req_89a9c0d7e11f

Internal pointers:
- Slack: #support thread started by PM (Aditya) with customer screenshots; #eng-sre pinged for eu-west capacity.
- Runbook followed: runbook-optimize-quality-gate-failures (timeout path).
comments: 2026-02-10 09:14 UTC — Talia Benson (Program Manager): Customer (Northstar) reports multiple gate runs timing out in eu-west since Fri. They are blocked from applying Optimize recommendations. Adding screenshots + gate_run_ids in #support. Requesting SRE oncall triage.

2026-02-10 09:36 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE oncall): Ack. Pulling gate execution metrics for eu-west. Initial glance: spike in eval job queue time starting 2026-02-07. Will confirm whether this is GPU capacity saturation in eval-harness pool.

2026-02-10 10:02 UTC — Chloe Martin (Console backend): From Console API perspective we just surface Optimize gate status. For gr_01JMBE8E2Q..., Optimize returns error_code=timed_out and no metric payload. Suggest checking Optimize → eval-harness RPC timeout vs eval-harness job queue + runtime.

2026-02-10 10:27 UTC — Allison Grant (Observability): Dashboard: quality-gates / eval-harness. eu-west p95 "time_to_start" jumped from ~2m to ~28m; p95 runtime flat (~11m). Looks like queueing/capacity, not slow tests. Also seeing elevated 504s from internal job-dispatch in eu-west.

2026-02-10 10:41 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE oncall): Confirmed capacity issue. eu-west eval-harness GPU pool at 95–100% utilization; queue depth > 200 for "gating-full" class. Optimize timeout currently 45m wall-clock; with 28m start delay + retries, we exceed deadline.
Actions now:
1) Temporarily reduce concurrency for non-gating eval suites in eu-west.
2) Increase eu-west gating pool by +16 A100s (if available).
3) Enable cross-region spillover (run evals in us-east when eu-west queue_time > 10m) — requires feature flag flip in eval-harness.

2026-02-10 11:20 UTC — Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): GPU scarcity in eu-west ongoing due to another internal perf job. I can pause the perf-canary batch jobs for 24h to free capacity. Doing that now; expect queue to drain within ~1h.

2026-02-10 12:08 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE oncall): Perf jobs paused; queue depth trending down (200 → 110). Still need spillover to prevent recurrence during peak.
Creating INC-7751 to track as reliability incident (internal).

2026-02-10 13:05 UTC — Olivia Grant (Eval harness integration): Spillover flag exists but was disabled for gating due to artifact locality concerns. We can enable spillover for gating-smoke immediately (artifacts already in global object store). For gating-full, ensure artifact URLs are region-agnostic (they should be). I will validate with a test run.

2026-02-10 15:22 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE oncall): Enabled spillover for gating-smoke in eu-west. For gating-full, keeping local for now but raising pool size + lowering non-gating concurrency.
Customer-facing update: we have mitigations in place; asking customer to rerun blocked gates.

2026-02-11 08:11 UTC — Talia Benson: Customer reran 3 gates overnight; 2 passed, 1 still timed out at ~52m. They noted the failing one was a quantization profile change on long-context prompt set. Requesting deeper look: is this still queueing or an outlier runtime?

2026-02-11 08:44 UTC — Allison Grant: That quant gate had time_to_start=6m, runtime=41m (p99-ish). So it is not only queue. It hit the long-context subset; runtime exceeded our current per-suite expected bounds. Might need suite-level timeouts + early termination / smaller shard sizes.

2026-02-11 09:12 UTC — Sean Gallagher: For immediate unblock: raised Optimize deadline for eu-west from 45m → 75m (config change, no deploy). Longer-term: ENG-3929 to handle slow suites + better sharding. Also filing note to Applied ML to review long-context quant coverage runtime bounds.

2026-02-12 16:30 UTC — Sean Gallagher: Post-change metrics: eu-west gate timeout rate down from 18% → 1.6% (24h). Remaining timeouts correspond to long-context quant suite only. Customer is unblocked for batching + caching changes; quant changes may still take longer but are completing within 75m.

2026-02-14 10:05 UTC — Sean Gallagher: Enabled cross-region spillover for gating-full as well after validating artifact URLs are region-agnostic. This should keep queueing bounded even during eu-west capacity crunch. Monitoring for any new failure mode (artifact permissions/URL issues).

2026-02-16 09:40 UTC — Talia Benson: Customer confirms all currently pending gates have completed successfully; no additional timeouts reported since spillover + deadline bump. Requesting we close ticket but keep follow-ups tracked in ENG issues.

2026-02-16 10:10 UTC — Sean Gallagher: Resolving. Follow-ups: (1) ENG-3929 flake/slow-suite quarantine and rerun policy; (2) ENG-3926 add alert on eu-west gate queue_time and timeout rate; (3) capacity reservation review for eu-west eval pool during regional peaks.
source.resolution: Mitigated eu-west gate run timeouts by (a) freeing GPU capacity (paused competing perf jobs), (b) increasing eu-west eval-harness gating pool and reducing non-gating concurrency, (c) enabling cross-region spillover for gating runs when eu-west is saturated, and (d) raising Optimize gate deadline for eu-west from 45m to 75m to accommodate long-context quant suites. Customer confirmed reruns completed successfully.
source.workaround: If a gate run times out in eu-west: rerun during off-peak or request support to force spillover execution to us-east; for urgent config changes, use time-bound override only with required approver + audit trail (per exception policy).
source.next_steps:
1) Add automated alerting for gate queue_time and timeout spikes in eu-west (ENG-3926).
2) Apply suite-level runtime bounds + sharding improvements for long-context quant gate suite; quarantine if consistently slow (ENG-3929).
3) Document spillover behavior and regional capacity expectations in customer-facing guidance for gating SLAs.
source.customer_update_summary: We identified eu-west capacity saturation in the eval-harness GPU pool causing gate runs to queue and exceed time limits. We increased available capacity, reduced competing workloads, enabled cross-region spillover, and extended the gate deadline for long-running suites. Please rerun blocked gates; we are monitoring closely and have follow-up engineering work in progress.

1) In Console → Optimize → Recommendations, select a recommendation that changes batching policy ("increase max_batch_tokens" / enable continuous batching profile).
2) Click "Run quality gates".
3) Gate transitions to Running.
4) After extended period, gate fails with "Timed out waiting for eval-harness completion".

Observed error text (Console):
"GateRunFailed: timed_out. The eval run did not complete within the configured deadline. gate_run_id=gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1"

Triage notes:
Initial suspicion is eu-west GPU job queue delays in eval-harness (capacity constraints) rather than test flakiness (no metrics returned; job never completes).

Artifacts / identifiers:
- Example gate_run_id: gr_01JMBE8E2Q0K5G9H7X2M4KJ9P1
- Example eval_run_id (from Optimize logs): eh_01JMBE9M7W3F0QJ7D3Y2VQ0YQD
- Correlation IDs: rw_req_2f3c1b2d3b1a, rw_req_89a9c0d7e11f

Internal pointers:
- Slack: #support thread started by PM (Aditya) with customer screenshots; #eng-sre pinged for eu-west capacity.
- Runbook followed: runbook-optimize-quality-gate-failures (timeout path).

source.summary: Dedicated: burst capacity not activating during traffic spikes (Northstar Payments, us-east)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Customer reports Dedicated burst capacity did not activate during predictable traffic spikes. Throughput capped at baseline replicas, causing queueing and elevated p95/p99 latency.
- Customer expectation: burst should add temporary replicas within 2–5 minutes when utilization/queue depth crosses threshold.

Impact
- 2026-02-12 17:05–18:20 UTC: sustained elevated latency and intermittent 5xx from upstream timeouts due to request queue buildup.
- Customer-visible symptoms:
  - Latency increase: p95 from ~850ms to ~4.8s (chat), p99 up to ~9–12s.
  - RPS capped at baseline; queue depth increased and stayed elevated.
  - No visible scale-out events in their Console view.

Customer / Tier
- Northstar Payments (Dedicated)

Environment
- prod, us-east
- Dedicated pool: northstar-prod-gpu-pool (A100-80GB)

What we expected
- Burst capacity should temporarily scale above reserved baseline up to configured burst_max_replicas when triggers are met, subject to regional capacity availability.

What happened (initial observation)
- Autoscaling policy evaluation occurred, triggers met, but scale-out did not proceed beyond baseline max.
- System logged repeated 'scale_out_blocked' events with reason: 'pool_max_replicas_reached' and later 'no_capacity_in_cell'.

Steps to reproduce (customer)
1) Run scheduled batch + interactive traffic at top of hour (17:00 UTC).
2) Observe utilization > 75% and queue depth > 300 for > 5 minutes.
3) Expect scale-out; instead replicas remain at baseline.

Triage checklist (Support)
- Confirmed request IDs provided by customer; sampled across the spike window.
- Confirmed region: us-east, dedicated deployment.
- Confirmed customer config changed earlier this week: raised baseline_min_replicas; no explicit change to burst settings.

Investigation notes (SRE + Platform)
- Reviewed autoscaling policy for pool:
  - baseline_min_replicas=12
  - baseline_max_replicas=12
  - burst_enabled=true
  - burst_max_replicas=20
  - scale_out_metric=queue_depth (threshold=200, window=3m)
  - scale_down_cooldown=15m
- Found two separate blockers:
  1) Config/guardrail interaction: pool-level max_replicas remained pinned to baseline_max_replicas=12. The burst_max_replicas was set, but the scheduler respected pool max as a hard cap due to an older policy schema path still in effect for this tenant.
  2) Regional capacity constraints: even after manual override to raise pool max, only 4 additional GPUs were available in the assigned cell; requests to allocate beyond that returned 'no_capacity_in_cell'.

Key signals checked
- Dedicated autoscaler events: scale_out_requested present, scale_out_applied absent during the window.
- Scheduler allocation logs: 'cap_exceeded: max_replicas' followed by 'insufficient_capacity'.
- Cluster capacity dashboard (internal): us-east cell use1-gpu-c3 at 96–99% A100 utilization.
- Customer-facing metrics (Support-safe quick view): queue depth and throttle indicators aligned with reported symptoms.

Mitigation
- Immediate:
  - Rafael (SRE) applied manual scale to 16 replicas (within available capacity) and raised pool max cap for this tenant to allow burst to exceed baseline.
  - Advised customer to temporarily reduce max_tokens and enable client-side exponential backoff for non-interactive traffic until burst stabilizes.
- Short-term (same day):
  - Reserved additional capacity in adjacent cell (use1-gpu-c4) and adjusted placement to allow up to 18 replicas during peak hours.

Customer communications
- Followed Dedicated SLA cadence per playbook: acknowledgement within 30 minutes; updates hourly during active mitigation.
- Shared explanation:
  - A configuration cap prevented burst from scaling above baseline.
  - Even after lifting the cap, us-east cell capacity limited burst headroom; we increased available capacity and will add guardrails to prevent silent non-activation.

Root cause
- Primary: legacy policy schema path treated baseline_max_replicas as a hard cap, ignoring burst_max_replicas for this tenant.
- Contributing: us-east A100 cell saturation reduced burst headroom during the customer’s spike window.

Resolution
- Burst now activates above baseline for this tenant after pool max cap correction.
- Capacity increased to provide headroom for expected peaks (target: +6 replicas available during scheduled spike windows).
- Monitoring: added internal alert on 'burst_enabled=true AND triggers_met AND scale_out_blocked>0 for 5m' for Dedicated pools in us-east (temporary alert rule pending formalization).

Follow-ups / Action items
- Engineering:
  - Track with ENG-4829 (autoscaling stability/guardrails): add explicit validation that burst_max_replicas is reachable; surface a clear warning when burst is configured but pool max cap blocks scale-out.
  - Add a Support-visible signal explaining why burst did not activate (cap vs capacity) to reduce back-and-forth.
- SRE / Capacity:
  - Capacity planning note: us-east A100 headroom is insufficient for burst commitments during weekday peaks; recommend reserving an additional 16 GPUs across use1-gpu-c3/c4 for Dedicated burst pool.
  - Evaluate whether burst should fall back to alternate cell/region for Dedicated (if contract allows) or provide pre-warm scheduling for known spikes.

Risk / Notes
- Customer-sensitive details (exact utilization/cell mapping) should not be copied into external KB; keep comms at the 'regional capacity constraints + increased headroom' level.

References
- Related: SUP-1844 (Dedicated autoscaling oscillation causing latency) for broader autoscaling improvements.
- Playbook: severity mapping + Dedicated SLA cadence + bridge conventions (see related Confluence pages).
comments: 2026-02-12 (Owen Phillips, Support): Customer escalation via CSM. Burst expected to kick in for top-of-hour spike; replicas stayed at 12 and queue depth climbed. Creating bridge per playbook; paging SRE on-call with request IDs + pool name.
2026-02-12 (Rafael Mendes, SRE): Looking at autoscaler events now. I see triggers met but scale-out blocked; suspect max cap. Pulling current policy + scheduler logs. Will update in 30m.

2026-02-12 (Rafael Mendes, SRE): Confirmed: burst_max_replicas set to 20 but pool max is still effectively 12 due to legacy schema path. Applying override to raise cap and manually scaling to 16 (capacity constraints in cell).

2026-02-12 (Priya Natarajan, Platform): +1 on schema mismatch. This tenant created before burst policy v2 rollout; we need validation/upgrade path. Please link to ENG-4829 for guardrail + clear UI/console messaging.

2026-02-12 (Rachel Kim, CS): Customer asked whether this counts as SLA breach. I responded that we’re actively mitigating and will follow up after stability; please keep timeline + comms in ticket for potential credits review if requested.

2026-02-13 (Rafael Mendes, SRE): Capacity note: us-east A100 saturation is real blocker for full burst. We reserved additional headroom in adjacent cell and adjusted placement. Watching next scheduled spike to confirm burst reaches 18.

2026-02-14 (Owen Phillips, Support): Customer confirms improved behavior at peak; replicas scaled to 17 within ~4 minutes and latency stayed <2s p95. Leaving ticket open for 1 more spike window before resolving.

2026-02-16 (Rafael Mendes, SRE): Observed two additional spikes; burst activation successful and no 'scale_out_blocked' events. Marking resolved. Follow-up remains with ENG-4829 + capacity planning action.
attachments: Customer-provided timestamps + request ID sample set (stored in Support drive; not for external sharing)
Internal dashboard link: Dedicated pool autoscaler events (use Support-safe quick view when sharing internally)

source.summary: Streaming intermittently stalls for Northwind Robotics (no [DONE], client waits indefinitely)
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Customer sees streaming responses stop after some tokens. Connection remains open; no terminal event.

Impact:
- Their in-product assistant gets stuck; they cancel and retry.

Environment:
- Hosted API in eu-west region.
- Using server-sent events over HTTPS via corporate proxy.

Triage notes:
- Metrics show elevated open connections with low egress bytes.
- Customer proxy may buffer small chunks; they reported it improves when disabling proxy or increasing chunk size.

Workaround shared:
- Disable HTTP buffering on proxy (nginx: proxy_buffering off), or use websocket transport (if available), or set client read timeout + retry.

Resolution:
- Not fully reproducible internally; linked to known issue page and provided proxy guidance. Marking closed once customer confirmed workaround reduced occurrences.
comments: Ben Carter: Customer says it started last week when they moved traffic to eu-west. They use a corporate outbound proxy.
Elliot Price: Asked for curl -N reproduction without proxy; suspicion is buffering. Not seeing runtime errors.
Ben Carter: Customer confirms proxy_buffering off helped. Still sees rare stalls but acceptable; closing ticket.

source.summary: 5xx spike during customer A/B test rollout; request configurable auto-rollback thresholds + visibility into rollback decisions
source.description:
Customer (Northstar HR) ran an A/B test (baseline model vs candidate quantized variant) on their primary chat completion route and observed a short but sharp increase in HTTP 5xx responses.

They are requesting:
1) Configurable safety rails for A/B and canary rollouts (auto-pause/rollback based on error-rate and latency thresholds).
2) Better visibility into rollout decisions: what triggered a pause/rollback, which monitors were evaluated, what thresholds/windows were applied, and links to the supporting metrics.

Issue Summary
- During an A/B test (approx. 10% candidate traffic), 5xx rate spiked above their internal SLO. Customer manually reverted traffic split to baseline.
- Customer states that existing alerts were not actionable enough during the event because they lacked a clear explanation of which part of the rollout was responsible and what the platform would have done automatically.

Impact
- End-user facing errors on their app (customer-reported).
- Customer temporarily disabled the A/B test and is hesitant to resume without automated rollback and auditability.

Customer-reported timeline (UTC)
- 2026-02-06 18:22: Enabled A/B test: baseline 90% / candidate 10%.
- 2026-02-06 18:29: Noticed elevated 5xx on /v1/chat.completions.
- 2026-02-06 18:33: Reverted candidate traffic to 0% (baseline 100%).
- 2026-02-06 18:38: 5xx returned to normal.

Environment / Scope
- Product: Hosted API
- Route: /v1/chat.completions
- Regions: us-east and eu-west
- Customer tier: Enterprise

What they want (exact asks from customer)
- Per-route and per-rollout thresholds (error-rate + p95/p99 latency), configurable in Console and via policy API.
- Choice of action: pause rollout vs rollback to last-known-good.
- “Why did we rollback?” view with:
  - evaluated signals and time windows
  - minimum sample-size gating
  - suppression / override indicators
  - links to dashboards and monitor definitions

Initial hypotheses
- Candidate model serving pool had transient saturation / mis-sized concurrency leading to elevated upstream 5xx (timeouts -> gateway mapped to 5xx).
- A/B scope may not have had a dedicated rollout-scoped SLO monitor; customer relied on aggregate service monitor, delaying detection and making attribution harder.

Links (internal)
- Slack: #incidents thread sources/slack/incidents/1770705405.json
- SRE discussion on defaults: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770039055.json
- PM MVP scope: sources/linear/product-management/PM-612-auto-rollback-on-slo-breach-mvp.json
comments: 2026-02-06 (Liam O'Rourke): Customer reported 5xx spike during A/B (baseline vs q4 candidate). They’re asking for auto rollback thresholds + an audit trail of rollback decisions. They explicitly want this to be configurable per route and to see it in Console. Creating ticket to track support investigation + feed into product work.

2026-02-06 (Rafael Mendes): Acknowledged. Pulling logs and metrics for their org + route. Will confirm if 5xx were gateway-level vs upstream inference timeouts. Also checking whether rollout-scoped monitors exist for this route/model/region.

2026-02-06 (Aisha Bello): From quick dashboard check (internal): elevated 5xx coincided with tail latency rise on candidate shard. Looks like saturation in us-east first, then eu-west. Sample sizes are high enough that this was a real event, not noise. I don’t see a rollout-scoped burn-rate monitor for the model version label; only service aggregate.

2026-02-07 (Chloe Martin): Controller currently supports manual pause/revert via Console, but no automatic action yet. We have an in-flight project to wire rollout controller to SLO monitors with configurable thresholds and an audit trail. Adding this ticket as customer input to PRD.

2026-02-07 (Rafael Mendes): Workaround recommended to customer: keep candidate traffic <= 2% while validating, and add explicit alerting on 5xx + latency for candidate label dimensions (where available). Also suggested running the candidate in a dedicated capacity pool if they need stronger isolation. Waiting on customer confirmation of next test window.

2026-02-08 (Sean Gallagher): For auto action semantics: in early MVP we’re leaning “auto-pause first” to avoid oscillation, then allow rollback once we have stable debounce/min-sample logic and robust auditability. Customer request aligns with PRD (per-tier defaults + overrides).

2026-02-10 (Rafael Mendes): Confirmed via logs: majority of errors were upstream request timeouts from candidate shard; gateway surfaced as 5xx. No evidence of auth/rate-limit. Once traffic split reverted, candidate saturation cleared and error rate normalized within minutes.

2026-02-12 (Liam O'Rourke): Customer follow-up: they can accept auto-pause for MVP but require (a) configurable thresholds, and (b) a clear Console event explaining action taken/suppressed. They also asked for a way to suppress auto actions during planned load tests (with justification).

2026-02-14 (Rafael Mendes): Marking resolved from support perspective: incident mitigated by reverting A/B traffic; root cause attributed to candidate capacity/saturation. Product request captured and linked to ongoing auto-rollback-on-SLO-breach work. Customer advised we’ll update them when opt-in is available behind feature flag and when audit timeline UI ships.
source.resolution: Mitigated by reverting A/B candidate traffic to baseline (0%). Investigation indicates upstream timeouts/saturation on candidate shard caused gateway 5xx. No rollout-scoped SLO monitor was configured for the exact A/B scope; attribution and alerting relied on aggregate monitors. Customer request for configurable auto pause/rollback thresholds and audit visibility has been captured and linked to the auto-rollback-on-SLO-breach initiative (planned staged enablement behind feature flag).

title: 5xx spike during customer A/B test rollout; request configurable auto-rollback thresholds + visibility into rollback decisions
preview:
Customer (Northstar HR) ran an A/B test (baseline model vs candidate quantized variant) on their primary chat completion route and observed a short but sharp increase in HTTP 5xx responses.

They are requesting:
1) Configurable safety rails for A/B and canary rollouts (auto-pause/rollback based on error-rate and latency thresholds).
2) Better visibility into rollout decisions: what triggered a pause/rollback, which monitors were evaluated, what thresholds/windows were applied, and links to the supporting metrics.

Issue Summary
- During an A/B test (approx. 10% candidate traffic), 5xx rate spiked above their internal SLO. Customer manually reverted traffic split to baseline.
- Customer states that existing alerts were not actionable enough during the event because they lacked a clear explanation of which part of the rollout was responsible and what the platform would have done automatically.

Impact
- End-user facing errors on their app (customer-reported).
- Customer temporarily disabled the A/B test and is hesitant to resume without automated rollback and auditability.

Customer-reported timeline (UTC)
- 2026-02-06 18:22: Enabled A/B test: baseline 90% / candidate 10%.
- 2026-02-06 18:29: Noticed elevated 5xx on /v1/chat.completions.
- 2026-02-06 18:33: Reverted candidate traffic to 0% (baseline 100%).
- 2026-02-06 18:38: 5xx returned to normal.

Environment / Scope
- Product: Hosted API
- Route: /v1/chat.completions
- Regions: us-east and eu-west
- Customer tier: Enterprise

What they want (exact asks from customer)
- Per-route and per-rollout thresholds (error-rate + p95/p99 latency), configurable in Console and via policy API.
- Choice of action: pause rollout vs rollback to last-known-good.
- “Why did we rollback?” view with:
  - evaluated signals and time windows
  - minimum sample-size gating
  - suppression / override indicators
  - links to dashboards and monitor definitions

Initial hypotheses
- Candidate model serving pool had transient saturation / mis-sized concurrency leading to elevated upstream 5xx (timeouts -> gateway mapped to 5xx).
- A/B scope may not have had a dedicated rollout-scoped SLO monitor; customer relied on aggregate service monitor, delaying detection and making attribution harder.

Links (internal)
- Slack: #incidents thread sources/slack/incidents/1770705405.json
- SRE discussion on defaults: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770039055.json
- PM MVP scope: sources/linear/product-management/PM-612-auto-rollback-on-slo-breach-mvp.json

source.summary: Console SAML SSO login loop (redirects back to IdP repeatedly) for Vertex Legaltech
source.description:
Issue summary
- Customer reports they cannot access Redwood Console using SAML SSO. After authenticating successfully in the IdP, they are redirected back to Redwood Console briefly and then immediately redirected back to the IdP again (infinite loop). No error message shown to end user.

Impact
- Blocked access to Console for admins and analysts.
- Customer cannot review audit log exports / usage dashboards during an active internal review.

Customer
- Vertex Legaltech (enterprise)
- IdP: Okta (SAML 2.0)

Environment
- Console URL: https://console.redwoodinference.com
- Occurs on: Chrome 121, Firefox 122
- Networks tested: corporate VPN + non-VPN (same behavior)
- Time window first observed: 2026-02-11 ~16:20 UTC

Steps to reproduce (from customer)
1) Navigate to https://console.redwoodinference.com
2) Click “Sign in with SSO”
3) Select org: vertex-legaltech
4) Okta login completes (MFA prompt appears and succeeds)
5) Redirect returns to console, then redirects back to Okta immediately. Repeats.

Observed behavior
- Browser network trace shows repeated 302 chain:
  /login -> /sso/saml/vertex-legaltech -> (Okta) -> /api/auth/saml/callback -> /login
- Customer reports no visible error page.

Expected behavior
- After SAML assertion, user lands on Console home with an active session.

What we’ve collected / requested (customer-safe)
- Okta app “Identity Provider metadata” (XML) and settings screenshots (provided)
- Timestamped HAR file (requested; customer provided excerpt + key fields)
- One impacted user email domain + confirmation whether SCIM/JIT provisioning is enabled (provided)

SAML / IdP details provided by customer (sanitized)
- IdP metadata URL: https://vertex.okta.com/app/<redacted>/sso/saml/metadata
- Issuer (IdP Entity ID): http://www.okta.com/<redacted>
- SSO URL: https://vertex.okta.com/app/<redacted>/sso/saml
- X.509 cert: present, valid through 2027-01-xx
- Assertion audience (from Okta preview): https://console.redwoodinference.com/saml/metadata/vertex-legaltech
- ACS URL configured in Okta: https://console.redwoodinference.com/api/auth/saml/callback
- NameID format: urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:unspecified
- NameID value: Okta username (NOT guaranteed to be email)
- Attribute statements:
  - email = (not configured)
  - firstName/lastName = (configured)
  - groups = (configured; 100-group limit enabled)

Initial hypothesis
- SAML assertion accepted at callback but user identity cannot be resolved to a Redwood user (missing/invalid email claim), causing auth to fall back to /login and re-initiate SSO.
- Secondary hypothesis: cookie/session not being set (SameSite / domain mismatch), but loop appears tied to identity mapping rather than browser cookie blocking (reproduces across browsers; no third-party cookie prompts observed).

Escalation path
- Platform Control Plane / Auth owners: Ethan Park (assignee), Platform on-call per escalation playbook.
- If urgent / multiple enterprise customers affected: page SRE via #incidents and follow P1/P0 triggers. Currently appears isolated to a single customer config.

Attachments / references
- Slack thread: #support (internal) “Vertex SAML loop” started 2026-02-11 17:05 UTC (link redacted in ticket export)
- Customer excerpt: browser shows requestId header on callback response: x-redwood-request-id: req_01HS9K*** (redacted)

Notes
- Please avoid copying customer-specific Okta URLs or certificates outside this ticket + the customer’s secure thread.
comments:
2026-02-11 17:12 UTC — Priya Shah (Support)
Acknowledged receipt to customer per enterprise SLA. Requested: (1) Okta app settings screenshot for NameID + attribute statements, (2) one SAML assertion preview with sensitive values redacted, (3) confirmation whether user exists in Redwood Console (invited) vs relying on JIT.

Internal: Assigning to Platform (Control Plane/Auth).

2026-02-11 18:01 UTC — Rachel Kim (Enterprise CS)
Flagging customer context: Vertex is in a compliance review and needs Console access today. They’re fine with a configuration workaround; prefer no custom code. Please provide a clear customer-safe instruction set we can send.

If workaround is “change NameID to email”, confirm exact claim Redwood expects.

2026-02-11 18:22 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform)
Looked at auth logs around the request-id provided (req_01HS9K***). Callback succeeds (SAMLResponse parses), but we fail user resolution with reason:
- "principal_email_missing" (no email attribute and NameID not email-like)
Then we redirect to /login (which immediately triggers SSO for org that enforces SSO), hence the loop.

Action for customer:
- In Okta SAML app, set NameID format = EmailAddress and Application username = Email (or set attribute statement "email" -> user.email).
- Ensure the value matches the user’s login email in Redwood and domain allowlist (if configured).

Also note: if they rely on JIT provisioning, we require an email value. If SCIM is enabled, email still must be present.

I’ll draft exact text for Support to send.

2026-02-11 18:35 UTC — Priya Shah (Support)
Sent customer-safe instructions drafted from Ethan’s notes:
1) Okta Admin → Applications → (Redwood Console) → Sign On → SAML Settings
2) Set Name ID format: EmailAddress
3) Set Application username: Email
4) Add Attribute Statements:
   - Name: email | Value: user.email
5) Save and re-test in an incognito window

Asked customer to confirm whether the impacted user email matches what was used to invite the user in Redwood Console.

Update cadence: next update in 2 hours or sooner on confirmation.

2026-02-11 20:06 UTC — Customer (via Support)
Customer updated Okta settings. Loop stopped for one admin user; another user still loops.
Additional detail: second user’s Okta username is not their email; they can change it but would prefer mapping.

Customer asks: can Redwood accept "userPrincipalName" or another claim instead of email?

2026-02-11 20:22 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform)
We can accept email via attribute statement named "email". We do not currently support arbitrary claim mapping per org; the mapping is fixed.

If NameID is not email, ensure the Attribute Statement "email" is set to user.email for all users (not user.login if that’s not an email).

For the user still looping: likely they have a Redwood Console user created with a different email, or their Okta profile email is blank/unverified. Ask them to confirm user.email in Okta directory for that user.

If they want to use UPN instead: set "email" attribute to user.userPrincipalName (only if it is a routable email and matches the Redwood user email).

2026-02-12 16:05 UTC — Priya Shah (Support)
Customer confirmed the affected user’s Okta profile had email = blank (legacy contractor account). They populated email and re-tested; SSO succeeds.

Customer request: document required SAML attributes for Redwood Console SSO (so they don’t hit this again). I pointed them to existing SSO setup guide (public doc) and offered to add a troubleshooting note internally.

Creating internal follow-up suggestion: add clearer error page instead of silent redirect loop when principal_email_missing.

2026-02-12 16:18 UTC — Ethan Park (Platform)
Agree. The current UX is confusing. I’ll log an internal improvement request with Platform to render a customer-safe error page on missing principal email (e.g., “Your SSO profile is missing an email attribute; contact your IT admin”).

For now, support workaround is correct: require email attribute.

2026-02-13 09:40 UTC — Priya Shah (Support)
Closing out with customer: confirmed multiple users can now log in via SSO, no further looping. Provided summary + preventative checklist:
- Ensure Attribute Statement "email" is mapped and non-empty for all users
- Ensure Redwood Console user emails match IdP email values
- Test in incognito after changes

Marking Resolved.
source.resolution:
Root cause: SAML assertion did not include a usable email identifier (no "email" attribute; NameID was configured as Okta username which was not an email for some users). Redwood Console could not resolve/principals without email, redirected back to /login which re-initiated SSO (appearing as a login loop).

Fix / Workaround (customer action): Updated Okta SAML app to provide email consistently:
- Set NameID format to EmailAddress and Application username to Email, and/or
- Add Attribute Statement "email" = user.email (or user.userPrincipalName if it is the correct email and matches Redwood Console user records)
Additionally, corrected one user’s Okta profile with missing email.

Customer outcome: SSO login successful for all tested users.

Follow-ups:
- Platform to consider a customer-safe error page instead of silent loop on missing principal email (not tracked in this ticket).
- Support: add/ensure KB troubleshooting callout in SSO setup docs (internal).

source.summary: GPU OOM during high-churn chat sessions (suspected KV allocator fragmentation) - Northwind Analytics
source.description:
Issue summary:
Intermittent GPU OOMs in Northwind Analytics dedicated pool during high-churn chat traffic (many short-lived sessions + cancellations). OOMs occur despite dashboards showing non-zero free GPU memory prior to failure. Initial hypothesis: KV cache arena fragmentation causing allocation failures / forced request shedding.

Impact:
- Customer saw ~0.6% of chat requests fail with 5xx between 2026-01-26 21:10 UTC and 2026-01-27 02:40 UTC.
- Two pods restarted due to CUDA OOM; transient increased p99 latency (~+1.2s) while pool rebalanced.
- Customer escalated due to concern about stability during upcoming load test.

Customer-reported behavior:
- Failures clustered during bursty load (spikes to 3–4x baseline QPS) with frequent client cancellations (mobile app backgrounding).
- Largest prompts were not the trigger; failures correlated with churn.

Environment:
- Dedicated: nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03 (A100 80GB)
- Region: us-east
- Runtime: 1.21.3-redwood (customer pinned)
- Models: llama-3.1-70b-instruct (primary), qwen2.5-72b-instruct (A/B)

Steps to reproduce (from customer + internal replay):
1) Create ~1,200 concurrent chat sessions.
2) Mix request sizes (prompt tokens: 200–2,000; target decode: 64–512 tokens).
3) Cancel ~20–35% of requests during decode (client disconnect / explicit cancel).
4) Repeat in bursts (30s spike, 60–90s trough) for 20–30 minutes.

Observed errors (excerpt):
- "CUDA out of memory" from KV allocation path.
- Preceded by increasing allocation retries and occasional opportunistic evictions.

Requested from customer:
- Request IDs around OOM window
- Confirmation of cancellation rate and client retry behavior
- Whether any long-lived pinned sessions exist (none reported)

Notes:
This ticket is tracking customer impact and the mitigation applied in the dedicated pool while KV compaction/eviction improvements roll out more broadly.
source.investigation_notes:
Timeline + investigation notes:

2026-01-27 (00:18 UTC) - Detection
- Oncall (Rafael Mendes) paged for elevated 5xx + pod restarts in nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03.
- Node exporter + NVML showed sawtooth free memory; OOM occurred while ~6–10GB free was reported, suggesting fragmentation / inability to satisfy a large contiguous allocation.

Allocator/KV signals (new + existing):
- KV arena fragmentation estimate (internal debug snapshot) increased over time; free bytes were present but split across many small extents.
- Spike in "kv_alloc_retry" and "kv_page_reclaim" counters.
- Prefix cache hit rate steady; no clear correlation with OOM.

Hypothesis
- High churn (cancels) creates many short-lived KV segments.
- Long-lived sessions keep older allocations pinned.
- Free list degenerates into many small holes; allocation path increasingly scans/retries.
- Under burst, allocator fails to find contiguous pages for new decode segments and triggers CUDA OOM (or fails during internal arena growth/defrag path).

Reproduction
- Runtime team (Omar El-Sayed + Samir Qureshi) replayed traffic pattern using a synthetic churn harness in staging and reproduced a similar fragmentation pattern within ~25 minutes.

Related work
- Compaction/eviction project already in flight; metrics PR referenced for fragmentation counters.
- Kill-switch/config plumbing PR referenced for safe toggling in Dedicated.

Customer-specific considerations
- Dedicated pool has predictable traffic but spiky bursts; better to accept slightly higher background maintenance than hard OOM.
- Customer requested no model changes and minimal downtime.

Decision
- Apply mitigation via runtime flags in customer pool to reduce worst-case fragmentation behavior until compaction is GA:
  (a) Enable compaction in "budgeted" mode.
  (b) Lower fragmentation trigger threshold to start earlier.
  (c) Slightly increase safety headroom to avoid operating near the cliff.

Risk assessment
- Main risk: compaction work adding latency spikes if not budgeted; mitigated by strict budget and scheduler boundary enforcement.
- Rollback plan: flip kill-switch to disable compaction if p99 regresses >10% sustained for 10 minutes (per SRE guardrails).
source.resolution:
Resolution / mitigation applied:

2026-01-27 (02:55 UTC)
- Applied dedicated-pool runtime config change (no model change) via control-plane rollout:
  - runtime.kv_compaction.enabled = true
  - runtime.kv_compaction.mode = incremental
  - runtime.kv_compaction.budget_ms_per_step = 0.25
  - runtime.kv_compaction.max_bytes_moved_per_step = 268435456 (256MiB)
  - runtime.kv_fragmentation.trigger_ratio = 0.18 (was 0.30)
  - runtime.kv_allocator.safety_headroom_mb = 2048 (was 1024)
  - runtime.kv_eviction.tier_aware = true (Dedicated: prefer evicting preemptible/prefix cache before active decode)

Observed outcome (next 24h):
- No further CUDA OOMs in nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03.
- p99 latency: neutral to slightly improved during bursts (estimated -4% to -7% in the affected windows).
- Compaction counters show steady low-volume moves; no single-step spikes exceeding budget.

Customer communication:
- Confirmed mitigation deployed and asked customer to re-run the same high-churn test; customer reported stability improvement and no failures over 45 minutes (per Support thread).

Follow-ups:
- Keep customer pinned on 1.21.3 but maintain pool-level flags until runtime 1.22 rollout is complete.
- Add this workload to perf canary / benchmark suite gating (linked to ENG-4828 externally; tracked internally by Runtime).

Close criteria:
- Ticket marked Resolved after 7 consecutive days with no OOMs and customer confirmation of successful retest.
- Will reopen if OOM recurs or if p99 regressions are observed from compaction settings.
comments: 2026-01-27 00:26 UTC — Rafael Mendes (Oncall): Opened SUP-28433 after two pod restarts (CUDA OOM) in nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03. Linking #incidents thread and requesting Runtime support. Customer impact: burst windows only.

2026-01-27 00:41 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Looking at SLO panels. Not seeing overall GPU saturation; the failure pattern looks like allocator thrash. Can we capture KV allocator snapshot + fragmentation ratio? Also confirm cancellation rate from customer.

2026-01-27 01:05 UTC — Support (triage note, via #support): Northwind says ~30% cancel rate on mobile during decode; retries enabled with exponential backoff (max 3). No pinned sessions; they do have a long-lived system prompt prefix shared across sessions.

2026-01-27 01:22 UTC — Omar El-Sayed (Runtime): This matches KV arena fragmentation under churn. We can mitigate with early compaction trigger + strict budget. Need SRE sign-off on rollout guardrails and a rollback condition.

2026-01-27 01:38 UTC — Allison Grant (Observability): For the dedicated pool, please ensure we’re emitting: kv_fragmentation_ratio, kv_compaction_bytes_moved_total, kv_compaction_step_duration_ms, kv_evictions_total (tier breakdown). Dashboard request already tracked in INT-11904.

2026-01-27 02:11 UTC — Samir Qureshi (Runtime): Staging repro succeeded with churn harness; fragmentation climbs steadily and allocation retries spike before OOM. Suggest lowering trigger ratio to 0.18 and enabling incremental compaction. Copy cost is small if we cap to 0.25ms/step.

2026-01-27 02:57 UTC — Rafael Mendes (Oncall): Config pushed to nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03. Monitoring for 30 minutes before declaring stable. Rollback plan: disable runtime.kv_compaction.enabled if p99 > +10% for 10m or if compaction step duration exceeds budget repeatedly.

2026-01-27 03:44 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Post-change looks good. p99 stable, compaction steps under budget. No new OOMs. Keep an eye on eviction rate; don’t want quality/user-visible truncations (shouldn’t happen if evicting preemptible/prefix first).

2026-01-28 16:10 UTC — Support: Customer reran the load test for 45 minutes, no 5xx observed. They request we keep this mitigation until the next runtime train and ask for guidance on what to monitor on their side.

2026-02-10 09:03 UTC — Omar El-Sayed (Runtime): Closing as Resolved. No OOMs since mitigation. Follow-up work continues in runtime 1.22 compaction rollout; linking PRs for metrics and config plumbing.

title: GPU OOM during high-churn chat sessions (suspected KV allocator fragmentation) - Northwind Analytics
preview:
Issue summary:
Intermittent GPU OOMs in Northwind Analytics dedicated pool during high-churn chat traffic (many short-lived sessions + cancellations). OOMs occur despite dashboards showing non-zero free GPU memory prior to failure. Initial hypothesis: KV cache arena fragmentation causing allocation failures / forced request shedding.

Impact:
- Customer saw ~0.6% of chat requests fail with 5xx between 2026-01-26 21:10 UTC and 2026-01-27 02:40 UTC.
- Two pods restarted due to CUDA OOM; transient increased p99 latency (~+1.2s) while pool rebalanced.
- Customer escalated due to concern about stability during upcoming load test.

Customer-reported behavior:
- Failures clustered during bursty load (spikes to 3–4x baseline QPS) with frequent client cancellations (mobile app backgrounding).
- Largest prompts were not the trigger; failures correlated with churn.

Environment:
- Dedicated: nw-analytics-gpu-pool-03 (A100 80GB)
- Region: us-east
- Runtime: 1.21.3-redwood (customer pinned)
- Models: llama-3.1-70b-instruct (primary), qwen2.5-72b-instruct (A/B)

Steps to reproduce (from customer + internal replay):
1) Create ~1,200 concurrent chat sessions.
2) Mix request sizes (prompt tokens: 200–2,000; target decode: 64–512 tokens).
3) Cancel ~20–35% of requests during decode (client disconnect / explicit cancel).
4) Repeat in bursts (30s spike, 60–90s trough) for 20–30 minutes.

Observed errors (excerpt):
- "CUDA out of memory" from KV allocation path.
- Preceded by increasing allocation retries and occasional opportunistic evictions.

Requested from customer:
- Request IDs around OOM window
- Confirmation of cancellation rate and client retry behavior
- Whether any long-lived pinned sessions exist (none reported)

Notes:
This ticket is tracking customer impact and the mitigation applied in the dedicated pool while KV compaction/eviction improvements roll out more broadly.

source.summary: Latency regression (p95) on model-xyz after runtime release (us-east Dedicated)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Customer (Tensorlytics) reports increased p95 latency on Dedicated traffic for model-xyz immediately following Redwood runtime release rollout in us-east.
- Report references internal canary alert firing around the same time.

Customer impact
- Elevated p95 end-to-end latency on generation requests; intermittent client-side timeouts (customer timeout set to 3.0s).
- Customer reports downstream job SLA impact on their real-time pipeline.

Scope / environment
- Environment: prod / us-east / Dedicated (Tensorlytics)
- Model: model-xyz (customer pinned; no model-version change reported by customer)
- Start time (customer-reported): ~2026-02-07 08:40 UTC
- Runtime change window: runtime rollout started ~2026-02-07 08:15 UTC (us-east dedicated pools)

Observed metrics (Support + SRE verification)
- Baseline (prior 24h): p95 e2e latency ~1.25s; tokens/sec steady (within normal variance)
- During incident window: p95 e2e latency ~1.85–2.10s (+45–65%); tokens/sec down ~15–20%
- perf-canary signal (internal): warning->paging transition for model-xyz p95 latency regression in us-east, sustained >30m

Initial hypothesis
- Regression is likely runtime/kernel-level (execution time increase) vs scheduler queueing, based on early perf-canary breakdown showing runtime-only latency shift with queueing roughly flat.

Steps to reproduce (internal)
1) Target us-east Dedicated inference endpoint for Tensorlytics pool.
2) Run synthetic prompts in the canary “medium+long prefill” buckets (prompt lengths ~1.5k–3k tokens; typical decode 128–256).
3) Compare runtime-only prefill and decode latency vs baseline artifact for model-xyz + H100-SXM class.

Triage notes / correlation
- Canary alert thread indicated a kernel tag change concurrent with runtime build change (kernel selection identifier changed for the prefill path).
- Queueing-time metrics did not show a corresponding increase; batch size distribution showed a mild downward shift but not enough to explain the full p95 movement.

Mitigation applied
- Rolled back runtime build for the affected dedicated pool in us-east to previous known-good build for model-xyz traffic.
- Additionally pinned kernel selection to prior kernel set for model-xyz (temporary safety measure while investigating new kernel path).

Outcome
- Post-rollback p95 returned to within ~5–10% of baseline within ~20 minutes; tokens/sec recovered.
- Customer confirmed latency returned to normal in their app monitoring.

Follow-ups
- Runtime team to root-cause new kernel path regression (prefill-heavy bucket) and add guardrail in kernel selection for model-xyz until fixed.
- SRE/Release Eng to update rollout checklist: require “top model” perf-canary green for 60 minutes before expanding runtime rollout in Dedicated.
- Support: send customer incident summary + confirmation of rollback, and note we will provide an RCA once available.

Customer-facing note (approved wording)
- “We identified a performance regression in our serving runtime rollout affecting model-xyz in us-east Dedicated. We mitigated by rolling back the runtime to the previous build, and latency has returned to normal. We’re continuing root-cause analysis on the new runtime path and will share more details and preventive steps when complete.”
comments: 2026-02-07 09:02 UTC — Rafael Mendes (Support/SRE): Customer (Tensorlytics) reports p95 jumped from ~1.2s to ~2.0s on model-xyz in us-east Dedicated. They’re seeing timeouts at 3s client timeout. Asking for request IDs + exact start time + whether model version changed on their side.

2026-02-07 09:11 UTC — Tensorlytics (Customer): Started noticing around 08:40 UTC. No application changes. Model pin unchanged. Example request IDs: req_7c1f… req_8a22… (more in attachment shared via secure channel).

2026-02-07 09:18 UTC — Aisha Bello (SRE): Confirmed perf-canary fired for model-xyz us-east about ~09:14 UTC (warning, then sustained). Dashboard shows runtime-only latency shift; queueing-time panel looks flat. Suggest checking kernel tag + runtime build tag for the pool.

2026-02-07 09:26 UTC — Omar El-Sayed (Runtime Instrumentation): Pulled telemetry for model-xyz: runtime_build changed to runtime-2026.02.07.1; kernel_selection_id changed on prefill path at same time. Decode looks stable; prefill p95 up ~40% in the long-prompt bucket.

2026-02-07 09:40 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE, assignee): Treating as Sev2 customer-impacting performance regression. Coordinating with runtime on rollback vs kernel pin. If rollback resolves quickly, we’ll keep investigation scoped to kernel change window.

2026-02-07 10:05 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet/Rollout): Initiated rollback for Tensorlytics us-east dedicated pool to runtime-2026.01.30.2. Also applied temporary kernel pin for model-xyz to previous kernel set (per Hiro’s recommendation). ETA ~15–20 min for full drain/replace.

2026-02-07 10:28 UTC — Caleb Johnson (Telemetry): Post-rollback metrics show p95 trending down; tokens/sec recovering. perf-canary no longer above threshold in last 10m, awaiting sustained confirmation window.
2026-02-07 10:44 UTC — Rafael Mendes (Support/SRE): Sent customer update: rollback in progress and early indications latency recovering. Asked them to confirm from their side once stable.
2026-02-07 11:02 UTC — Tensorlytics (Customer): Confirmed p95 back to normal range (~1.2–1.3s). Timeouts stopped. Thanks.

2026-02-08 16:15 UTC — Hiro Tanaka (Kernel): Early suspicion: new prefill kernel path regression for seq_len ~2k–3k on H100 in this build; reproducing in perf suite. Will provide RCA details once confirmed. Recommend keeping kernel pin for model-xyz until fix is merged and canary-green in us-east.

2026-02-10 09:30 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Marking Resolved. Mitigation: rollback + kernel pin. Action items tracked in runtime/release workflows (outside Jira Support). Will share RCA summary with customer when available.

source.summary: Latency regression after runtime-1.22 canary: elevated p99 decode latency correlated with KV compaction activity
source.content:
Issue Summary
- During a runtime-1.22 canary expansion in prod (hosted, us-east-1), we observed a statistically significant p99 latency regression on decode-heavy traffic, most visible for mixed sequence-length distributions.
- Regression correlated with elevated KV compaction work (bytes moved + compaction time) and increased allocator fragmentation ratio oscillation.
- Canary was rolled back (feature flag disabled + cohort drained) per guardrails.

Customer Impact
- Customer: Northwind Analytics (enterprise) reported increased p99 latency and intermittent request timeouts during bursty chat traffic.
- Symptoms: p95 largely stable; p99 degraded (notable tail spikes) during bursts and after churn events (rapid session start/stop).
- Scope: Only canary cohort in us-east-1. Control region (eu-west-1) did not show comparable change.

Detection
- 2026-01-29 09:12 PT: SRE alert fired: hosted decode p99 exceeded threshold for canary cohort for 10m.
- 2026-01-29 09:18 PT: Support received escalation via CSM thread referencing prior tail-latency concerns (see SUP-28411).

Environment
- Runtime: runtime-1.22 canary (hosted)
- Canary cohort: ~5% of us-east-1 hosted capacity (dedicated pools not included)
- Traffic pattern: mixed prompt lengths, bursty chat sessions, high cancellation/churn

Evidence / Observations
- Dashboard signals (high level):
  - p99 decode latency: +18–35% vs baseline during burst windows
  - Compaction bytes moved: spiky (peaks aligned with p99 spikes)
  - Compaction time per step: higher variance than staging
  - Eviction counters: low/near-zero (so tail impact likely compaction rather than eviction)
  - Fragmentation ratio: saw sawtooth behavior (trigger threshold repeatedly crossed)
- Trace exemplars (from compaction spans): decode loop shows intermittent stalls where compaction step executed near decode boundary.

Timeline / Comments
- 2026-01-29 09:22 PT (Sean Gallagher, SRE): Confirmed alert corresponds to canary nodes only. Requesting runtime team to validate compaction budget enforcement and whether compaction can run during decode.
- 2026-01-29 09:31 PT (Rafael Mendes, Oncall): Opened incident bridge in #incidents; linked thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json. Requested latest canary diff + feature flag defaults.
- 2026-01-29 09:40 PT (Sofia Ivanova, Runtime): Suspected scheduler boundary issue: compaction budget being applied but step can still run in an unfavorable phase when queues are shallow (burst recovery) and decode becomes latency-sensitive.
- 2026-01-29 09:55 PT (Allison Grant, Observability): Noted instrumentation overhead increase in the same canary build (compaction tracing spans). Suggested testing with spans disabled to isolate.
- 2026-01-29 10:05 PT (Decision): Roll back canary per hosted tail-latency guardrails. Disable KV compaction feature flag in hosted us-east-1 canary cohort; drain cohort to baseline build.
- 2026-01-29 10:11 PT (Rafael Mendes): Executed kill-switch via config propagation (validated propagation <2m). p99 began recovering within 15m as cohort drained.
- 2026-01-29 10:38 PT (Support): Informed Northwind Analytics we rolled back the canary change and are investigating; requested a 30-minute window of request IDs for trace correlation.
- 2026-01-29 12:10 PT (Samir Qureshi, Runtime): Preliminary hypothesis: compaction relocation step sometimes exceeds time budget due to larger-than-expected copy size when fragmentation is high; budget accounting is per-step but actual move cost depends on page locality. Also flagged potential overhead from tracing spans.
- 2026-01-30 08:45 PT (Runtime): Added follow-up to test: disable compaction spans (keep metrics) and re-run canary in a smaller cohort; additionally adjust compaction trigger hysteresis to reduce repeated threshold crossings.
- 2026-02-03 16:20 PT (Rafael Mendes): Confirmed no further p99 regressions after rollback; customer reports normalized behavior. Closing support incident; tracking engineering fixes under runtime epic/tickets.

Rollback / Mitigation Actions Taken
- Rolled back canary cohort in us-east-1 hosted (KV compaction kill-switch OFF).
- Left fragmentation metrics ON for continued monitoring.
- Paused further canary expansion pending fixes + perf canary pass.

Root Cause (Provisional)
- KV compaction work occasionally executed at an unfavorable time relative to decode scheduling, causing transient stalls that manifest as p99 spikes under bursty mixed-length workloads.
- Contributing factors:
  - Compaction trigger oscillation (threshold crossed repeatedly) causing repeated compaction attempts.
  - Increased tracing span overhead in the same build (amplifying tail effects).

Resolution
- Customer-facing: Regression resolved by rolling back canary (feature flag disabled; nodes drained to baseline).
- Internal: Incident closed with action items assigned; canary expansion blocked until updated perf canary scenario and revised compaction budgeting/hysteresis are validated.

Follow-up Tasks / Next Steps
- Runtime (owner: Sofia Ivanova / Samir Qureshi):
  - Tighten scheduler phase gating: ensure compaction steps never run on latency-critical decode path when queue depth is below threshold.
  - Add hysteresis + cooldown to fragmentation trigger to prevent sawtooth compaction behavior.
  - Verify budget enforcement uses wall-clock + bytes moved with hard stop when approaching decode deadlines.
- Observability (owner: Allison Grant):
  - Add dashboard panel overlay: p99 vs compaction bytes moved and compaction step time.
  - Reduce tracing overhead or sample compaction spans; keep metrics at full fidelity.
- SRE (owner: Sean Gallagher):
  - Update automated rollback condition to consider compaction bytes moved rate + p99 correlation (not just p99 absolute).

External Communication
- Northwind Analytics updated 2026-01-29: rollback performed; performance back to baseline; follow-up planned after next canary with improved guardrails.

References
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json
- Oncall procedures draft: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1767660044.json
- Guardrails: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-api-tail-latency-guardrails.json
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook.json
