  - p99 decode latency: +18–35% vs baseline during burst windows
  - Compaction bytes moved: spiky (peaks aligned with p99 spikes)
  - Compaction time per step: higher variance than staging
  - Eviction counters: low/near-zero (so tail impact likely compaction rather than eviction)
  - Fragmentation ratio: saw sawtooth behavior (trigger threshold repeatedly crossed)
- Trace exemplars (from compaction spans): decode loop shows intermittent stalls where compaction step executed near decode boundary.

Timeline / Comments
- 2026-01-29 09:22 PT (Sean Gallagher, SRE): Confirmed alert corresponds to canary nodes only. Requesting runtime team to validate compaction budget enforcement and whether compaction can run during decode.
- 2026-01-29 09:31 PT (Rafael Mendes, Oncall): Opened incident bridge in #incidents; linked thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json. Requested latest canary diff + feature flag defaults.
- 2026-01-29 09:40 PT (Sofia Ivanova, Runtime): Suspected scheduler boundary issue: compaction budget being applied but step can still run in an unfavorable phase when queues are shallow (burst recovery) and decode becomes latency-sensitive.
- 2026-01-29 09:55 PT (Allison Grant, Observability): Noted instrumentation overhead increase in the same canary build (compaction tracing spans). Suggested testing with spans disabled to isolate.
- 2026-01-29 10:05 PT (Decision): Roll back canary per hosted tail-latency guardrails. Disable KV compaction feature flag in hosted us-east-1 canary cohort; drain cohort to baseline build.
- 2026-01-29 10:11 PT (Rafael Mendes): Executed kill-switch via config propagation (validated propagation <2m). p99 began recovering within 15m as cohort drained.
- 2026-01-29 10:38 PT (Support): Informed Northwind Analytics we rolled back the canary change and are investigating; requested a 30-minute window of request IDs for trace correlation.
- 2026-01-29 12:10 PT (Samir Qureshi, Runtime): Preliminary hypothesis: compaction relocation step sometimes exceeds time budget due to larger-than-expected copy size when fragmentation is high; budget accounting is per-step but actual move cost depends on page locality. Also flagged potential overhead from tracing spans.
- 2026-01-30 08:45 PT (Runtime): Added follow-up to test: disable compaction spans (keep metrics) and re-run canary in a smaller cohort; additionally adjust compaction trigger hysteresis to reduce repeated threshold crossings.
- 2026-02-03 16:20 PT (Rafael Mendes): Confirmed no further p99 regressions after rollback; customer reports normalized behavior. Closing support incident; tracking engineering fixes under runtime epic/tickets.

Rollback / Mitigation Actions Taken
- Rolled back canary cohort in us-east-1 hosted (KV compaction kill-switch OFF).
- Left fragmentation metrics ON for continued monitoring.
- Paused further canary expansion pending fixes + perf canary pass.

Root Cause (Provisional)
- KV compaction work occasionally executed at an unfavorable time relative to decode scheduling, causing transient stalls that manifest as p99 spikes under bursty mixed-length workloads.
- Contributing factors:
  - Compaction trigger oscillation (threshold crossed repeatedly) causing repeated compaction attempts.
  - Increased tracing span overhead in the same build (amplifying tail effects).

Resolution
- Customer-facing: Regression resolved by rolling back canary (feature flag disabled; nodes drained to baseline).
- Internal: Incident closed with action items assigned; canary expansion blocked until updated perf canary scenario and revised compaction budgeting/hysteresis are validated.

Follow-up Tasks / Next Steps
- Runtime (owner: Sofia Ivanova / Samir Qureshi):
  - Tighten scheduler phase gating: ensure compaction steps never run on latency-critical decode path when queue depth is below threshold.
  - Add hysteresis + cooldown to fragmentation trigger to prevent sawtooth compaction behavior.
  - Verify budget enforcement uses wall-clock + bytes moved with hard stop when approaching decode deadlines.
- Observability (owner: Allison Grant):
  - Add dashboard panel overlay: p99 vs compaction bytes moved and compaction step time.
  - Reduce tracing overhead or sample compaction spans; keep metrics at full fidelity.
- SRE (owner: Sean Gallagher):
  - Update automated rollback condition to consider compaction bytes moved rate + p99 correlation (not just p99 absolute).

External Communication
- Northwind Analytics updated 2026-01-29: rollback performed; performance back to baseline; follow-up planned after next canary with improved guardrails.

References
- Slack incident thread: sources/slack/incidents/1767735109.json
- Oncall procedures draft: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1767660044.json
- Guardrails: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-api-tail-latency-guardrails.json
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runbooks/runtime-kv-cache-compaction-runbook.json

source.summary: Console onboarding nudge intermittently blocks API key creation (Create API key button unclickable)
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports they cannot create an API key in the Console. The "Create API key" button appears, but is not clickable / click does nothing.

Impact:
- Blocks activation milestone (API key creation → first request).
- Customer unable to complete onboarding and send first API call.
- Support volume risk during lifecycle growth experiments rollout (console nudges).

Customer context:
- Company: DevFlow AI
- Plan: self-serve Hosted API
- User report: "The onboarding checklist is telling me to create a key, but I can't click the button. It looks like something is on top of it."

Environment:
- Console: app.redwoodinference.com
- Observed in: prod (us-east)
- Browser: Chrome 121 / macOS (also reproduced on Arc)
- Feature flags: lifecycle growth console nudges enabled for cohort (holdout = false)

Steps to reproduce (internal repro):
1) Use a user in cohort with console onboarding nudges enabled.
2) Navigate to Console → Settings → API Keys.
3) If the "Next best action" nudge state is "create_api_key" and the nudge is rendered as a sticky banner, attempt to click "Create API key".
4) Result: button is visually enabled but click does not fire; cursor indicates underlying element is blocked.

Expected:
User can always create an API key. Nudges must not intercept pointer events on primary onboarding CTAs.

Actual:
In some states, the nudge container overlays the API key creation header/CTA region. Because the nudge wrapper has an incorrect z-index/pointer-events configuration, it captures clicks (and for some window sizes, overlaps the CTA entirely).

Notes / suspected cause:
- Regression likely introduced with the reusable nudge component rollout.
- Seems sensitive to viewport height and whether the checklist is expanded.
- Potential state mismatch: after dismissal, the nudge component re-renders on API Keys route with stale step state and keeps overlay position.

Workaround (until fix):
- Reduce zoom to 90% or collapse the checklist panel (if visible) and hard refresh.
- Alternatively create API key via the Redwood CLI (shared with customer) while we hotfix Console.

Requested outcome:
Hotfix in Console to ensure onboarding nudges never block API key creation flow. Validate across common viewports and keep behind the lifecycle-growth experiment flag.
comments: 2026-01-24 09:18 PT — Ben Carter (Reporter): Flagging from #support escalation. DevFlow AI cannot create API key; seems like the new onboarding nudge is sitting on top of the CTA. This is directly impacting activation funnel for the sprint cohorts.

2026-01-24 09:42 PT — Support (triage) (Ben Carter): Acknowledged to customer. Requested screenshot + browser/OS. Shared CLI workaround to unblock them for today. Set priority P1 due to activation blocker.

2026-01-24 10:05 PT — Kenji Nakamura (Design): Looked at current banner layout; on smaller viewports the sticky nudge bar can overlap the page header. Suggest we make the banner non-sticky on Settings routes or enforce a safe top offset. Also ensure pointer-events is only on buttons inside the nudge, not on the container.

2026-01-24 10:31 PT — Dylan Brooks (Assignee): Reproduced in prod with a test cohort user. Root cause: NudgeContainer wrapper has pointer-events:auto and spans full width with transparent background; z-index > header. Clicking CTA is intercepted. Implementing CSS fix + route-specific layout guard.

Action: patch NudgeContainer to pointer-events:none, enable pointer-events:auto only for interactive children; reduce z-index; add e2e check for API Keys route.

2026-01-24 11:12 PT — Chloe Martin: Confirmed route-level placement logic is using a generic header anchor; Settings pages have different stacking context. Suggest use dedicated mount point for Settings pages or disable this nudge placement on /settings/* until mount point ships.

2026-01-24 12:03 PT — Dylan Brooks: Hotfix proposal:
1) CSS pointer-events change as above (safe and low risk).
2) Add placement rule: do not render sticky nudge on /settings/api-keys; render inline below page title instead.
Shipping behind existing lifecycle growth flag; will ramp from 10% to 100% of flagged cohort after QA.

2026-01-24 13:26 PT — Leila Farouk (QA): Validated fix on Chrome/Safari, 100%/110% zoom, laptop viewport. Confirmed "Create API key" clickable in all tested variants. Also verified nudge dismiss persists and does not re-overlay on navigation back to API Keys.
2026-01-24 14:10 PT — Ben Carter: Customer updated: shared ETA (today) + workaround until confirmed. Requested they retest after we notify.

2026-01-24 16:02 PT — Dylan Brooks: Patch merged and deployed to prod behind flag. Ramp plan: 10% cohort for 1 hour monitoring console errors → 50% → 100% if clean. Monitoring: console route errors + click-through event to API key modal open.

Notes: no backend change; purely console layout / interaction fix.

2026-01-25 09:07 PT — Allison Grant (Observability): No spike in console JS errors post-deploy. Funnel event "api_key_modal_opened" recovered for the flagged cohort segment compared to prior hour. Looks stable.
2026-01-25 11:18 PT — Ben Carter: Confirmed with DevFlow AI: they can now create an API key and completed first request. Closing the loop; keeping ticket open until full ramp completes.
2026-01-29 15:40 PT — Dylan Brooks: Full ramp complete for lifecycle-growth cohorts. Added regression test coverage for Settings → API Keys route. Marking Resolved.

source.summary: Streaming responses freeze mid-generation for Arcadia Health (SSE stalls, request never completes)
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports streaming chat responses intermittently stop mid-generation. Client keeps the HTTP connection open; server stops emitting SSE tokens and never sends [DONE].

Impact:
- Production incident for Arcadia Health’s support-agent. End users see partial completions and stuck spinners.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool in us-east.
- Client uses OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Start streaming completion with max_tokens ~800.
2) After ~10–40 seconds, stream stalls mid-sentence.
3) No further bytes, no close, no error.

Investigation notes:
- Correlates with longer generations + high concurrent streams.
- Gateway shows upstream still open; runtime logs show request context cancelled late or not at all.
- Suspected backpressure interaction between token streamer + gzip + chunked transfer.

Workaround:
- Customer can set client-side read timeout and retry with idempotency key.

Resolution:
- Rolled out gateway change: disable gzip for SSE responses and flush after each token chunk.
- Added runtime guard: heartbeat/flush every 2s while streaming.

References:
- Slack: #support thread_ts 1769084201
- Customer emails in Ava Chen mailbox (thread_id gth_9a3f1b0a)
comments: Ava Chen: Customer escalation via email + screenshot of stalled curl. They see it ~1 in 20 requests; worse at peak.
Alex Martinez: Seeing similar symptoms in perf-canary when gzip enabled. I can reproduce in staging with 200 concurrent streams.
Dylan Brooks: Gateway fix in progress; will ship a config toggle for SSE gzip off by default.
Ava Chen: Confirmed with customer: after change, no stalls observed over 2 hours of load test. Closing with workaround note still recommended on client side.

source.summary: Proxima Bank: unexpected spike in 429s after enterprise priority routing rollout (chat + embeddings)
source.description:
## Issue summary
Customer (Proxima Bank) reports a sharp increase in HTTP 429 responses starting ~2026-01-20 09:10 PT on /v1/chat/completions and /v1/embeddings. They believe this correlates with Redwood’s recent “priority routing / capacity protection” changes.

## Impact
- Customer batch + online traffic intermittently fails with 429s, causing retry storms on their side.
- Reported elevated end-user latency due to retries; some requests fail after their max-retry budget.
- Customer concern: enterprise tier expected fewer 429s, not more.

## Environment
- Hosted API, prod
- Region: us-east
- Routes: /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings
- Auth: enterprise API key (account: PROXIMA-ENT-014)

## What customer is seeing
- 429 response body includes Redwood reason codes (examples pasted by customer):
  - "reason": "overload_protection_admission"
  - "reason": "quota_exceeded" (less frequent)
- Response headers observed:
  - Retry-After: 1 (sometimes 2)
  - X-Redwood-Request-Id present
  - X-Redwood-Throttle-Reason: overload_protection_admission (most common)

## Customer notes
- They run bursty workloads: 10–30s spikes during market-open events.
- Client currently retries immediately with fixed 100ms delay and no jitter.

## Steps to reproduce (customer)
1) Send ~800–1200 RPS bursts to /v1/chat/completions with streaming disabled.
2) Concurrently send embedding bursts (~2k RPS) during the same window.
3) Observe 429 rates rising from <0.2% to 6–12% during bursts.

## Expected behavior
- Enterprise priority should protect them during fleet pressure; customer expects either stable acceptance or clearly defined, predictable throttling limits.

## Actual behavior
- Customer sees throttling during burst windows and requests clarification on:
  1) Why enterprise traffic is being rejected
  2) Whether the 429s are overload shedding vs quota
  3) Whether Redwood can grant a temporary burst exception / larger budget

## Initial support response (guidance provided)
- Confirmed that the new system returns distinct reason codes and customers should key their retry/backoff behavior on:
  - "quota_exceeded" (respect quota windows; reduce steady-state rate)
  - "overload_protection_admission" (transient overload; respect Retry-After; use exponential backoff + jitter)
- Recommended changes:
  - Use exponential backoff with full jitter; cap concurrency; do not retry immediately.
  - Honor Retry-After; treat 429 as a signal to shed load client-side.

## Investigation notes (internal)
Hypothesis: us-east protected-route capacity protection enabled for Proxima allowlisted accounts; per-route/per-tier admission control is active and may be gating bursts more aggressively than intended for this customer’s contractual traffic shape.

Checks performed:
- Correlated spike with protection annotations in hot-route dashboard (SRE): overload protection activated for route-group "chat" + "embeddings" during a concurrent regional pressure event.
- Confirmed most 429s for this account carry reason=overload_protection_admission (not quota).
- Verified no corresponding 5xx spike; p99 latency for enterprise class remained within SLO during event, indicating protections are working for fleet health but customer’s burst profile is being clipped.

Open questions
- What is Proxima’s contracted peak/burst behavior (RPS and token/s) for these routes?
- Is Proxima being routed to a stricter per-route budget due to route-group defaults?

## Requested outcome
- Explain semantics and provide an actionable plan.
- If eligible, grant a temporary burst exception with guardrails (time-boxed, monitored), or propose a dedicated capacity option.
comments: 2026-01-20 09:26 PT - Owen Phillips (Support): Customer reports 10x increase in 429s since this morning. Asking if we changed rate limiting / routing. Provided immediate guidance: honor Retry-After, implement exponential backoff + jitter, and send us 10 sample X-Redwood-Request-Id values from 429s and 200s around the same time window.

2026-01-20 10:02 PT - Customer (Proxima Bank): Shared 12 request IDs and headers. Majority show X-Redwood-Throttle-Reason=overload_protection_admission; a few quota_exceeded. They confirm current retry policy is fixed 100ms, no jitter, up to 10 retries.

2026-01-20 10:18 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Acknowledged correlation with overload protection activation in us-east. Confirmed this is not a billing quota-only issue; most are admission control during regional pressure. Asked Platform to confirm policy config for PROXIMA-ENT-014 and whether they’re on canary allowlist.

2026-01-20 10:41 PT - Jordan Lee (Policy Engine): PROXIMA-ENT-014 is in enterprise priority class, but route-group defaults include a conservative burst multiplier. Their combined chat+embeddings bursts can exceed the enterprise route budget during pressure windows (designed to preserve latency SLO). Decision logs show deny reason=admission_over_budget with retry_after_seconds=1–2.

2026-01-20 11:07 PT - Amara Diallo (API Gateway): Confirmed gateway is emitting new reason codes/headers from PR-27441. For this account, 429 payloads are consistent. Suggested customer should branch retry logic by reason: quota_exceeded (longer wait; smooth traffic) vs overload/admission (short retry-after, but exponential backoff and reduced concurrency).

2026-01-20 11:32 PT - Mina Haddad (Routing): During 09:10–09:30 PT, us-east had elevated scheduler lag and queue depth on shared pools; best-effort traffic was shed first, but enterprise admission control still clips if the enterprise route budget is exceeded. No evidence of misrouting or fallback. This appears like expected behavior but thresholds may be too tight for Proxima’s contract.

2026-01-20 12:10 PT - Owen Phillips (Support): Sent customer explanation of reason codes and concrete retry guidance. Asked for their target peak RPS and max prompt/response sizes during market-open events. Offered a call with TAM (Liam O'Rourke) to align on traffic shape and burst needs.

2026-01-21 08:55 PT - Liam O'Rourke (TAM): Spoke with customer. They need up to ~1,500 RPS chat for 20–40 seconds and ~3,000 RPS embeddings for 60 seconds during market-open. They can accept some smoothing but want fewer hard 429s. They requested a temporary exception for 2 weeks while they deploy client-side backoff improvements.

2026-01-21 09:24 PT - Cole Summers (Quotas): Noted some quota_exceeded events are due to embeddings daily quota window nearing cap; separate from overload. For overload/admission, we can grant a time-boxed burst multiplier increase for the specific route-group, but must monitor shed rates and enterprise latency burn.

2026-01-21 10:03 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Approved a guarded exception: increase burst multiplier for PROXIMA-ENT-014 on chat+embeddings in us-east only, 14-day expiry, automatic rollback if enterprise p99 latency burn > threshold or shed rate sustained > 2%. Added monitoring annotation + incident-bot notice rule. Customer must implement exponential backoff + jitter within 7 days.

2026-01-21 10:37 PT - Benji Okafor (Platform): Applied policy override (time-boxed) and verified via internal debug endpoint (PR-27477) that admission decisions now show higher burst budget for Proxima. Recorded change in audit log with expiry date and rollback plan.

2026-01-22 16:12 PT - Owen Phillips (Support): Follow-up: customer reports 429 rate down to ~0.8% during a smaller spike; they’re deploying client retry updates tonight. Reminded them that 429s can still occur under extreme regional pressure and that Retry-After must be honored.

2026-01-23 09:05 PT - Rafael Mendes (SRE): Post-change review: no negative impact on enterprise SLO dashboards; Proxima-specific 429s reduced; reason mix shifted mostly to quota_exceeded at end-of-day usage peaks (expected). Marking ticket resolved with note: longer-term solution may be Dedicated capacity or revisiting contracted burst terms.
source.resolution: Resolved with (1) customer education on 429 reason codes and correct retry/backoff behavior, and (2) a time-boxed, region-scoped burst exception for PROXIMA-ENT-014 on chat+embeddings (14-day expiry) with automated rollback guardrails. Customer to deploy improved client-side exponential backoff + jitter and concurrency caps. Separate guidance provided for quota_exceeded events (smooth usage / avoid end-of-window spikes).

- Is Proxima being routed to a stricter per-route budget due to route-group defaults?

## Requested outcome
- Explain semantics and provide an actionable plan.
- If eligible, grant a temporary burst exception with guardrails (time-boxed, monitored), or propose a dedicated capacity option.
text:
## Issue summary
Customer (Proxima Bank) reports a sharp increase in HTTP 429 responses starting ~2026-01-20 09:10 PT on /v1/chat/completions and /v1/embeddings. They believe this correlates with Redwood’s recent “priority routing / capacity protection” changes.

## Impact
- Customer batch + online traffic intermittently fails with 429s, causing retry storms on their side.
- Reported elevated end-user latency due to retries; some requests fail after their max-retry budget.
- Customer concern: enterprise tier expected fewer 429s, not more.

## Environment
- Hosted API, prod
- Region: us-east
- Routes: /v1/chat/completions, /v1/embeddings
- Auth: enterprise API key (account: PROXIMA-ENT-014)

## What customer is seeing
- 429 response body includes Redwood reason codes (examples pasted by customer):
  - "reason": "overload_protection_admission"
  - "reason": "quota_exceeded" (less frequent)
- Response headers observed:
  - Retry-After: 1 (sometimes 2)
  - X-Redwood-Request-Id present
  - X-Redwood-Throttle-Reason: overload_protection_admission (most common)

## Customer notes
- They run bursty workloads: 10–30s spikes during market-open events.
- Client currently retries immediately with fixed 100ms delay and no jitter.

## Steps to reproduce (customer)
1) Send ~800–1200 RPS bursts to /v1/chat/completions with streaming disabled.
2) Concurrently send embedding bursts (~2k RPS) during the same window.
3) Observe 429 rates rising from <0.2% to 6–12% during bursts.

## Expected behavior
- Enterprise priority should protect them during fleet pressure; customer expects either stable acceptance or clearly defined, predictable throttling limits.

## Actual behavior
- Customer sees throttling during burst windows and requests clarification on:
  1) Why enterprise traffic is being rejected
  2) Whether the 429s are overload shedding vs quota
  3) Whether Redwood can grant a temporary burst exception / larger budget

## Initial support response (guidance provided)
- Confirmed that the new system returns distinct reason codes and customers should key their retry/backoff behavior on:
  - "quota_exceeded" (respect quota windows; reduce steady-state rate)
  - "overload_protection_admission" (transient overload; respect Retry-After; use exponential backoff + jitter)
- Recommended changes:
  - Use exponential backoff with full jitter; cap concurrency; do not retry immediately.
  - Honor Retry-After; treat 429 as a signal to shed load client-side.

## Investigation notes (internal)
Hypothesis: us-east protected-route capacity protection enabled for Proxima allowlisted accounts; per-route/per-tier admission control is active and may be gating bursts more aggressively than intended for this customer’s contractual traffic shape.

Checks performed:
- Correlated spike with protection annotations in hot-route dashboard (SRE): overload protection activated for route-group "chat" + "embeddings" during a concurrent regional pressure event.
- Confirmed most 429s for this account carry reason=overload_protection_admission (not quota).
- Verified no corresponding 5xx spike; p99 latency for enterprise class remained within SLO during event, indicating protections are working for fleet health but customer’s burst profile is being clipped.

Open questions
- What is Proxima’s contracted peak/burst behavior (RPS and token/s) for these routes?
- Is Proxima being routed to a stricter per-route budget due to route-group defaults?

## Requested outcome
- Explain semantics and provide an actionable plan.
- If eligible, grant a temporary burst exception with guardrails (time-boxed, monitored), or propose a dedicated capacity option.

source.summary: Tokens/sec drop in eu-west Dedicated pool (isolated to region; perf-canary signal correlates with fleet image rollout)
source.content:
Issue summary
- Customer (Tensorlytics) reports a sudden ~20–30% drop in throughput (tokens/sec) for their Dedicated pool in eu-west.
- Impact appears isolated to eu-west; us-east Dedicated pool for the same workload unchanged.
- Redwood perf-canary also flagged a tokens/sec regression in eu-west for at least one overlapping model.

Customer impact
- Customer-facing symptoms: longer time-to-first-token (mild) and materially slower generation throughput (primary complaint).
- Customer observed: same prompt + parameters, same concurrency, but higher wall-clock completion times starting ~2026-01-18 08:10 UTC.
- Business impact: degraded SLOs for their production workload; they asked whether a rollback is possible.

Environment
- Deployment mode: Dedicated
- Region: eu-west
- Hardware: H100 pool (80GB) (per fleet inventory)
- Models: llama-3.1-70b-instruct (primary), mixtral-8x7b-instruct (secondary)
- Runtime: redwood-runtime v0.41.x (exact build tag confirmed below)

Initial customer report (from Support intake)
- Timestamp of onset: ~2026-01-18 08:10 UTC
- Comparison: eu-west Dedicated slower vs eu-west historical baseline; us-east Dedicated stable
- Workload: chat completions, streaming enabled, max_tokens 512–1024, typical prompt lengths 1.5k–4k tokens, concurrency ~12–20

What we looked at (triage checklist)
1) Confirm scope (region-only vs global)
2) Check perf-canary signals for eu-west + same model/hardware
3) Separate queueing/scheduler vs runtime execution (prefill/decode) vs infrastructure
4) Correlate against recent changes: runtime release, kernel change, scheduler config, fleet/image/driver changes, power caps

Timeline / investigation notes

2026-01-18 08:32 UTC - Comment (Rafael Mendes, Support)
- Acknowledged customer report and opened Sev2 incident track.
- Asked customer for a representative request_id(s) and their own before/after timestamps.
- Routed to #eng-sre and #eng-runtime with note: “eu-west only, tokens/sec down; possible fleet change.”

2026-01-18 09:05 UTC - Comment (Aisha Bello, SRE)
- Confirmed perf-canary warning in eu-west on llama-3.1-70b-instruct: tokens/sec down ~24% vs baseline, p95 latency slightly elevated but not the primary regression.
- Canary breakdown: runtime decode time increased; queueing time not materially changed. Batch size distribution roughly stable.
- Noted canary started flagging within ~20–30 mins of customer-reported onset.

2026-01-18 09:22 UTC - Comment (Noah Patel, Serving Runtime TL)
- Quick check: no runtime release rolled out to eu-west in the last 24h beyond standard stagger; runtime build tag observed in telemetry matches us-east.
- Kernel selection tags (from runtime telemetry) unchanged for the model (same kernel id family) — makes a pure kernel regression less likely.
- Requested Infra/Fleet to check eu-west node image / driver / power management changes.

2026-01-18 09:41 UTC - Comment (Vanessa Ortiz, Fleet)
- Found eu-west Dedicated H100 pool had an accelerated node image rollout starting 2026-01-18 07:55 UTC (image “gpu-node-euw-2026.01.18a”).
- Rollout reason: security patch + minor NVIDIA driver update.
- Rollout reached ~60% of eu-west dedicated nodes by 08:25 UTC.
- Hypothesis: new driver defaulted to a lower power limit / application clocks not being set by our init script.

2026-01-18 10:10 UTC - Comment (Logan Wright, Telemetry)
- Confirmed canary vs customer metrics align when filtering on eu-west + H100 SKU + node_image label.
- Observed tokens/sec on nodes with new image is ~0.74x compared to old image for same model/version.
- No corresponding increase in queueing time, suggesting per-GPU execution throughput regression.

2026-01-18 10:26 UTC - Comment (Vanessa Ortiz, Fleet)
- Verified via node bootstrap logs: nvidia-smi shows power cap not being applied on new image; running at a lower default limit.
- Noted a packaging change: init script path changed; our “set-clocks” unit did not run.
- Proposed mitigation: halt rollout and roll back eu-west dedicated pool to previous image; then hotfix image.

2026-01-18 10:38 UTC - Comment (Sean Gallagher, SRE)
- Approved rollback given customer impact + canary corroboration.
- Asked for safe-change plan: staggered rollback to avoid capacity shock.

2026-01-18 11:05 UTC - Comment (Vanessa Ortiz, Fleet)
- Action taken: paused rollout at 63% and began rollback to “gpu-node-euw-2026.01.12c” with max surge constraints.
- Expected time to recover: 60–90 mins.

2026-01-18 12:14 UTC - Comment (Rafael Mendes, Support)
- Updated customer: identified region-local infra change; rollback in progress; ETA ~1–2 hours for full recovery.
- Provided workaround for immediate relief: lower concurrency by ~15% to reduce tail effects while throughput stabilizes.

2026-01-18 13:02 UTC - Comment (Aisha Bello, SRE)
- Metrics: tokens/sec recovered gradually as rollback progressed.
- Perf-canary warning cleared after rollback reached ~85% (alert auto-resolved based on anti-flap window).
- p95 latency returned close to baseline; tokens/sec within 3–5% of baseline.

2026-01-18 14:20 UTC - Comment (Customer via Support summary)
- Customer confirms throughput is “back to normal” for their eu-west Dedicated deployment.
- Requested post-incident write-up and assurance about change controls.

Root cause
- Eu-west Dedicated H100 pool node image rollout (gpu-node-euw-2026.01.18a) changed the location/behavior of GPU clock/power-limit bootstrap. The unit responsible for applying application clocks/power cap did not execute.
- As a result, GPUs ran at lower effective power limit/clock settings, reducing decode throughput and overall tokens/sec.
- The issue was isolated to eu-west because the accelerated image rollout was region-scoped; other regions did not receive the image at the time.

Detection
- Customer ticket triggered initial response.
- Perf-canary independently detected tokens/sec regression for the same model/region and helped isolate the issue via node_image/hardware dimension correlation.

Resolution
- Paused eu-west rollout and rolled back the Dedicated GPU fleet to the previous node image.
- Confirmed recovery via:
  - tokens/sec back within baseline tolerance
  - perf-canary warning resolved
  - no sustained increase in queueing time or error rates

Follow-ups / action items
- Fleet: publish hotfix node image restoring clock/power-limit bootstrap; add explicit boot-time validation (fail health check if clocks not applied).
- Runtime/Telemetry: ensure perf-canary alert payload includes node_image/build tags in the “top contributors” section for faster infra attribution.
- SRE: add a pre-rollout canary gate for eu-west fleet image changes using perf-canary tokens/sec signal on top dedicated models.
- Support/CS: provide customer-facing summary and confirm change-management improvements.

Customer comms
- Customer received interim updates during rollback and a final confirmation after recovery.
- Support to send brief RCA summary + preventive steps (no customer data involved; synthetic canary detected regression).

Internal links referenced during incident (for context)
- Slack: #incidents thread for INC-2026-0118-EUW-THROUGHPUT (internal)
- Runbook used: runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert
- Dashboards: perf-canary per-model tokens/sec (eu-west) and fleet rollout tracking (eu-west H100 dedicated)

Resolution date
- Service restored: 2026-01-18
- Ticket marked Resolved after customer confirmation and follow-up plan logged: 2026-01-22

source.summary: Hosted API: p95/p99 latency regression on /v1/chat/completions during burst (Proxima Bank) — correlated with overload protection activation
source.description:
Issue summary (customer-reported)
- Proxima Bank reported a sudden latency increase for Hosted API /v1/chat/completions in us-east during a traffic burst on 2025-12-12.
- They observed slow but mostly successful responses (HTTP 200) and a subset of client-side timeouts.
- They did not report elevated 5xx; primary symptom was tail latency.

Customer impact
- Window: 2025-12-12 18:06–18:34 UTC (peak)
- Reported by customer: p95 rose from ~1.4s to ~7–9s; p99 up to ~25–40s; intermittent client timeouts at 30s.
- Business impact: degraded user experience in an internal “risk analyst copilot” workflow; they paused a batch job that was driving concurrency.

Environment
- Hosted API, prod
- Region: us-east
- Route: POST /v1/chat/completions
- Model: rw-chat-3.1-70b (customer-pinned)

Expected behavior
- Enterprise route tier should maintain defined latency/availability SLOs during fleet pressure.
- If shedding is required, it should primarily affect best-effort traffic (predictable 429/Retry-After) and not manifest as prolonged queuing for enterprise requests.

What we found (support + SRE investigation)
- Overload protection/admission control activated for the chat route in us-east during the burst.
- Best-effort traffic was shed as expected (elevated 429 with overload reason codes) which protected overall fleet health.
- However, enterprise traffic still experienced elevated queueing delay before execution due to a combination of:
  1) request shape shift toward long prompts / higher max_tokens, increasing per-request GPU occupancy,
  2) scheduler lag increasing during the burst (runtime queue depth + scheduling delay grew), and
  3) enterprise admission thresholds being tuned too leniently for this workload shape, allowing queue buildup rather than earlier backpressure.

Correlations (dashboards)
- Hot-route protection dashboard: shed rate for best-effort increased sharply at ~18:08 UTC, while enterprise admission remained mostly open.
- Runtime metrics: queue depth and scheduler lag increased in the same window and tracked closely with Proxima’s p99 latency.
- 5xx remained stable; dominant issue was time spent queued / waiting for compute.

Notes
- This ticket is specifically about latency regression (not 429 spikes). Proxima’s key is enterprise; they were not the primary target of shedding, but the queue buildup still impacted tail latency.
- Customer provided a request timestamp sample; we matched it against gateway logs and routing traces to confirm queueing delay dominated.

Customer-provided data (sanitized)
- Example timestamps (UTC): 18:11, 18:17, 18:21 (multiple requests)
- Client timeout: 30s
- Concurrency during batch: ~120–160 in-flight requests

Immediate workaround (shared with customer)
- Reduce concurrency to 40–60 in-flight for the batch job until we complete tuning.
- Set client timeouts to 60s for the bursty batch path, and implement exponential backoff + jitter for retries on timeouts.
- Keep prompt + max_tokens within documented recommended ranges for the pinned model; avoid max_tokens > 2k during peak hours unless pre-approved.

Resolution
- SRE + Routing adjusted admission control thresholds for enterprise traffic on /v1/chat/completions in us-east to cap queue buildup (earlier backpressure rather than prolonged queuing).
- Added an enterprise route-specific guardrail: when scheduler lag exceeds threshold for N seconds, new enterprise requests are throttled earlier with a clear overload 429 (rather than waiting in queue) and a short Retry-After.
- Monitored post-change: Proxima’s p95 returned to baseline within ~30 minutes on 2025-12-16 validation run; no further >20s p99 observed in the subsequent burst test.

Next steps / follow-ups
- Validate tuning across different workload shapes (short chat vs long context) and confirm we don’t increase false-positive throttling.
- Ensure support macros clearly differentiate: slow-200 queueing vs explicit overload 429 behavior.

References
- Slack incident coordination: #incidents thread (see sources/slack/incidents/1762395502.json)
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-overload-protection-and-admission-control.json
- Dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/dashboard-spec-hot-route-protection.json
comments: 2025-12-12 18:42 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support): Ticket opened from Proxima TAM escalation. Customer seeing p99 ~30–40s on chat completions in us-east. No 5xx spike reported; main concern is tail latency + client timeouts. Requesting confirmation whether hot-route protection changes were active.

2025-12-12 19:05 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Confirmed overload protection activated for /v1/chat/completions us-east starting ~18:08 UTC. Best-effort traffic shed as designed; investigating why enterprise keys saw increased queueing delay. Linking dashboards + will pull scheduler lag timeseries.

2025-12-12 19:22 UTC — Logan Wright (Telemetry): Queue depth and scheduler lag increased 4–6x in the same window; latency tracks scheduler lag, not gateway RTT. Suggests execution wait dominates. No obvious metric-cardinality issues; signals look trustworthy.

2025-12-12 19:37 UTC — Elena Popov (Routing): Looking at routing traces: enterprise admission stayed open while runtime lag rose; policy did not tighten until after queue already built. We should tighten enterprise admission earlier for this route/model when lag crosses threshold (avoid slow-200).

2025-12-12 20:10 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support) to customer: We see a burst affecting the us-east chat route; fleet health protections activated. Your requests were largely admitted (hence 200s), but queued longer due to compute pressure. Temporary recommendation: reduce batch concurrency and increase client timeout; we’re tuning admission controls to prevent queue buildup.

2025-12-13 09:18 UTC — Sofia Ivanova (Runtime Scheduling): Burst included a higher proportion of long-context prompts; per-request GPU occupancy increased, so queue drains slower. Agree with earlier admission/backpressure. Also suggest documenting recommended max_tokens for that pinned model under peak.

2025-12-14 16:03 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Proposed change: enterprise admission should begin returning explicit overload 429 when scheduler lag > threshold for sustained window, with Retry-After, rather than letting queue grow unbounded. Routing + gateway owners aligned; change behind flag for us-east only.

2025-12-15 11:41 UTC — Amara Diallo (API Gateway): Gateway can return consistent overload 429 payload/headers when routing denies due to lag-based admission control. We’ll ensure response includes overload reason + Retry-After so customer behavior is deterministic.

2025-12-16 17:28 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Deployed tuned thresholds in us-east (flagged). Ran controlled burst replay; p95 recovered to ~1.6–2.0s and p99 stayed <10s for enterprise keys; best-effort shedding increased earlier (expected). Monitoring for regressions.

2025-12-16 18:02 UTC — Owen Phillips (Support): Customer confirmed improvement during their validation batch run; no 30s timeouts observed. Closing as Resolved. Follow-up: add a support macro + FAQ snippet on “slow 200 vs explicit 429 overload” for enterprise routes.
source.customer_visible_update: We identified that the latency spike during the 2025-12-12 burst was driven primarily by runtime queueing (scheduler lag) on the us-east chat route. While best-effort traffic was shed, enterprise requests were still being admitted and queued longer than desired. We’ve tuned the admission control thresholds for this route/region to prevent queue buildup and, when necessary, respond earlier with a clear overload signal (429 + Retry-After) instead of prolonged waiting. Please continue to keep batch concurrency within the recommended range; we can review limits with you if your workload requires higher steady-state throughput.
source.resolution: Tuned enterprise admission control for /v1/chat/completions in us-east to cap queue buildup under scheduler lag; added earlier backpressure behavior and standardized overload response path. Customer validated improved tail latency; no further timeouts observed in follow-up run.
source.internal_notes: Key learning: protecting fleet health via best-effort shedding is necessary but not sufficient; enterprise experience can still degrade via queueing if admission remains open during lag. Prefer early deterministic overload 429 (with Retry-After) over slow-200 under sustained lag. Ensure this is reflected in runbook and support macros.

title: Hosted API: p95/p99 latency regression on /v1/chat/completions during burst (Proxima Bank) — correlated with overload protection activation
preview:
Issue summary (customer-reported)
- Proxima Bank reported a sudden latency increase for Hosted API /v1/chat/completions in us-east during a traffic burst on 2025-12-12.
- They observed slow but mostly successful responses (HTTP 200) and a subset of client-side timeouts.
- They did not report elevated 5xx; primary symptom was tail latency.

Customer impact
- Window: 2025-12-12 18:06–18:34 UTC (peak)
- Reported by customer: p95 rose from ~1.4s to ~7–9s; p99 up to ~25–40s; intermittent client timeouts at 30s.
- Business impact: degraded user experience in an internal “risk analyst copilot” workflow; they paused a batch job that was driving concurrency.

Environment
- Hosted API, prod
- Region: us-east
- Route: POST /v1/chat/completions
- Model: rw-chat-3.1-70b (customer-pinned)

Expected behavior
- Enterprise route tier should maintain defined latency/availability SLOs during fleet pressure.
- If shedding is required, it should primarily affect best-effort traffic (predictable 429/Retry-After) and not manifest as prolonged queuing for enterprise requests.

What we found (support + SRE investigation)
- Overload protection/admission control activated for the chat route in us-east during the burst.
- Best-effort traffic was shed as expected (elevated 429 with overload reason codes) which protected overall fleet health.
- However, enterprise traffic still experienced elevated queueing delay before execution due to a combination of:
  1) request shape shift toward long prompts / higher max_tokens, increasing per-request GPU occupancy,
  2) scheduler lag increasing during the burst (runtime queue depth + scheduling delay grew), and
  3) enterprise admission thresholds being tuned too leniently for this workload shape, allowing queue buildup rather than earlier backpressure.

Correlations (dashboards)
- Hot-route protection dashboard: shed rate for best-effort increased sharply at ~18:08 UTC, while enterprise admission remained mostly open.
- Runtime metrics: queue depth and scheduler lag increased in the same window and tracked closely with Proxima’s p99 latency.
- 5xx remained stable; dominant issue was time spent queued / waiting for compute.

Notes
- This ticket is specifically about latency regression (not 429 spikes). Proxima’s key is enterprise; they were not the primary target of shedding, but the queue buildup still impacted tail latency.
- Customer provided a request timestamp sample; we matched it against gateway logs and routing traces to confirm queueing delay dominated.

Customer-provided data (sanitized)
- Example timestamps (UTC): 18:11, 18:17, 18:21 (multiple requests)
- Client timeout: 30s
- Concurrency during batch: ~120–160 in-flight requests

Immediate workaround (shared with customer)
- Reduce concurrency to 40–60 in-flight for the batch job until we complete tuning.
- Set client timeouts to 60s for the bursty batch path, and implement exponential backoff + jitter for retries on timeouts.
- Keep prompt + max_tokens within documented recommended ranges for the pinned model; avoid max_tokens > 2k during peak hours unless pre-approved.

Resolution
- SRE + Routing adjusted admission control thresholds for enterprise traffic on /v1/chat/completions in us-east to cap queue buildup (earlier backpressure rather than prolonged queuing).
- Added an enterprise route-specific guardrail: when scheduler lag exceeds threshold for N seconds, new enterprise requests are throttled earlier with a clear overload 429 (rather than waiting in queue) and a short Retry-After.
- Monitored post-change: Proxima’s p95 returned to baseline within ~30 minutes on 2025-12-16 validation run; no further >20s p99 observed in the subsequent burst test.

Next steps / follow-ups
- Validate tuning across different workload shapes (short chat vs long context) and confirm we don’t increase false-positive throttling.
- Ensure support macros clearly differentiate: slow-200 queueing vs explicit overload 429 behavior.

References
- Slack incident coordination: #incidents thread (see sources/slack/incidents/1762395502.json)
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-overload-protection-and-admission-control.json
- Dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/dashboard-spec-hot-route-protection.json

source.summary: Orbit Analytics: p95 latency spike after runtime config update in Dedicated H100 pool (suspected hardware tuning profile rollout)
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Customer reports a sudden increase in tail latency (p95/p99) beginning ~2025-12-08 09:40 PT on their Dedicated H100 pool (orbit-h100-prod, us-east).
- No customer-side deploy at that time. Customer correlates onset with Redwood runtime config update/rollout.

Customer impact:
- Primary impact: p95 end-to-end latency for chat completions increased from ~650–800ms to ~1.3–1.6s for their typical workloads.
- Secondary impact: increased request timeouts on their side (they enforce a 2.0s upstream timeout for non-streaming calls); customer reported ~0.6% timeout rate during spike.
- Business impact: degraded UX in their analytics assistant; customer requested immediate mitigation and RCA.

What changed:
- Suspected: H100 hardware tuning profile pack enabled (scheduler preset + kernel heuristics) in orbit-h100-prod pool.
- Change window aligns with staged rollout for runtime profile selection and H100 presets.

Environment:
- Dedicated: orbit-h100-prod
- Region: us-east
- GPUs: H100 (mixed SXM/PCIe per fleet notes; exact mix unknown at time of report)
- Model: llama-3.1-70b-instruct pinned
- Traffic: mixture of streaming and non-streaming; concurrency bursts at top of minute (customer cron-driven workloads)

Requested by customer:
- Immediate rollback/disable of new profile if confirmed.
- Evidence: graphs before/after + explanation of expected behavior/trade-offs.

Attachments/logs requested:
- Customer request IDs and timestamps around spike (pending at ticket creation).
source.investigation_notes:
Timeline (all times PT):
- 2025-12-08 10:02: Ticket created from #support escalation. Customer reports p95 doubled.
- 2025-12-08 10:10: SRE (Sean Gallagher) checks Dedicated pool deploy history; sees runtime config rollout completed ~09:37–09:45.
- 2025-12-08 10:18: Observability review: new dashboard panels segmenting latency by selected_profile indicate orbit-h100-prod switched from profile=default to profile=h100/balanced.
- 2025-12-08 10:25: Runtime debug endpoint confirms effective config drifted vs prior defaults. Notable deltas:
  - scheduler.prefill_decode_fairness_mode = "strict" (previously adaptive)
  - scheduler.queue_limit increased
  - scheduler.target_batch_tokens increased
  - backpressure thresholds shifted (more throughput-oriented)
- 2025-12-08 10:33: Hypothesis: new fairness/queue settings improved throughput at moderate concurrency but worsened tail latency under customer’s bursty arrival pattern and long-ish prompt tail.
- 2025-12-08 10:41: Quick mitigation plan agreed: disable H100 profile for this pool via per-pool override (set profile_selection_override=default; restart canary slice first).
- 2025-12-08 10:58: Canary slice (10%) shows p95 recovering within ~15 minutes (back to ~700–850ms), timeout rate returns to baseline.
- 2025-12-08 11:30: Rollback expanded to 100% of orbit-h100-prod.

Data checked:
- Dedicated pool latency dashboard (p50/p95/p99) for /v1/chat.completions.
- Scheduler metrics: queue depth, time-in-queue, batch formation wait.
- GPU metrics: SM utilization stable; no thermal throttling signals; memory headroom acceptable; no OOM spikes.
- Error metrics: 5xx unchanged; timeouts observed were customer-side.
- Profile selection metrics: selected_profile and override_source changed at rollout time.

Most likely cause:
- H100 balanced preset increased batching aggressiveness + stricter prefill/decode fairness. Under bursty traffic with mixed sequence lengths, queueing delay increased for a subset of requests, pushing p95/p99 up.

Notes:
- This matches earlier internal signals from H100 validation about tail-latency regressions tied to prefill/decode fairness (see runtime PR notes and internal Slack thread on suspected fairness regression).
- No evidence of kernel mismatch; tokens/sec improved slightly during high concurrency, but customer’s SLO is tail latency, not max throughput.
comments: 2025-12-08 10:04 PT — Kira Thompson (Support): Customer (Orbit Analytics) seeing p95 jump ~2x since ~09:40. They claim no deploy. Asking if we changed runtime config / scheduler knobs. Escalating to SRE/oncall.

2025-12-08 10:12 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Confirmed config rollout in orbit-h100-prod completed ~09:45 PT. Pulling dashboards now; suspect profile pack enablement. Will update with graphs + mitigation proposal.

2025-12-08 10:20 PT — Aisha Bello (SLO dashboards): Looking at profile-selection segmented panels. Clear step function at 09:43: selected_profile=h100-balanced. Latency step coincident. Sharing dashboard link in #incidents and attaching to ticket.

2025-12-08 10:29 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Debug endpoint shows fairness mode strict + higher queue limit / batch tokens. Plan: override to default profile for this pool first at 10% canary; watch p95 + time-in-queue + customer timeout rate.
2025-12-08 10:46 PT — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra): Ready to apply per-pool override and restart canary slice. Confirming no other pools impacted in us-east. Proceeding once SRE gives go-ahead.
2025-12-08 11:02 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Canary slice looks good: p95 back in expected band; queue time dropped. Proceeding to 100% rollback for orbit-h100-prod. Customer update going out next.

2025-12-08 11:18 PT — Kira Thompson (Support): Updated Orbit: we found correlation with newly enabled H100 tuned profile; rolling back for their pool now. Provided interim ETA (30–45 min) and asked for request IDs for any remaining spikes.
2025-12-08 12:07 PT — Kira Thompson (Support): Customer confirms p95 back to normal and timeout alarms cleared. They request RCA + guidance on future profile rollouts (opt-in/opt-out).

2025-12-09 09:14 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Follow-up: this aligns with known tail-latency sensitivity in prefill/decode fairness. Runtime team has fix in pr-22033 + perf canary gate tightening. Will coordinate whether we re-enable with a safer preset (latency-first) later.

2025-12-10 16:22 PT — Kira Thompson (Support): Sent final note to customer: we will keep profile disabled for their pool until H100 preset update is validated against bursty workload matrix; we’ll offer controlled re-enable window with canary + rollback. Marking ticket resolved.
source.customer_facing_update:
We identified that a runtime configuration change enabled a new H100 tuning profile in your Dedicated pool around the time the latency spike began. That profile increases batching aggressiveness and adjusts scheduler fairness behavior; in your workload shape (bursty concurrency + mixed prompt lengths), it caused higher queueing delay and worsened tail latency.

We rolled back the profile for your pool (returned to prior defaults), and latency returned to baseline within ~30 minutes. We’ll keep the profile disabled for your pool until an updated H100 preset is validated against this workload pattern. We can schedule a controlled re-enable window with canary monitoring and an immediate rollback switch.

We will follow up with a short RCA summary and the guardrails we’re adding to prevent this regression class from escaping canary again.
source.resolution:
Mitigation applied:
- Disabled H100 tuned profile for orbit-h100-prod via per-pool override (forced selected_profile=default).
- Performed staged restart (10% canary -> 100%).

Result:
- p95 latency returned to baseline band (~700–850ms) and customer timeouts cleared.

Root cause (working):
- Tail-latency regression introduced by H100 scheduler preset (prefill/decode fairness + batching/queue defaults) under bursty request arrival + mixed seq-len distribution.

Status:
- Customer stable on default profile.
- Pending: runtime preset adjustment + additional perf-canary coverage for bursty workloads before re-enabling.
follow_ups: Add explicit guidance in H100 tuned profile reference: workload envelope + known trade-offs (throughput vs tail latency) and recommended opt-out for strict p95 SLOs until latency-first preset is available.
Ensure perf-canary includes bursty arrival patterns and mixed seq-len buckets for H100 (tie to workload matrix runner).
Add automated guardrail: if queue_time_p95 exceeds threshold after profile enablement, auto-disable profile at pool level (requires product/infra sign-off).
Coordinate with Support to standardize customer comms template: 'expected behavior' vs guarantees.

source.summary: Dedicated: p95 latency spikes under mixed short/long prompts (head-of-line blocking suspected)
source.description:
Issue Summary
Northpeak AI reports intermittent but severe p95 latency spikes on their Dedicated pool when traffic mixes short chat prompts with occasional long-context prompts. Symptoms line up with head-of-line blocking during prefill and decode batch selection.

Customer Impact
- User-visible slow responses (p95) during business hours.
- Primary pain: chat endpoints where short prompts should remain snappy even when long-context requests are present.
- Observed impact window: 2025-11-24 onward; most noticeable 16:00–22:00 UTC.

Environment
- Deployment: Dedicated
- Region: us-east
- Pool: ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01
- Runtime version at report: runtime-1.17.9 (baseline scheduler)
- Models: redwood-llama3.1-70b-instruct (primary), redwood-qwen2.5-72b-instruct (secondary)
- Client pattern: mixed prompt-length distribution (short ~0.2–1.5k tokens, long ~10–32k tokens) with bursts of long-context traffic.

Customer-Provided Symptoms / Repro (as described)
1) Run 80/20 mix of short/long requests against the same model endpoint.
2) When long requests arrive in bursts (5–20 concurrent), p95 for short requests increases 3–8x.
3) Throughput remains roughly stable, but tail latency and TTFT for short requests degrade.

Initial Triage Notes (Support)
- Checked pool-level saturation: GPU utilization high but not pegged at 100% constantly; queue time spikes correlate with long prefill events.
- No corresponding increase in 5xx/429. This is primarily latency / scheduling behavior, not hard capacity exhaustion.

Investigation Notes (Eng/SRE)
Findings (2025-11-27 to 2025-12-02)
- Correlated spikes with mixed-workload periods: long-prefill jobs dominate selection in continuous batching loop and delay short requests.
- Observed pattern: time-in-queue for short requests increases disproportionately during long-prefill waves, while decode tokens/sec stays similar.
- Hypothesis: scheduler is not sufficiently prompt-length-aware; prefill work steals iteration budget, causing head-of-line blocking.

Data Links (internal)
- Grafana dashboard (Dedicated pool): https://grafana.redwood.example/d/ded-pool-latency?var_pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01
- Trace sample (anonymized): https://traces.redwood.example/trace?pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01&from=2025-11-28T18:00Z&to=2025-11-28T19:00Z
- Scheduler metrics (new canary panels): https://grafana.redwood.example/d/scheduler-class?var_pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01

Proposed Mitigation
Enable mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long classification + two-queue dispatch) behind feature flag for this dedicated pool at low ramp, with conservative aging parameters to prevent long-request starvation.

Canary / Benchmark Evidence
- Internal results summary for runtime-1.18 candidate indicates meaningful p95 improvement under 80/20 mixes without material throughput loss on comparable SKUs:
  - Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/mixed-workload-results-summary-runtime-1-18.json
- Perf-canary mixed-workload scenario also shows reduced tail amplification (nightly run): https://perf-canary.redwood.example/jobs/mixed-workload?runtime=1.18

Change Plan (Dedicated)
- Step 1: upgrade pool to runtime-1.18.2 (includes scheduler hooks + metrics)
- Step 2: enable feature flag scheduler.mixed_workload_policy=true at 10% of traffic
- Step 3: ramp to 50% after 2 hours if p95 short-class latency improves and no starvation counters trip
- Step 4: ramp to 100% with on-call coverage and rollback readiness

Rollback Plan
- Disable flag scheduler.mixed_workload_policy=false (instant)
- Revert policy knobs to defaults; if needed roll back runtime to 1.17.9 during maintenance window

Outcome / Current Status
- Mitigation applied to Northpeak AI pool; p95 spikes reduced and short prompt TTFT stabilized during long-context bursts.
- No increase in error rates observed.

Next Actions
- Keep scheduler prototype enabled for Northpeak AI pending 7-day observation.
- Feed this ticket into productization requirements for Dedicated QoS guardrails (out of scope for prototype, but will influence defaults and safety bounds).
comments: 2025-11-26 (Rafael Mendes, Support): Ticket opened after Northpeak AI reported p95 spikes (3–8x) when long-context prompts hit the same dedicated endpoint as short chat traffic. Requesting Eng Serving Runtime triage; suspect head-of-line blocking in scheduler.

2025-11-26 (Aisha Bello, SRE): Confirmed no 5xx spike. Latency increase appears queue-time driven. GPU utilization elevated during long-prefill windows; recommending scheduler-level mitigation vs adding capacity. Added internal dashboard link and trace window.

2025-11-27 (Marcus Reed, Eng Serving Runtime): Looked at traces: batch selection heavily biased toward long-prefill when long requests arrive concurrently. Short requests accumulate in queue and miss dispatch opportunities. This matches known mixed workload issue we’re addressing with the short/long two-queue prototype.

2025-11-28 (Julian Moore, Eng Serving Runtime): We can enable per-class scheduler metrics once runtime-1.18 is on the pool. That will let us validate queue depth/time-in-queue split and starvation counters. Coordinating with Observability for dashboard panels.

2025-11-29 (Sean Gallagher, SRE sponsor): OK to proceed with a limited dedicated canary as long as we (1) ramp gradually, (2) watch starvation counters and p99, and (3) keep rollback path clear. Please follow the mixed scheduler rollout runbook.

2025-11-30 (Marcus Reed, Eng Serving Runtime): Upgraded ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01 to runtime-1.18.2 during agreed maintenance window. Enabled scheduler.mixed_workload_policy at 10%. Initial 30-min readout: short-request p95 down ~35% vs prior day at similar traffic; throughput within noise.

2025-11-30 (Rafael Mendes, Support): Customer asked whether long requests will be throttled/starved. Responded: policy is bias toward short prompts but includes aging to guarantee progress for long prompts; no request dropping; only scheduling order changes.

2025-12-01 (Marcus Reed, Eng Serving Runtime): Ramp to 50% completed. Noticed one brief long-queue wait spike; starvation counter stayed at 0. Adjusted aging half-life slightly more aggressive to ensure long queue drains under sustained short load (aligns with follow-up fix in pr-24905).

2025-12-02 (Aisha Bello, SRE): Ramp to 100% completed. No p95 regression alerts; p95 short-class stable through a long-context burst window (20+ concurrent long). No additional operational risk observed. Added incident reference INC-2025-11-30-us-east-runtime-tail-latency-canary for internal tracking.

2025-12-04 (Rafael Mendes, Support): Customer reports noticeable improvement; subjective “snappiness” back to expected. Requested 1 week monitoring before we consider permanent default change. Marking as Resolved with mitigation in place.
source.resolution: Resolved by enabling the mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long classification + two-queue dispatch with aging) on Northpeak AI’s Dedicated pool after upgrading to runtime-1.18.2. Tail latency for short prompts improved substantially during long-context bursts with no observed error-rate increase. Feature flag left enabled pending 7-day observation; rollback remains available via scheduler.mixed_workload_policy=false.

title: Dedicated: p95 latency spikes under mixed short/long prompts (head-of-line blocking suspected)
preview:
Issue Summary
Northpeak AI reports intermittent but severe p95 latency spikes on their Dedicated pool when traffic mixes short chat prompts with occasional long-context prompts. Symptoms line up with head-of-line blocking during prefill and decode batch selection.

Customer Impact
- User-visible slow responses (p95) during business hours.
- Primary pain: chat endpoints where short prompts should remain snappy even when long-context requests are present.
- Observed impact window: 2025-11-24 onward; most noticeable 16:00–22:00 UTC.

Environment
- Deployment: Dedicated
- Region: us-east
- Pool: ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01
- Runtime version at report: runtime-1.17.9 (baseline scheduler)
- Models: redwood-llama3.1-70b-instruct (primary), redwood-qwen2.5-72b-instruct (secondary)
- Client pattern: mixed prompt-length distribution (short ~0.2–1.5k tokens, long ~10–32k tokens) with bursts of long-context traffic.

Customer-Provided Symptoms / Repro (as described)
1) Run 80/20 mix of short/long requests against the same model endpoint.
2) When long requests arrive in bursts (5–20 concurrent), p95 for short requests increases 3–8x.
3) Throughput remains roughly stable, but tail latency and TTFT for short requests degrade.

Initial Triage Notes (Support)
- Checked pool-level saturation: GPU utilization high but not pegged at 100% constantly; queue time spikes correlate with long prefill events.
- No corresponding increase in 5xx/429. This is primarily latency / scheduling behavior, not hard capacity exhaustion.

Investigation Notes (Eng/SRE)
Findings (2025-11-27 to 2025-12-02)
- Correlated spikes with mixed-workload periods: long-prefill jobs dominate selection in continuous batching loop and delay short requests.
- Observed pattern: time-in-queue for short requests increases disproportionately during long-prefill waves, while decode tokens/sec stays similar.
- Hypothesis: scheduler is not sufficiently prompt-length-aware; prefill work steals iteration budget, causing head-of-line blocking.

Data Links (internal)
- Grafana dashboard (Dedicated pool): https://grafana.redwood.example/d/ded-pool-latency?var_pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01
- Trace sample (anonymized): https://traces.redwood.example/trace?pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01&from=2025-11-28T18:00Z&to=2025-11-28T19:00Z
- Scheduler metrics (new canary panels): https://grafana.redwood.example/d/scheduler-class?var_pool=ded-northpeak-ai-us-east-01

Proposed Mitigation
Enable mixed-workload scheduler prototype (short/long classification + two-queue dispatch) behind feature flag for this dedicated pool at low ramp, with conservative aging parameters to prevent long-request starvation.

Canary / Benchmark Evidence
- Internal results summary for runtime-1.18 candidate indicates meaningful p95 improvement under 80/20 mixes without material throughput loss on comparable SKUs:
  - Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/mixed-workload-results-summary-runtime-1-18.json
- Perf-canary mixed-workload scenario also shows reduced tail amplification (nightly run): https://perf-canary.redwood.example/jobs/mixed-workload?runtime=1.18

Change Plan (Dedicated)
- Step 1: upgrade pool to runtime-1.18.2 (includes scheduler hooks + metrics)
- Step 2: enable feature flag scheduler.mixed_workload_policy=true at 10% of traffic
- Step 3: ramp to 50% after 2 hours if p95 short-class latency improves and no starvation counters trip
- Step 4: ramp to 100% with on-call coverage and rollback readiness

Rollback Plan
- Disable flag scheduler.mixed_workload_policy=false (instant)
- Revert policy knobs to defaults; if needed roll back runtime to 1.17.9 during maintenance window

Outcome / Current Status
- Mitigation applied to Northpeak AI pool; p95 spikes reduced and short prompt TTFT stabilized during long-context bursts.
- No increase in error rates observed.

Next Actions
- Keep scheduler prototype enabled for Northpeak AI pending 7-day observation.
- Feed this ticket into productization requirements for Dedicated QoS guardrails (out of scope for prototype, but will influence defaults and safety bounds).

source.summary: Hosted Chat: TTFT + p95 latency regression when long-context traffic increases (mixed prompt lengths)
source.description:
Issue summary
Customer reports a regression in Hosted Chat streaming latency (TTFT and p95) when their traffic mix shifts toward long-context prompts. Symptoms match head-of-line blocking in mixed prompt-length workloads (long prefill dominating batch selection), causing short chat requests to wait behind long-prefill work.

Impact
- User-visible TTFT regression for short prompts during periods of long-context load.
- Increased p95 for end-to-end latency on streaming chat.
- Customer has a latency SLO for interactive chat; current behavior is impacting production rollouts.

Customer context
- Company: Northpeak AI (enterprise)
- Workload: chat assistant with periodic long-context retrieval (8k–32k context) mixed with short conversational turns.
- Client uses streaming responses and measures TTFT as time until first token chunk.

Environment
- Hosted API, prod, region: us-east
- Models reported: redwood/llama-3-70b-instruct (primary), redwood/qwen2.5-72b-instruct (fallback)
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/completions (stream=true)

Observed behavior (customer-reported)
- Baseline (mostly short prompts): TTFT p95 ~0.7–0.9s
- During long-context bursts: TTFT p95 rises to ~2.5–3.8s; end-to-end p95 rises disproportionately
- Customer notes this correlates strongly with an increase in long-context requests; overall QPS stays roughly constant.

Internal observations (initial)
- Our us-east pool shows increased prefill time-in-queue coincident with long-context traffic spikes.
- Tail amplification appears driven by queueing/scheduler effects rather than a single-model regression.

Reproduction steps (customer)
1) Start N=40 concurrent streaming chat sessions (short prompts, 50–150 input tokens).
2) Add a background load of N=8 concurrent long-context sessions (8k–16k input tokens; same model).
3) Keep max_output_tokens constant (e.g., 256–512) across both classes.
4) Observe TTFT distribution for short prompts.

Reproduction steps (Redwood suggested harness)
- Use benchmark-lab “mixed” scenario with 80/20 short/long mix and long spike (see benchmark-lab PR-903 scenarios).
- Compare baseline scheduler vs short/long-aware two-queue scheduler prototype.

Grafana / dashboards (internal)
- Hosted API latency overview (us-east): https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/hosted-api-latency/overview?var-region=us-east
- Streaming TTFT percentiles: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/hosted-api-streaming/ttft?var-region=us-east
- Runtime queueing + batch metrics: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/runtime-batching/queue-and-batch?var-region=us-east
- (If enabled) Scheduler class metrics dashboard: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/scheduler-class/metrics?var-region=us-east

Initial hypotheses
- Mixed prompt-length workload causing head-of-line blocking: long prefill batches dominate GPU iteration selection, delaying short prompt prefill/first token.
- Continuous batching heuristics overweighting backlog tokens, effectively biasing toward long requests under bursty long-context traffic.
- Potential kernel selection instability when mixing sequence lengths (less likely primary driver for TTFT, but noted in earlier prototype work).

Requested customer artifacts
- Request IDs for 10–20 slow TTFT examples during a long-context burst
- Their long-context prompt length distribution (approx buckets are fine)
- Confirmation of any client-side retry/timeouts (to rule out reconnect effects)

Recommendation / next steps (internal)
- Validate with mixed-workload scheduler prototype behind feature flag in staging, then limited us-east canary per SRE guardrails.
- Ensure per-class scheduler metrics are enabled for the canary pool (queue depth/time-in-queue by class, starvation counters).
- If confirmed, provide customer a mitigation path:
  - Short-term: route long-context traffic to a separate pool/model variant if available (Dedicated-like isolation behavior in Hosted is limited, but we can apply internal routing policy for their org as a stopgap).
  - Medium-term: enable the short/long scheduling policy once canary passes and metrics are stable.

Owner notes
- Tracking under mixed-workload scheduler initiative; aligns with runtime prototype work currently staged for canary validation.
comments: 2025-12-02 (Rafael Mendes - Support): Created from customer escalation. Customer reports TTFT p95 jumping from <1s to ~3s when they run long-context evals in parallel. They provided a chart screenshot; requesting request IDs + timestamps. Looping in runtime + SRE.

2025-12-02 (Aisha Bello - SRE): Confirmed us-east had a TTFT p95 increase during the same window. No broad 5xx increase. Suspect queueing/scheduler effects. Please provide 10–20 request IDs and we can correlate to runtime traces.

2025-12-03 (Julian Moore - Serving Runtime): Looking at traces: prefill queue time spikes during long-context traffic. This matches the mixed-workload head-of-line blocking we’ve been prototyping a fix for (short/long class-based scheduling). We should test the two-queue policy in staging and compare TTFT p95 for short class.

2025-12-03 (Customer - Northpeak AI via Support): We can reproduce reliably with 40 short sessions + 8 long sessions. Long prompts are typically 10k–14k input tokens; shorts are ~80 tokens. We can send request IDs for 15 slow TTFT samples (attached in email thread).

2025-12-04 (Sean Gallagher - SRE sponsor): If we enable the scheduler prototype in a canary pool, must follow hosted-api tail latency guardrails. Please ensure rollback trigger is set (TTFT p95 +30% sustained 10m, or error budget burn increase). Also confirm starvation prevention is active for long class.

2025-12-05 (Julian Moore - Serving Runtime): Action items: (1) run mixed 80/20 and long-spike scenarios in perf-canary for runtime-1.18 candidate; (2) confirm new per-class metrics are present; (3) propose initial knobs (classification threshold, token budgets, aging) for us-east canary. Will update ticket with benchmark deltas before requesting canary approval.
source.resolution: Pending. Next checkpoint: staged benchmark results + SRE sign-off to run limited us-east canary with short/long scheduler prototype enabled for Northpeak AI traffic (or a representative pool).
