source.summary: Intermittent 5xx spikes correlated with KMS throttling (private deployment, us-east)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Customer reported intermittent 5xx responses from Redwood Private inference endpoints. Spikes correlate with KMS throttling errors during envelope decrypt.

Impact
- Symptoms: elevated 5xx at API gateway, increased p95 latency, sporadic request failures (non-deterministic).
- Customer impact: end-user facing errors during peak traffic windows; retries from upstream increased load and made spikes worse.
- Duration: recurring spikes over ~3 days; most acute on 2025-11-19 15:10–16:05 ET.

Environment
- Deployment: Redwood Private
- Region: us-east-1
- Cloud/KMS: AWS KMS (customer-managed key)
- Workloads: chat + embeddings, moderate concurrency with periodic burst traffic

Detection / signals
- Alert: "KMS dependency error rate" (new dashboard pack) triggered at 15:14 ET.
- Observed metrics:
  - redwood.kms.requests_total increased sharply with retries
  - redwood.kms.errors_total{error_category="Throttling"} spiked
  - api.gateway.5xx_rate correlated 1:1 with KMS throttling bursts

Dashboard links (internal)
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/kms-health/kms-dependency-health?var=env:prod&var=region:us-east-1
- Gateway errors: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/api-gw/api-gateway?var=env:prod&var=region:us-east-1
- Trace sample (Honeycomb): https://ui.honeycomb.io/redwood/datasets/inference/trace?trace_id=2f6c3c9b6a7c4d9a

Investigation notes
1) Confirmed error mapping
- Logs show AWS KMS returning "ThrottlingException" on Decrypt operations. The errors propagate as 5xx due to decrypt failure on request path.
- Log fields (new-ish): kms.error_category=Throttling, kms.operation=Decrypt, kms.key_arn=.../key/xxxx, kms.request_id=...

2) Retry amplification hypothesis
- During throttling, our client retries with exponential backoff, but with multiple callers and burst traffic, total KMS QPS increased (retry storm). KMS throttling then worsened.
- p95 decrypt latency rose; some requests exceeded upstream budgets, contributing to gateway timeouts and 5xx.

3) Quota / provisioning
- Customer KMS account-level quota appeared near default for Decrypt TPS for their region.
- Customer had increased inference burst without revisiting KMS quotas.

Root cause
- Primary: AWS KMS Decrypt throttling at customer quota limits during burst windows.
- Contributing: retry policy lacked sufficient jitter + global throttle budget/backpressure, leading to request amplification under throttling.

Mitigation (immediate)
- Recommended customer actions (same day):
  - Submit AWS quota increase request for KMS Decrypt in us-east-1.
  - Reduce burst concurrency temporarily (if possible) or add upstream jittered retries with cap.
- Redwood config guidance (customer-approved):
  - Reduce max retry attempts for KMS Decrypt.
  - Enforce a retry budget so per-request decrypt does not exceed the end-to-end latency budget.

Mitigation (product / platform)
- Implemented throttle-budget/backpressure for KMS wrapper to avoid saturating KMS under load and to prevent retry amplification.
- Observability: confirmed dashboards and alerts include Throttling by region/key ARN and show request volume vs errors.

Customer communication
- Provided interim explanation: KMS throttling (not key mismatch) + recommended quota increase path.
- Provided runbook excerpt and troubleshooting steps from the Private operator guide; included sample AWS CLI command to validate key access and confirm throttling errors.

Resolution
- 2025-11-22: Customer confirmed AWS quota increase approved (Decrypt TPS). Error spikes reduced significantly.
- 2025-11-25: Redwood hotfix + wrapper improvements rolled to their deployment (backoff/jitter + throttle budget defaults). No further 5xx spikes observed for 24h under similar traffic.

Verification
- Post-change metrics:
  - redwood.kms.errors_total{error_category="Throttling"} near-zero
  - api.gateway.5xx_rate returned to baseline
  - p95 end-to-end latency back within expected range

Follow-ups
- [Eng] Ensure KMS wrapper safe defaults are enforced and cannot be overridden without explicit override in prod (guardrails).
- [Docs] Add operator-guide callout: KMS TPS/quota sizing guidance for bursty workloads + how to recognize throttling patterns.
- [SRE] Tune alert thresholds to avoid noise while still catching real throttling onset; split by env/region.
comments:
2025-11-19 15:26 ET — Tomáš Novák (Reporter)
Customer reports "sporadic 500s" and elevated latency. Initial triage suggests decrypt path failures. Pulling logs + dashboards.

2025-11-19 15:41 ET — Rafael Mendes (Assignee)
Confirmed KMS ThrottlingException in dataplane logs. Correlates with gateway 5xx spikes. This looks like retry amplification: KMS request volume is higher than baseline during throttling windows.

2025-11-19 16:02 ET — Nadia Rahman
I’m seeing redwood.kms.errors_total{error_category="Throttling"} jump alongside redwood.kms.requests_total. Trace samples show multiple decrypt retries per request. Sharing a trace link in description.

2025-11-19 16:15 ET — Sean Gallagher
On-call view: this matches the pattern from the recent KMS timeout spike thread (#incidents). Suggest we advise quota increase + reduce retry attempts short-term; long-term fix is throttle budget/backpressure in wrapper.

2025-11-19 16:33 ET — Kira Thompson
Pulled relevant troubleshooting text from Private KMS Operator Guide draft. Adding a section proposal: "KMS throttling and quotas" with explicit AWS quota guidance + what metrics/log fields to look at.

2025-11-20 10:12 ET — Jordan Lee
Customer’s IAM looks fine (no AccessDenied). This is pure throttling. Recommended they request KMS Decrypt quota increase in us-east-1 and we should ensure our wrapper uses jitter on backoff to avoid sync retries.

2025-11-21 13:47 ET — Rafael Mendes
Opened eng follow-up and linked PR plan: add KMS throttle budget/backpressure + cap retries. Also recommend enabling the new KMS dashboards pack for this customer.

2025-11-22 09:18 ET — Support (Tomáš Novák)
Customer confirmed they submitted AWS quota increase request; temporary mitigation applied by reducing burst concurrency during peak windows.

2025-11-25 17:05 ET — Rafael Mendes
Rolled wrapper changes to customer deployment (throttle budget + improved jitter). Observed KMS throttling drop to near-zero. Monitoring overnight.

2025-11-26 11:22 ET — Tomáš Novák
24h verification complete: no recurrence of 5xx spikes, latency stable. Closing as Resolved with follow-up doc improvements.

title: Intermittent 5xx spikes correlated with KMS throttling (private deployment, us-east)
preview:
Issue summary
- Customer reported intermittent 5xx responses from Redwood Private inference endpoints. Spikes correlate with KMS throttling errors during envelope decrypt.

Impact
- Symptoms: elevated 5xx at API gateway, increased p95 latency, sporadic request failures (non-deterministic).
- Customer impact: end-user facing errors during peak traffic windows; retries from upstream increased load and made spikes worse.
- Duration: recurring spikes over ~3 days; most acute on 2025-11-19 15:10–16:05 ET.

Environment
- Deployment: Redwood Private
- Region: us-east-1
- Cloud/KMS: AWS KMS (customer-managed key)
- Workloads: chat + embeddings, moderate concurrency with periodic burst traffic

Detection / signals
- Alert: "KMS dependency error rate" (new dashboard pack) triggered at 15:14 ET.
- Observed metrics:
  - redwood.kms.requests_total increased sharply with retries
  - redwood.kms.errors_total{error_category="Throttling"} spiked
  - api.gateway.5xx_rate correlated 1:1 with KMS throttling bursts

Dashboard links (internal)
- Grafana: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/kms-health/kms-dependency-health?var=env:prod&var=region:us-east-1
- Gateway errors: https://grafana.redwoodinference.internal/d/api-gw/api-gateway?var=env:prod&var=region:us-east-1
- Trace sample (Honeycomb): https://ui.honeycomb.io/redwood/datasets/inference/trace?trace_id=2f6c3c9b6a7c4d9a

Investigation notes
1) Confirmed error mapping
- Logs show AWS KMS returning "ThrottlingException" on Decrypt operations. The errors propagate as 5xx due to decrypt failure on request path.
- Log fields (new-ish): kms.error_category=Throttling, kms.operation=Decrypt, kms.key_arn=.../key/xxxx, kms.request_id=...

2) Retry amplification hypothesis
- During throttling, our client retries with exponential backoff, but with multiple callers and burst traffic, total KMS QPS increased (retry storm). KMS throttling then worsened.
- p95 decrypt latency rose; some requests exceeded upstream budgets, contributing to gateway timeouts and 5xx.

3) Quota / provisioning
- Customer KMS account-level quota appeared near default for Decrypt TPS for their region.
- Customer had increased inference burst without revisiting KMS quotas.

Root cause
- Primary: AWS KMS Decrypt throttling at customer quota limits during burst windows.
- Contributing: retry policy lacked sufficient jitter + global throttle budget/backpressure, leading to request amplification under throttling.

Mitigation (immediate)
- Recommended customer actions (same day):
  - Submit AWS quota increase request for KMS Decrypt in us-east-1.
  - Reduce burst concurrency temporarily (if possible) or add upstream jittered retries with cap.
- Redwood config guidance (customer-approved):
  - Reduce max retry attempts for KMS Decrypt.
  - Enforce a retry budget so per-request decrypt does not exceed the end-to-end latency budget.

Mitigation (product / platform)
- Implemented throttle-budget/backpressure for KMS wrapper to avoid saturating KMS under load and to prevent retry amplification.
- Observability: confirmed dashboards and alerts include Throttling by region/key ARN and show request volume vs errors.

Customer communication
- Provided interim explanation: KMS throttling (not key mismatch) + recommended quota increase path.
- Provided runbook excerpt and troubleshooting steps from the Private operator guide; included sample AWS CLI command to validate key access and confirm throttling errors.

Resolution
- 2025-11-22: Customer confirmed AWS quota increase approved (Decrypt TPS). Error spikes reduced significantly.
- 2025-11-25: Redwood hotfix + wrapper improvements rolled to their deployment (backoff/jitter + throttle budget defaults). No further 5xx spikes observed for 24h under similar traffic.

Verification
- Post-change metrics:
  - redwood.kms.errors_total{error_category="Throttling"} near-zero
  - api.gateway.5xx_rate returned to baseline
  - p95 end-to-end latency back within expected range

Follow-ups
- [Eng] Ensure KMS wrapper safe defaults are enforced and cannot be overridden without explicit override in prod (guardrails).
- [Docs] Add operator-guide callout: KMS TPS/quota sizing guidance for bursty workloads + how to recognize throttling patterns.
- [SRE] Tune alert thresholds to avoid noise while still catching real throttling onset; split by env/region.

source.summary: Quarantine policy during Dedicated expansion quarantined too many nodes, causing temporary capacity shortfall (us-east A100 pool)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Customer (Northwind Analytics) reported a throughput drop after initiating a Dedicated pool expansion in us-east (A100). Expansion nodes were provisioned but a high fraction were automatically moved to Quarantined during post-provision validation, leading to a temporary capacity shortfall.

Customer impact
- Increased queueing and elevated tail latencies for their Dedicated pool.
- Observed intermittent request timeouts when concurrency exceeded the reduced active capacity.
- No data loss. Customer has fallback disabled and pins model alias; they could not route to hosted/shared capacity.

When
- First customer report: 2025-10-21 09:12 PT
- Peak impact window: ~09:00–11:30 PT

Environment / scope
- Prod, us-east
- Dedicated pool: nw-analytics-ded-us-east-a100-01 (internal pool id: dp_7f3d9f)
- GPU type: A100 80GB
- Expansion request: +24 nodes

What happened (Redwood view)
- During automated provision-to-ready rollout, nodes must pass Validating before joining the serving pool.
- Validation agent reported failures for the NCCL smoke test and/or GPU health checks (Xid/ECC thresholds) on multiple newly provisioned nodes.
- After repeated failures, quarantine logic triggered (per current policy), resulting in >30% of expansion nodes being quarantined. This pushed effective capacity below the customer’s expected post-expansion target.

Signals
- Fleet readiness summary showed: Ready nodes flat, Quarantined nodes spiking during the expansion window.
- Provision-to-ready step breakdown: higher failure rate in post-provision validation (NCCL + GPU health) than baseline.
- Scheduler metrics: fewer allocatable GPUs than planned; increased pending inference pods.

Customer provided symptoms
- “Throughput dropped right after the scale-up completed; we expected +24 GPUs but only saw a small increase. We’re now getting timeouts at our normal peak.”
- Customer screenshots: their internal monitoring showing p95 latency increase and request timeout spikes.

Hypothesis
- Validation thresholds and/or timeouts are too aggressive for the current us-east A100 networking variance during bring-up.
- Quarantine triggers are currently too sensitive during staged rollout; we quarantine rather than extending retries or routing for additional diagnostics.

Immediate mitigation plan (agreed during incident)
1) Add temporary buffer capacity by attaching spare ready nodes from the same image channel where available (Fleet/Infra).
2) For newly provisioned nodes failing NCCL only, perform a controlled reimage + rerun validation once before quarantine (operator workflow).
3) Temporarily increase NCCL validation timeout and reduce false-positive quarantine by increasing max attempts (feature-flagged per region/pool) while keeping hard GPU health failures quarantine-worthy.
4) Keep customer informed with a clear timeline and interim operating guidance (reduce burst concurrency until capacity returns).

Resolution criteria
- Customer serving pool returns to expected GPU count (or within 1–2 GPUs of target) and error/timeout rate normalizes.
- Quarantine rate returns to baseline; no ongoing spike in validation failures.

Notes
- This ticket focuses on customer-facing escalation + mitigation. Root-cause tuning items are tracked as follow-ups (see linked issues).
comments: 2025-10-21 09:18 PT — Harish Reddy (Infra/Fleet): Created ticket from escalation. Customer reports capacity shortfall after Dedicated expansion. Looping in SRE + Fleet automation. Pool id dp_7f3d9f, us-east A100. Initial check: many nodes in Quarantined state post-validation.

2025-10-21 09:26 PT — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Acknowledged. Taking incident coordinator role. Pulling dashboards: provision-to-ready failure heatmap shows spike in us-east A100 within last 2h. Starting internal incident INC-2025-10-21-07 for tracking.

2025-10-21 09:41 PT — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra/Fleet): Confirmed: 8/24 new nodes quarantined (33%). Top failure reason: nccl_smoke_allreduce timeout; secondary: gpu_health_xid_count_above_threshold (2 nodes). Proposing we treat GPU health failures as hard quarantine, but for NCCL-only failures, reimage and retry validation before quarantine.

2025-10-21 09:53 PT — Aisha Bello (SRE): From dashboard: time-to-ready P95 elevated but main customer issue is quarantine spike. Also seeing validation flake rate increase (NCCL). Suggest raising NCCL timeout + adding jitter backoff; ensure we don’t page on flake noise during rollout. Logging pointers captured in fleet-readiness artifacts.

2025-10-21 10:05 PT — Connor O’Brien (Release Eng): Reminder: this is within staged rollout scope (us-east A100 only). Any threshold/timeout change should be behind rollout flag with rollback plan. Can do a hotfix config change via helm values for validation agent if approved by SRE + Fleet.

2025-10-21 10:22 PT — Support (on behalf of customer): Customer asks if we can ‘unquarantine’ nodes to restore capacity quickly. Also asks whether they should temporarily enable fallback. (They prefer not to due to compliance constraints.)

2025-10-21 10:34 PT — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Response to Support: We can restore capacity without enabling fallback. Plan: reimage + rerun validation for NCCL-only quarantines; add buffer nodes where possible. ETA 60–90 minutes for initial recovery; will provide updates every 30 minutes.

2025-10-21 11:11 PT — Harish Reddy (Infra/Fleet): Executed controlled reimage on 5 NCCL-only quarantined nodes; 4 passed on retry, 1 failed again and remains quarantined. Added 3 spare Ready nodes from same channel. Effective serving GPU count now +19 vs pre-expansion (target +24).

2025-10-21 11:28 PT — Vanessa Ortiz (Infra/Fleet): For remaining NCCL-only failures, suspect transient network bring-up jitter. Proposing temporary config: NCCL smoke timeout 90s -> 180s and max attempts 2 -> 4 for us-east A100 only, with auto-quarantine only after retries. Will track as ENG-4830 follow-up + config change PR if needed.

2025-10-21 11:43 PT — Support: Shared update with customer. Customer confirms request timeout rate is dropping; p95 improving. They can operate under reduced peak concurrency for next ~2 hours if needed.

2025-10-21 12:17 PT — Benji Okafor (Platform): Confirmed readiness service is correctly recording state transitions and artifacts. Failure reasons are consistent (nccl_smoke_allreduce_timeout). No evidence of control-plane misclassification; looks like validation sensitivity/timeout tuning.

2025-10-22 08:52 PT — Sergio Costa (Observability): Added temporary panel annotation and filtered view for dp_7f3d9f in readiness summary dashboard; confirms quarantine spike ended after reimage/retry. Captured metrics snapshot for post-incident review.

2025-10-22 10:06 PT — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Customer stable overnight. Serving GPU count at expected target (pre-expansion +24; 2 original nodes also replaced). Closing internal incident; leaving this ticket open until customer confirms no recurrence during next peak.

2025-10-24 09:30 PT — Support: Customer confirmed normal throughput and no new timeouts during peak. They requested a short write-up and assurance that quarantine won’t aggressively reduce capacity during future expansions.

2025-10-24 10:02 PT — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Provided customer-facing summary: root cause likely validation timeouts too strict under transient network jitter; mitigations applied (reimage/retry, spare capacity); follow-ups underway to tune thresholds and add capacity-safe behavior. Marking ticket Resolved.
source.resolution:
Mitigated capacity shortfall by (1) reimaging and re-validating NCCL-only quarantined nodes, (2) adding spare Ready nodes to the pool, and (3) operationally treating GPU health failures as hard quarantine while allowing additional retries for NCCL-only failures. Customer confirmed throughput/latency returned to baseline.

Follow-ups
- Tune NCCL smoke test timeout/retry/backoff and quarantine thresholds (us-east A100 staged rollout) — tracked in ENG-4830.
- Review quarantine policy to avoid large capacity drops during expansions (e.g., cap % quarantined before pausing expansion and paging ops) — to be added under reliability initiative.
- Document operator workflow: when to reimage vs quarantine, and how to communicate capacity deltas to customers — runbook update referenced.

source.summary: Benchmark landing page intermittently timing out / failing to load during launch traffic (504/5xx)
source.environment: prod (public marketing site) - global via CDN; origin in us-east
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Customer reported the public benchmark landing page intermittently timing out during launch-day traffic.
- Symptoms included blank page loads, long spinner, and browser console showing failed fetch for benchmark results JSON.

Customer report:
- Company: HelixCode (self-serve evaluation)
- Contact: (via support form) “Alex M., Eng”
- Reported at: 2025-07-09 10:18 PT
- Customer impact: Unable to view interactive benchmark table; blocked from sharing results internally during evaluation.

Impact (internal):
- Multiple reports from community + at least 1 prospect via support.
- Marketing-site conversion path impacted (benchmark page -> Hosted API signup / Dedicated demo CTA).

Environment:
- Production marketing site behind CDN
- Benchmark results fetched client-side from results service (JSON)

Steps to reproduce (as observed during incident window):
1) Navigate to https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
2) Hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) a few times or open in an incognito window
3) Observe request to /benchmarks/results/latest.json (or similar) occasionally failing

Expected:
- Landing page renders quickly; interactive table loads benchmark dataset consistently.

Observed:
- Intermittent 504/5xx on benchmark JSON fetch; page appears to hang waiting for data.
- Some edge locations served slow responses leading to timeouts.

Attachments / evidence:
- Customer screenshot described: “Gateway timeout” on data request.
- Internal monitoring: elevated CDN 5xx and increased origin latency correlated with cache misses.

Notes:
- Linked to launch traffic spike and cache behavior; see related incident INC-2025-07-09-EDGE-03.
comments: 2025-07-09 10:26 PT — Jasmine Liu (Support): Created ticket based on inbound report from HelixCode. Also seeing similar complaints in #support and #marketing. Looping in SRE on-call. Related: #incidents thread mentions benchmark page latency.

2025-07-09 10:33 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Acknowledged. We’re tracking elevated 5xx at CDN edge + increased origin latency for benchmark results service. Tying this SUP to incident INC-2025-07-09-EDGE-03. Will update with mitigation ETA.

2025-07-09 10:41 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Preliminary: cache miss storm. Suspect cache key includes query params from table filters and/or an upstream header variation, causing low hit rate and origin overload. Seeing p95 origin latency > 8s during spikes.

2025-07-09 10:52 PT — Logan Wright (Telemetry): Confirmed spike in benchmark page interactions; filter changes drive repeated fetches. Some clients appear to request unique URLs (export + filter state). Recommend forcing canonical fetch URL and tightening cache key at CDN.

2025-07-09 11:08 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Mitigation in progress: (1) set CDN cache key to ignore non-functional query params for latest.json, (2) enable stale-while-revalidate, (3) increase TTL for versioned artifacts, (4) warm cache from 3 regions.

2025-07-09 11:29 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Metrics improving: edge 5xx down, origin CPU back to baseline. Synthetic checks from eu-west/us-east now passing consistently. Keeping heightened monitoring for 2 hours.
2025-07-09 11:46 PT — Jasmine Liu (Support): Replied to customer with status + workaround (direct PDF link + retry guidance) while fix propagates. Asked them to confirm if still seeing timeouts.
2025-07-09 12:22 PT — Jasmine Liu (Support): Customer confirmed page loads successfully now; interactive table populates. They were able to share the link internally.

2025-07-10 09:05 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE): Incident resolved. Root cause summary posted in INC-2025-07-09-EDGE-03: cache miss storm due to overly granular cache key + lack of SWR leading to origin overload during launch spike. Follow-ups filed under ENG-18610 (guardrails/alerts) and ENG-18566 (results service caching defaults).
2025-07-10 09:12 PT — Jasmine Liu (Support): Marking SUP resolved. Customer updated with final note + reassurance that methodology assets/PDF remain accessible if any future edge issues.
source.resolution: Resolved via CDN caching configuration changes and cache warming to prevent cache miss storms on the benchmark results JSON during launch traffic. Enabled stale-while-revalidate and adjusted cache key behavior to improve hit rate; origin latency and edge 5xx returned to baseline. Customer confirmed recovery.
source.customer_facing_update: We identified an issue affecting the benchmark page load during a launch-traffic spike, where the results dataset request could intermittently time out. We applied a caching fix and validated from multiple regions. The page should now load consistently; please let us know if you see any further timeouts.
post_resolution_followups: Add synthetic monitoring and alerting specifically for benchmark results JSON and marketing-page critical path (ENG-18610).
Harden results service caching defaults + document recommended CDN settings for public artifact delivery (ENG-18566).
Review analytics-driven fetch patterns to reduce unnecessary unique requests from filter/export actions (owned by Logan Wright + Felix Schneider).

- Customer impact: Unable to view interactive benchmark table; blocked from sharing results internally during evaluation.

Impact (internal):
- Multiple reports from community + at least 1 prospect via support.
- Marketing-site conversion path impacted (benchmark page -> Hosted API signup / Dedicated demo CTA).

Environment:
- Production marketing site behind CDN
- Benchmark results fetched client-side from results service (JSON)

Steps to reproduce (as observed during incident window):
1) Navigate to https://redwoodinference.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar
2) Hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) a few times or open in an incognito window
3) Observe request to /benchmarks/results/latest.json (or similar) occasionally failing

Expected:
- Landing page renders quickly; interactive table loads benchmark dataset consistently.

Observed:
- Intermittent 504/5xx on benchmark JSON fetch; page appears to hang waiting for data.
- Some edge locations served slow responses leading to timeouts.

Attachments / evidence:
- Customer screenshot described: “Gateway timeout” on data request.
- Internal monitoring: elevated CDN 5xx and increased origin latency correlated with cache misses.

Notes:
- Linked to launch traffic spike and cache behavior; see related incident INC-2025-07-09-EDGE-03.

source.summary: Private VPC installer fails pulling container images due to missing/incorrect ECR VPC endpoints (private DNS)
source.description:
Issue summary:
Acme Bank pilot install in a private-only AWS VPC fails during the "pull images" step. Nodes cannot pull images from ECR because required VPC endpoints are missing OR private DNS is not enabled on the ECR endpoints.

Impact:
- Blocks initial Redwood Private install (hard stop during bootstrap)
- Affects pilot timeline; customer unable to proceed to validation tests

Customer / context:
- Customer: Acme Bank (regulated)
- Deployment: Redwood Private (VPC) on AWS (EKS assumed)
- Network posture: no direct internet egress from private subnets; NAT intentionally not provisioned
- Customer is using Terraform-based VPC provisioning but did not use our endpoint defaults (yet)

Environment details (as provided by customer):
- AWS Region: us-east-1
- VPC: vpc-0f2c*** (private subnets only for worker nodes)
- EKS: private cluster endpoint enabled; worker nodes in private subnets
- DNS: AmazonProvidedDNS enabled on VPC, but endpoint private DNS settings unclear
- Egress: no NAT gateway; no outbound proxy

Expected result:
Installer should pull required images (ECR) successfully when the VPC is configured with Redwood-supported endpoints/DNS.

Actual result:
Image pulls fail with DNS resolution errors / timeouts to ECR.

Steps to reproduce (customer):
1) Provision EKS cluster in private subnets (no NAT)
2) Run redwood-private-installer (v0.19.3) from a bastion inside the VPC
3) During bootstrap, installer schedules core services; nodes attempt to pull images
4) Pods remain in ImagePullBackOff / ErrImagePull

Logs / evidence:
From customer (kubectl describe pod):
- Failed to pull image "***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/redwood/control-plane:0.19.3":
  rpc error: code = Unknown desc = failed to resolve reference ***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/redwood/control-plane:0.19.3: failed to do request: Head "https://***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/v2/redwood/control-plane/manifests/0.19.3": dial tcp: lookup ***.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com on 10.60.0.2:53: no such host

Additional node events:
- Warning  Failed     kubelet  Error: ErrImagePull
- Warning  Failed     kubelet  Error: ImagePullBackOff

Customer notes:
- They created "some" interface endpoints (STS, logs) but did not create ECR endpoints initially.
- After adding ECR endpoints, error changed from timeout to NXDOMAIN, suggesting private DNS not active or resolver path not reaching the endpoint.

Investigation notes (Redwood):
- For private-only subnets with no NAT, ECR requires:
  - Interface VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ecr.api
  - Interface VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.ecr.dkr
  - Gateway VPC endpoint: com.amazonaws.us-east-1.s3 (for image layer download path)
  - DNS must resolve *.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com and api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com to the endpoint ENIs (private DNS enabled on endpoints).
- Common failure mode: endpoints created but Private DNS disabled (or conflicting private hosted zone).
- Secondary common failure: endpoint security group does not allow inbound 443 from node subnets / node security group.

What we asked the customer to verify:
- Confirm these endpoints exist in the VPC and are in the same VPC as EKS nodes:
  - ecr.api, ecr.dkr, s3
- Confirm on the ECR interface endpoints:
  - "Enable Private DNS name" = true
- Confirm endpoint SG allows inbound tcp/443 from:
  - EKS worker node SG (or the node CIDR ranges)
- Confirm VPC attributes:
  - enableDnsSupport=true
  - enableDnsHostnames=true

Workaround provided (short-term):
Option A (preferred): Add/enable required endpoints
- Create interface endpoints for ecr.api and ecr.dkr with Private DNS enabled
- Ensure endpoint SG allows inbound 443 from node SG / node CIDR
- Create S3 gateway endpoint and associate to private route tables

Option B (temporary, if policy allows): Provide controlled egress
- Add NAT gateway for private subnets OR allow outbound via approved proxy to ECR/S3
- This is not recommended for the target locked-down posture; use only to unblock while endpoints are provisioned

Resolution:
Customer created the missing endpoints and reconfigured endpoint Private DNS settings:
- Added interface endpoints: ecr.api, ecr.dkr
- Enabled Private DNS on both
- Updated endpoint SG to allow 443 from the EKS node SG
- Added S3 gateway endpoint and associated it to the private route tables

After changes:
- New pods pulled images successfully
- Existing failing pods recovered after delete/recreate (or rollout restart)

Product / engineering follow-up:
- Filed as recurring pilot blocker. We should proactively detect missing endpoints during installer preflight and provide explicit remediation steps.
- Linked PR adds installer preflight detection + improved warning/error messaging:
  - sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-19110-detect-vpc-endpoints-and-warn-on-missing-ecr.json

Support notes / customer comms:
- Sent customer a checklist and endpoint list (ECR + S3) and asked for screenshots of endpoint Private DNS + SG rules.
- Customer confirmed resolution on 2025-06-21 during pilot check-in.

Suggested doc update (for DevEx/Docs, not blocking closure):
- Deployment guide should explicitly call out S3 gateway endpoint requirement for ECR layer pulls (in addition to ecr.api/ecr.dkr).
- Add troubleshooting section: "NXDOMAIN for *.dkr.ecr" -> check Private DNS on endpoint + conflicting private hosted zones.

Closure criteria:
- Customer install proceeds past image pull stage
- Installer preflight improvement tracked via linked PR

source.summary: eu-west: burst admission controller timeouts causing elevated 5xx for Dedicated burst requests
source.description:
Issue summary:
Northpeak Search reports intermittent request failures when attempting to use Dedicated Burst in eu-west. Their baseline Dedicated capacity remains healthy, but when traffic exceeds baseline and routing attempts to borrow burst capacity, requests fail with 5xx.

Impact:
- Affected: burst-eligible traffic (requests that require capacity above reserved baseline).
- Customer-visible symptoms: elevated 503/504 with message "burst admission timeout"; occasional retries succeed.
- Not affected: baseline (non-burst) requests; other regions.

Customer notes:
- Spike window: 2025-05-22 ~10:35–11:20 UTC.
- They observed 3–6% failure rate on burst workload during indexing job.
- They can reproduce by running their weekly indexing job (high sustained QPS + long contexts).

Environment:
- Deployment: Dedicated
- Region: eu-west
- Burst: enabled (beta)
- Gateway: rw-gw-eu-west-3

Initial hypothesis:
Admission controller in eu-west is intermittently timing out due to dependency latency (fleet telemetry lag / policy evaluation) or internal saturation; routing is configured to fail closed on admission timeout, returning 503/504 for burst-only requests.

Links:
- Slack: #incidents thread (see sources/slack/incidents/1739649912.json) referenced by on-call.
- Runbook: Burst Pool Overload Mitigation.

Request:
Please investigate eu-west burst admission timeouts, mitigate customer impact, and confirm if burst should be temporarily disabled in eu-west while we stabilize telemetry / circuit breaker thresholds.
comments: 2025-05-22 11:02 UTC — Miguel Santos (CS): Customer (Northpeak Search) reports burst requests failing in eu-west. They see 503/504 and their client logs show "burst admission timeout". Asking SRE to triage; customer is running an indexing job and cannot easily pause.

2025-05-22 11:07 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Acknowledged. Pulling dashboards for eu-west burst admission latency + denial breakdown. Will update within 30 min. Current status page: no ongoing incident declared.

2025-05-22 11:15 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Preliminary: burst_admission_controller request latency p99 spiking > 4s in eu-west; routing timeout for admission call is 2s. This aligns with burst-only failures. Baseline Dedicated serving latency looks stable. Suspecting telemetry lag / policy engine dependency latency. Starting incident bridge and tagging #incidents.

2025-05-22 11:18 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE lead): Please treat as Sev2. Goal: protect baseline tenants and reduce customer errors. If admission is timing out, we should fail fast with deterministic denial (429/overload) rather than hanging; consider enabling circuit breaker / kill-switch for eu-west burst if needed.

2025-05-22 11:25 UTC — Ethan Park (Control plane): Checking admission controller logs for eu-west. Seeing periodic spikes in policy-eval duration correlated with fleet telemetry staleness. There may be a retry loop causing head-of-line blocking when telemetry is behind >30s.

2025-05-22 11:31 UTC — Peter Holtz (Routing SRE): Confirmed routing config: admission call timeout is 2000ms, and on timeout we currently return 503 (no fallback to baseline because request is already above reserved concurrency cap). We can switch to fail-closed immediate denial with reason code (BURST_ADMISSION_TIMEOUT) and shorter timeouts to reduce tail, but customer will still see rejections.

2025-05-22 11:37 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Mitigation option 1: temporarily disable burst in eu-west for affected tenant(s) via region/hardware allowlist toggle (prevents timeout path; customer hits baseline caps with explicit 429/limit reached behavior).
Mitigation option 2: enable global circuit breaker for eu-west burst pool to shed burst admission attempts while telemetry recovers.
Recommendation: do both (disable eu-west bursting broadly) until we fix the admission path latency.

2025-05-22 11:45 UTC — Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): eu-west burst pool is currently at 78–85% utilization (expected during beta load test window). We also had telemetry lag from a collector restart around 10:40 UTC. That likely pushed the admission controller into conservative mode with extra checks; shouldn’t be taking seconds though.

2025-05-22 11:52 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Executed: enabled eu-west burst circuit breaker (fleet safety cap) + reduced admission controller timeout budget to 750ms while we roll back eu-west burst allowlist. Errors should convert from 503 to deterministic 429/overload for burst-only attempts.
Customer impact expectation: fewer timeouts/5xx; more clear rejections while burst is temporarily unavailable.

2025-05-22 12:05 UTC — Miguel Santos (CS): Informed customer we’re mitigating by temporarily disabling burst in eu-west to stop timeouts; baseline Dedicated remains available. Asked them to share a 15-min window of request IDs for correlation.

2025-05-22 12:22 UTC — Ethan Park (Control plane): Found likely cause: telemetry staleness triggered an internal recompute path (fairness bucket refill + safety cap recalculation) which was running synchronously on the request path for some requests. That path is expensive when telemetry is missing/lagging. We have a hotfix in progress to guard/smooth under telemetry lag (aligns with prior beta fix).

2025-05-22 12:40 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Rolled back eu-west burst allowlist (region-level toggle) for beta tenants. Admission latency is now back to normal (p99 < 120ms) since call volume is near zero. Confirmed no elevated baseline latency for non-burst tenants.
Declaring incident mitigated; keeping ticket open until customer confirms stability and we have follow-up owner + rollout plan.

2025-05-23 09:10 UTC — Miguel Santos (CS): Customer confirms baseline is stable and 5xx errors are gone. They understand burst is temporarily disabled in eu-west and asked for ETA for re-enable. They request at least 48h notice before next indexing run (Tuesday 06:00 UTC).

2025-05-23 10:05 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE): Plan: keep eu-west burst disabled until hotfix is deployed and we complete a small game-day style test in eu-west. Target earliest re-enable window: 2025-05-27 pending SLO gate green.
Action items added in #incidents: (1) move telemetry-staleness recompute off request path, (2) tighten admission timeout + return deterministic denial reason codes, (3) confirm circuit breaker threshold tuning for eu-west.

2025-05-23 10:18 UTC — Sean Gallagher (SRE lead): Closing as Resolved with burst disabled in eu-west as the immediate mitigation. Reopen if customer requires burst re-enable before fix is validated or if baseline SLOs regress.
source.resolution:
Mitigated customer-facing 5xx by disabling burst in eu-west (region allowlist rollback) and enabling burst circuit breaker behavior to shed load. Confirmed baseline Dedicated traffic unaffected.

Root cause (working): admission controller request-path latency spikes under fleet telemetry staleness; synchronous recompute path created head-of-line blocking, leading to admission timeouts and 503/504 returned by routing.

Customer outcome: 5xx eliminated; burst temporarily unavailable in eu-west until hotfix + validation.

Follow-ups:
- Ship telemetry-lag guard/hotfix and validate in eu-west before re-enabling burst.
- Ensure admission timeout returns deterministic denial reason and avoids 5xx where possible.
- Review eu-west collector/telemetry restart procedures to reduce staleness windows.
- Coordinate re-enable ETA and change notice with CS.

Please investigate eu-west burst admission timeouts, mitigate customer impact, and confirm if burst should be temporarily disabled in eu-west while we stabilize telemetry / circuit breaker thresholds.

source.summary: Noisy-neighbor tail latency increase for non-burst Dedicated tenant during another tenant’s burst window (us-east / H100)
source.description:
Issue summary
- Northpeak Search (Dedicated, burst NOT enabled) reported elevated p99 latency and intermittent request timeouts during a 35–45 min window on 2025-05-16.
- Correlation observed between Northpeak’s latency spike and a separate tenant’s burst activity against the shared us-east H100 burst pool.

Impact
- Customer-reported: p99 latency increased from ~2.2s baseline to 6–9s for chat completion requests; a subset of requests hit client timeout (30s) and were retried.
- Redwood telemetry: elevated tail latency (p95/p99) on Northpeak’s dedicated route, plus increased queueing time at router/scheduler boundary.
- Duration: ~14:05–14:47 UTC (approx; see investigation notes).

Customer expectations / concern
- Customer is explicitly not opted into burst and expects neighbor activity not to affect their SLOs.
- Asked whether Dedicated implies strict isolation and whether Redwood is prioritizing burst traffic over reserved baseline traffic.

Environment
- prod / us-east
- Hardware class: H100
- Northpeak Dedicated baseline capacity: 8x H100 (reserved)
- Burst pool: shared managed pool, us-east H100

Initial hypotheses
1) Smart routing fallback or capacity spillover incorrectly sending burst traffic into non-burst dedicated pools.
2) Shared upstream bottleneck (router CPU, rate limiter, admission controller latency, shared KV cache service, or network) causing head-of-line blocking.
3) Fleet safety cap not engaging quickly enough; burst pool saturation caused global contention in the serving runtime / scheduler on the same cluster.
4) Telemetry lag causing the admission controller to admit too many burst requests temporarily.

What we need to answer
- Confirm whether Northpeak traffic ever ran on burst GPUs (it should not) vs. whether the impact was an upstream shared component.
- Identify the guardrail that failed (or was missing) and put a mitigation in place before continuing beta burst testing in us-east.

Customer-visible request
- Provide written explanation of root cause, assurances on isolation, and what changes were made to prevent recurrence.

Attachments / references
- Slack: #incidents thread 1739649912 (beta burst test latency event)
- Dashboards: Burst Saturation (us-east/H100), Dedicated Tenant Latency (Northpeak), Router Queue Depth (us-east)
- Runbook: Burst pool overload mitigation
comments:
2025-05-16 14:18 UTC — Miguel Santos (Reporter)
Customer (Northpeak) escalation via CS: “Our p99 jumped to ~8s starting around 14:05 UTC. We do not have burst enabled. Did you move us onto shared capacity?” Customer provided request IDs and noted spike coincided with their internal batch job, but request rate is within normal envelope (+10%).

Asks: please confirm isolation, and whether an incident is in progress. Tagging SRE due to potential neighbor impact + beta burst testing today.

2025-05-16 14:23 UTC — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Acknowledged. Treating as Sev2 until confirmed. Pulling dedicated tenant latency dashboard + burst pool panels. Also pinging #incidents and #eng-sre.

Immediate checks:
- Any failover/fallback routing toggles?
- Any evidence Northpeak ran on burst pool nodes?
- Burst pool saturation and admission rate in us-east/H100.

2025-05-16 14:31 UTC — Allison Grant (Observability)
Dashboard notes:
- us-east/H100 burst pool utilization hits ~92–96% between 14:06–14:41 UTC.
- burst_admission_denied_total spikes after 14:20 UTC, but only after saturation was already high.
- Router queue depth increased ~3x during that window across multiple tenants (not only burst-enabled).
- Northpeak p99 latency tracks router queue depth more closely than GPU busy time.

Hypothesis: shared router/scheduler bottleneck rather than Northpeak running on burst GPUs.

2025-05-16 14:36 UTC — Mina Haddad (Smart Routing)
Routing check: Northpeak tenant policy still pins to dedicated pool, burst disabled. No policy change events. No evidence of fallback into burst pool either.

However, we do see increased “admission decision latency” in routing logs during that time. If routing calls admission controller synchronously for burst-enabled tenants, and that service is overloaded, it could increase latency for unrelated routing paths depending on how shared the threadpools are.

2025-05-16 14:44 UTC — Rafael Mendes (SRE Manager)
Suggest applying runbook step 1: reduce max burst ratio and/or enable global circuit breaker for us-east/H100 while we confirm blast radius.

Also: check if we are sharing the same ingress/Envoy cluster for both dedicated and burst traffic. If yes, we may be CPU-saturated on ingress which would explain cross-tenant impact.

2025-05-16 14:51 UTC — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Mitigation applied at 14:49 UTC:
- Reduced burst pool safety cap (us-east/H100) from 0.30 to 0.15 of regional headroom.
- Temporarily disabled burst allow-list for the one tenant driving sustained burst (per Product-approved beta controls).

Result: router queue depth trending down within ~3–5 minutes; Northpeak p99 started recovering by ~14:55 UTC. Continuing to monitor for 30 minutes before telling customer we’re stable.

2025-05-16 15:05 UTC — Miguel Santos (CS)
Updated customer: We are investigating a suspected interaction with ongoing burst beta activity in the region. Confirmed no intentional changes were made to Northpeak’s Dedicated configuration. Provided status: mitigation in place and metrics recovering. Customer asked for RCA within 48 hours.

2025-05-16 16:12 UTC — Ethan Park (Control Plane)
Checked audit events for Northpeak burst settings: no changes in last 14 days; burst_enabled=false; no admin overrides.

Admission controller metrics:
- P99 admission decision latency rose from ~12ms to ~180–250ms during 14:10–14:35 UTC.
- CPU throttling observed on admission controller pods.

This supports the theory that a shared dependency (admission controller) increased request processing time. Action item: make admission checks async/isolated per routing path, or ensure non-burst traffic doesn’t wait on burst admission control.

2025-05-17 09:02 UTC — Ravi Menon (Routing)
Deep dive:
- Dedicated traffic path SHOULD bypass burst admission entirely, but current routing implementation still performs a “capability fetch” call that hits the same policy evaluation service used by burst admission (shared client + shared circuit breaker).
- Under load, the shared client pool backs up and impacts non-burst tenants.

Fix direction:
1) Split routing clients: non-burst path must not depend on admission controller/policy engine latency.
2) Add strict timeouts + fail-open for non-burst capability fetch (do not block request).
3) SLO gate: if non-burst p95/p99 degrades, automatically hard-disable burst in region/hardware.

Tracking to ENG-4902 / PR-23988.

2025-05-18 13:40 UTC — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Status update:
- No evidence Northpeak requests ran on burst GPUs (confirmed by capacity attribution logs and per-node tenant tags).
- Root cause is shared routing dependency coupling: burst admission / policy evaluation latency bled into non-burst routing due to shared client pools and insufficient isolation.
- Mitigations still active: lowered us-east/H100 burst cap + tenant-level burst paused.

Requesting approval to re-enable burst only after PR-23988 SLO gating is deployed to prod us-east.

2025-05-19 18:22 UTC — Keiko Yamamoto (Autoscaling/Guardrails)
Added an additional guardrail on the burst pool autoscaler to avoid pushing admission controller and router into CPU contention:
- If router CPU > 75% for 5m OR admission decision p99 > 100ms for 5m, freeze burst scale-up and progressively tighten burst token bucket rate.

This is a short-term safety valve until routing decoupling lands.

2025-05-20 10:11 UTC — Miguel Santos (CS)
Customer-facing summary sent to Northpeak:
- Confirmed: Northpeak traffic remained on reserved dedicated capacity (no spillover onto shared burst pool).
- What happened: during a burst beta test in us-east/H100, the burst admission/policy evaluation path experienced elevated latency. A shared routing dependency caused increased queueing that affected some non-burst tenants’ tail latency.
- What we changed immediately: tightened burst caps and paused sustained burst activity in the region; latency returned to baseline.
- Preventing recurrence: shipping an SLO regression gate + additional isolation in routing; adding alerts that page SRE before non-burst SLOs degrade.

Customer satisfied with explanation; requested notification once burst is re-enabled in region so they can watch their own dashboards.

2025-05-20 10:27 UTC — Sean Gallagher (Assignee)
Closing as resolved.

Resolution criteria met:
- Northpeak p99 back to baseline for 72 hours.
- Guardrail tightened in us-east/H100.
- Engineering work tracked and prioritized (SLO gate + routing decoupling).

Follow-ups:
- Ensure SLO gate (PR-23988) is enabled for us-east before resuming beta bursts.
- Add explicit runbook step: verify non-burst routing path does not hit admission controller under load.
- Link this incident in burst beta retro notes.
source.resolution: Mitigated by tightening us-east/H100 burst safety caps and pausing sustained burst activity for the burst-enabled tenant. Investigation confirmed no spillover of non-burst Dedicated traffic onto burst GPUs; tail latency increase was caused by a shared routing dependency on the burst admission/policy evaluation path, leading to queueing under admission controller load. Preventative work tracked: routing decoupling, stricter timeouts/fail-open for non-burst paths, and SLO-based burst gating (auto-disable burst when non-burst latency regresses).
next_steps: Deploy and enable SLO regression gate for burst in us-east/H100 (PR-23988) and verify rollback behavior.
Routing: split client pools / remove non-burst dependency on admission controller and policy evaluation latency (owner: Smart Routing).
Add alert on admission decision p99 and router CPU that pages before non-burst SLO impact (owner: SRE/Observability).
Update CS enablement: how to explain that Dedicated does not run on burst GPUs, and what ‘shared dependencies’ are (owner: CS + Docs).

source.summary: Route experiments: variant traffic skew in eu-west during beta (expected 50/50, observed ~90/10)
source.description:
Issue summary:
- Acme AI reports that their route-level experiment in Console (beta) is heavily skewed in eu-west.
- Expected split: 50/50 between variants (control vs candidate model).
- Observed split: ~90/10 (varies by hour), affecting rollout confidence.

Impact:
- Experiment results are not interpretable; customer paused ramp due to concern that assignment is not deterministic/consistent.
- Potential product trust issue during beta.

Customer context:
- Customer: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Deployment: Dedicated; primary traffic in eu-west
- Experiment: Route experiment on /v1/chat/completions (route_id: chat-completions-prod)

Environment:
- prod, eu-west

What customer sees:
- Console results dashboard shows variant B receiving disproportionate traffic vs configured split.
- Customer also notes that skew is more pronounced for streaming requests.

Initial hypotheses:
1) Missing/low-entropy stickiness key leading to bucket collisions (e.g., IP-based fallback behind NAT).
2) Assignment cache/config propagation inconsistency specific to eu-west.
3) Metrics attribution/aggregation issue (skew in telemetry rather than actual routing).

Requested from customer:
- Sample request IDs and headers (redacted) for a few requests that landed in the "wrong" variant.
- Confirm what stickiness key is being passed (if any) via SDK.

Links:
- Slack thread: #eng-platform "variant-skew report / missing sticky key" (internal): sources/slack/eng-platform/1736538810-variant-skew-report-missing-sticky-key.json
- Dashboard (Grafana): Route Experiments > Variant Traffic Share (eu-west) (internal link)
- Dashboard (Grafana): Experiment Attribution Drop Rate (eu-west) (internal link)
comments:
2025-03-08 10:14 UTC — Logan Wright (Reporter)
Customer (Acme AI) pinged Support via shared Slack channel: configured 50/50 split but seeing ~90/10 in eu-west. They provided screenshots from Console results view and note this started after they rolled out a new build of their gateway/SDK wrapper on Mar 7.

Asked for:
- whether they pass a stable stickiness key (customer_id/user_id/session_id)
- sample request_id + response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id)

2025-03-08 11:02 UTC — Jordan Lee (Assignee)
Acknowledged. Will check policy engine evaluator logs and assignment distribution for experiment_id=exp_8f3a (from Console screenshot).

Next:
- Use internal assignment debug endpoint to compare hash inputs in eu-west vs us-east.
- Verify eu-west router build version and config cache behavior.

2025-03-08 12:20 UTC — Caleb Johnson
Checked telemetry: attribution is present for >99% of requests; this does not look like a pure attribution-drop artifact. The skew shows up in both request counts and token counts.

Noted: highest skew correlates with traffic that has no explicit stickiness key header. For those requests, the router is falling back to "client_ip" in eu-west (per span attributes).

2025-03-08 13:05 UTC — Jordan Lee
Used experiment assignment debug endpoint for 20 sample request_ids from eu-west.
Finding:
- stickiness_key_source=client_ip for most (missing x-redwood-sticky-key / no customer_id)
- Many requests map to same /24 NAT range; effective entropy is low.
- Hashing distribution across variants is sensitive to low-entropy keys; with current bucketing implementation, skew can happen in smaller populations and with IP clustering.

Also observed:
- In us-east, same customer traffic includes x-redwood-sticky-key from their edge layer (seems region-specific deployment mismatch on their side).

Action:
- Ask customer to confirm stickiness header is being set in eu-west.
- In parallel, propose product-side mitigation: improve default stickiness fallback (do not use client_ip by default) + emit warnings/guardrails in Console when stickiness key missing.

2025-03-08 13:22 UTC — Logan Wright
Sent customer guidance:
- For experiments, MUST pass a stable, high-entropy stickiness key per end-user or per customer session. IP-based stickiness is not recommended (NAT / proxies).
- Recommended: x-redwood-sticky-key = stable user_id or session_id.

Customer replied they assumed Redwood would "just split" without extra headers; their eu-west edge path is not injecting the sticky key header yet.

2025-03-09 09:10 UTC — Allison Grant
Adding observability notes:
- Confirmed skew alarm in eu-west: variant_share deviates >20% absolute from configured allocation for >15m.
- This is consistent with true routing skew, not just dashboard aggregation.

Proposed:
- Add a dedicated metric: experiment_missing_sticky_key_rate by {route, experiment_id, region}
- Add a Console banner when missing sticky key rate > threshold for the selected time range.

2025-03-10 16:40 UTC — Jordan Lee
Engineering fix in progress:
- PR opened to adjust safe defaults when stickiness key is missing (avoid low-entropy fallback causing skew; prefer request-scoped stable hashing inputs when available, and warn rather than silently falling back to IP).
- Also adding validation/warning surface to reduce recurrence.

Tracking PR:
- https://github.com/redwood/pr-28705-fix-variant-skew-when-stickiness-key-missing

Mitigation for customer now:
- Customer should set x-redwood-sticky-key in eu-west edge path ASAP.
- Until then, advise running experiment at 5/95 with manual override cohorts rather than relying on implicit bucketing.

2025-03-11 10:05 UTC — Logan Wright
Customer confirmed they deployed sticky key injection to eu-west (using hashed end_user_id). Traffic started normalizing within ~30 minutes.

Observed:
- Variant shares approaching expected 50/50.
- Customer still requests Redwood-side fix so other beta users don’t hit this.

2025-03-12 18:30 UTC — Jordan Lee
PR-28705 merged to main. Rollout plan:
- Feature flag gated in eu-west first, then expand.
- Add a warning when stickiness key is absent and population is small/likely to skew.

Also queued:
- Add/update integration test coverage to simulate missing sticky key and ensure we don’t silently choose client_ip fallback.

Ops ask:
- SRE to watch for any latency impact from new warning/metrics tagging (should be negligible).

2025-03-14 08:55 UTC — Sean Gallagher
SRE sign-off:
- Deployed to eu-west canary at 10%.
- No regression in p95 router latency; no increase in 5xx.
- Confirmed new metric series is within cardinality budget.

Plan:
- Ramp to 100% in eu-west today; then us-east tomorrow (beta accounts only).

2025-03-15 12:10 UTC — Logan Wright
Customer update:
- Fix rolled out to eu-west.
- Customer’s experiment allocation stable and matches configured split.
- Provided customer with updated troubleshooting guidance: always set x-redwood-sticky-key and confirm via response headers.

Marking Resolved; will close after 7 days if no recurrence.
source.resolution:
Root cause: In eu-west, a significant fraction of requests entered the experiment without an explicit stickiness key; router fell back to client_ip-based hashing (low entropy behind NAT/proxies), producing sustained variant skew. Customer’s eu-west edge path was missing sticky key injection, amplifying the issue.

Fix:
- Product-side: updated routing/assignment behavior to avoid low-entropy default stickiness fallback and to surface warnings/validation when sticky key is missing (PR-28705).
- Customer-side mitigation: customer deployed x-redwood-sticky-key injection in eu-west.

Verification:
- Post-mitigation, variant share converged to configured split in eu-west and remained stable for >24h.
- No detected performance regressions from the change.
next_steps: Follow up with Console team to ensure UX copy clearly communicates sticky key requirement and shows missing-sticky-key rate in results view.
Add runbook section: diagnosing skew specifically due to missing stickiness key + NAT/IP clustering patterns (link in alerts).
Monitor eu-west skew and missing_sticky_key_rate for Acme AI for 7 days; close if stable.

source.summary: Console route experiment results appear stale / not updating for active experiment (aggregation + propagation lag suspected)
source.description:
Customer reports that the new Console "Route Experiments" results dashboard is not updating (appears stuck on the same deltas/graphs) even though traffic is flowing to both variants.

Issue summary:
- Console results view for an active route experiment shows stale charts/metrics (latency/cost/error deltas unchanged for ~45–90 minutes).
- Customer validated traffic is being split (they see variant headers in responses).

Impact:
- Customer cannot make ramp/stop decisions from Console due to stale guardrail readouts.
- Increased risk of running an experiment longer than intended / missing regression.

Customer context:
- Account: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Experiment: route-level A/B test (A=baseline, B=model swap) on production route.
- Customer expectations: near-real-time results (they assumed <5–10 min lag).

Environment:
- Prod
- Regions observed: us-east and eu-west

What we think is happening (initial hypotheses):
1) Config propagation lag: experiment config updates (start/ramp) not consistently visible to results attribution pipeline, causing mismatched {experiment_id, variant_id} in rollups.
2) Analytics aggregation job delay: per-variant rollup dataset behind (job stalled, backlog, or partition not advancing), causing Console to read older materialized windows.
3) Attribution drop: experiment dimensions missing for a subset of requests (especially streaming/retries), reducing signal and keeping deltas flat.

Requested by customer:
- Confirmation whether this is expected / what the typical freshness SLA is.
- Workaround guidance while we investigate.

Triage checklist (Support):
- Confirm experiment status is "Running" in Console + capture experiment_id, route_id, time range.
- Validate variant assignment is active by checking response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id) and sample 20–50 requests.
- Check freshness banner behavior in Console (if any) and whether changing time window updates numbers.
- Ask customer to provide approximate time they started/ramped the experiment and their local timezone.

Internal notes:
- Likely tied to recent schema/attribution changes during beta (see related incident and attribution drop rate work).
comments: 2025-03-10 09:18 PT — Marissa Cole (Reporter): Customer (Acme AI) says results dashboard hasn’t moved since ~07:30 PT. They’re seeing variant headers in their own logs. Asking if data is delayed or stuck.

2025-03-10 09:33 PT — Sean Gallagher (SRE leadership): Please capture (a) experiment_id, (b) route_id normalization form, (c) regions with traffic, and (d) their selected Console time range. We’ve seen rollups lag when partitions don’t advance. Also check propagation lag metric for the experiment config service.

2025-03-10 09:41 PT — Support (Connor O’Brien): Sent customer quick workaround: widen time range to last 6h, hard refresh, and verify variant headers. Asked for experiment_id/route_id and timestamps of start/ramp actions.

2025-03-10 10:02 PT — Logan Wright (Telemetry): Looking at dashboards. Not seeing ingestion failure, but rollup job for experiment_variant_metrics is ~62 minutes behind for eu-west partitions. us-east is ~8 minutes behind. Could explain why customer sees “frozen” deltas if most of their traffic is eu-west.

2025-03-10 10:08 PT — Caleb Johnson (Metrics ingestion): Confirmed backlog in the aggregation worker queue. No crashes, but throughput dropped after we added dimensions last week. CPU saturation on one worker pool. We can scale workers and reprocess the missing partitions.

2025-03-10 10:17 PT — Rafael Mendes (Runbooks/alerts): Propagation lag metric looks okay (<90s p95) for config distribution in us-east; eu-west has occasional spikes (up to ~9m) but not enough to explain 60m staleness alone. This smells more like analytics delay than control-plane propagation.

2025-03-10 10:25 PT — Chloe Martin (Console backend): Console results API caches responses for 2 minutes max, so this isn’t front-end caching. If rollups lag, results API will return older windows with no explicit “data freshness” today. We should add a freshness indicator banner for beta users.

2025-03-10 10:46 PT — Logan Wright (Telemetry): Scaled aggregation workers + restarted one stuck consumer. Lag is dropping: eu-west now ~28 minutes behind and trending down. Expect caught up within ~20–30 minutes if traffic stays stable.

2025-03-10 11:12 PT — Support (Connor O’Brien): Updated customer: identified analytics rollup delay (eu-west) causing stale results; mitigation in progress. Provided workaround: use per-request variant headers for immediate verification + check Grafana guardrail dashboard if they need near-real-time signals while Console catches up.

2025-03-10 11:55 PT — Logan Wright (Telemetry): eu-west rollup lag now ~6 minutes behind. Console should start reflecting changes as next windows materialize. Also seeing slightly elevated attribution drop for streaming retries earlier in the morning; likely related to SUP-24877 path, but primary issue here was rollup backlog.

2025-03-11 08:20 PT — Support (Connor O’Brien): Customer confirmed results started updating again ~12:10 PT yesterday. They requested an explicit freshness indicator + an “as of” timestamp in Console.

2025-03-12 14:05 PT — Logan Wright (Telemetry): Follow-up actions filed internally: add alerting on rollup lag threshold for experiment datasets; add Console UI “Data freshness” indicator and surface rollup lag (read-only). Marking support ticket resolved.
source.resolution:
Root cause: analytics aggregation/rollup job backlog for experiment_variant_metrics (eu-west partitions) caused Console results to appear stale for ~60–90 minutes. Control-plane config propagation lag was not the primary driver.

Mitigation applied:
- Scaled aggregation worker pool and restarted a stuck consumer to drain backlog.
- Verified rollup lag returned to ~<10 minutes and Console results resumed updating.

Customer workaround provided:
- For immediate verification during staleness: sample responses and confirm variant assignment via response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id).
- Use Grafana experiment guardrail dashboards for near-real-time latency/error/cost by variant when Console is behind.
- Widen Console time range (e.g., last 6h) and refresh after ~10 minutes to catch up once rollups advance.
next_steps: Add alerting for experiment rollup lag per region with paging threshold (SRE + Telemetry).
Add Console “Data as of <timestamp>” + rollup lag indicator for results view (Console & Tooling).
Capacity review for aggregation workers after adding experiment dimensions; confirm headroom for beta expansion (Observability).
Track attribution drop rate separately (esp. streaming retries) under SUP-24877 to avoid conflating with rollup delays.

title: Console route experiment results appear stale / not updating for active experiment (aggregation + propagation lag suspected)
preview:
Customer reports that the new Console "Route Experiments" results dashboard is not updating (appears stuck on the same deltas/graphs) even though traffic is flowing to both variants.

Issue summary:
- Console results view for an active route experiment shows stale charts/metrics (latency/cost/error deltas unchanged for ~45–90 minutes).
- Customer validated traffic is being split (they see variant headers in responses).

Impact:
- Customer cannot make ramp/stop decisions from Console due to stale guardrail readouts.
- Increased risk of running an experiment longer than intended / missing regression.

Customer context:
- Account: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Experiment: route-level A/B test (A=baseline, B=model swap) on production route.
- Customer expectations: near-real-time results (they assumed <5–10 min lag).

Environment:
- Prod
- Regions observed: us-east and eu-west

What we think is happening (initial hypotheses):
1) Config propagation lag: experiment config updates (start/ramp) not consistently visible to results attribution pipeline, causing mismatched {experiment_id, variant_id} in rollups.
2) Analytics aggregation job delay: per-variant rollup dataset behind (job stalled, backlog, or partition not advancing), causing Console to read older materialized windows.
3) Attribution drop: experiment dimensions missing for a subset of requests (especially streaming/retries), reducing signal and keeping deltas flat.

Requested by customer:
- Confirmation whether this is expected / what the typical freshness SLA is.
- Workaround guidance while we investigate.

Triage checklist (Support):
- Confirm experiment status is "Running" in Console + capture experiment_id, route_id, time range.
- Validate variant assignment is active by checking response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id) and sample 20–50 requests.
- Check freshness banner behavior in Console (if any) and whether changing time window updates numbers.
- Ask customer to provide approximate time they started/ramped the experiment and their local timezone.

Internal notes:
- Likely tied to recent schema/attribution changes during beta (see related incident and attribution drop rate work).

source.summary: Tool call arguments occasionally returned as plain text; downstream JSON parser crashes
source.description:
Issue summary:
Northwind CX reports that tool calling sometimes produces `tool_calls.arguments` that is not JSON at all (e.g., English sentences like 'Call search_tickets with query=...'). Their orchestrator expects valid JSON and treats this as a fatal error.

Impact:
~1-2% of requests fail in their production workflow; they are rate-limited and cannot just retry many times.

Notes:
Customer is pinning model version but sees this when their prompt is near context limit.
comments: Chloe Martin (Support): Customer provided 8 examples. In each, tool name is correct but args are natural language.
Ethan Park (Eng): Hypothesis: when we hit context truncation, the tool-call schema instructions get dropped; model still decides to call tool but formats args conversationally.
Chloe Martin (Support): Proposed workaround: move tool schema earlier in system prompt + add 'Respond with STRICT JSON' guard. Customer will test.
Ethan Park (Eng): Also enabling `tool_args_json_mode=strict` on their route. This adds a post-generation repair/validation step. Not perfect but should reduce failures.
Chloe Martin (Support): Customer confirms failure rate down to ~0.2%. Closing.

source.summary: token accounting discrepancy: embeddings usage higher than client-side tokenization
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer sees token accounting discrepancy for embeddings. Redwood Usage dashboard shows ~8-10% more prompt tokens than their local tokenization of input strings.

Impact:
Mostly finance reconciliation; no outage.

Customer notes:
- They use their own tokenizer (tiktoken-like)
- They batch many short strings
- They also send JSON with whitespace/newlines

Requested outcome:
Confirm tokenizer + billing rules for embeddings route.
comments: Rachel Kim (Support): tagging Eng for confirmation of billing tokenizer for rw-embed-3-large. Customer calling it a 'token accounting discrepancy' and wants to align finance dashboards.

Ethan Park (Eng): For rw-embed-3-large we use the model-native tokenizer; it differs from their tokenizer on unicode normalization + whitespace. Also, we count the full request payload text field; if they include leading/trailing whitespace it counts.
Rachel Kim (Support): Provided guidance + suggested they use /v1/tokenize endpoint in staging to compare counts with Redwood.
Ethan Park (Eng): Added note: embeddings requests with multiple inputs are counted per input after normalization; batching doesn't change total tokens.
source.resolution: Mismatch due to different tokenizer/normalization and inclusion of whitespace; customer will validate via Redwood tokenization endpoint and adjust reconciliation.

source.summary: Route experiments: missing experiment_id/variant_id attribution on streaming requests (worse on retries) causing gaps in Console results
source.environment: prod (us-east primary; reports also from eu-west)
source.content:
Issue Summary
- Customer (Acme AI, route experiments beta) reports that experiment results in Console are inconsistent when using streaming responses. Specifically, p95/p99 latency and cost/token charts show a large percentage of traffic under “Unknown / unattributed” despite the route being under an active experiment.
- Customer verified they are receiving variant headers intermittently on streaming responses.

Customer Impact
- Console experiment dashboards appear incomplete/incorrect (variant comparison not trustworthy).
- Guardrail evaluation confidence reduced because unattributed traffic is excluded from per-variant aggregations.
- Customer is holding ramp from 10% -> 50% until attribution is reliable.

Environment
- Redwood Hosted API via Dedicated capacity.
- Regions: primarily us-east; customer also observed similar behavior after failover to eu-west.
- Endpoints: /v1/chat/completions with streaming enabled.
- Client: Redwood Python SDK + custom retry wrapper (retries on network disconnect / 5xx / upstream timeout).

Expected
- For every request routed under an experiment, experiment_id + variant_id should be present:
  - In response headers (for debugging) where applicable.
  - In request context so OTel spans + metrics emit {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} consistently.
  - Persisted across streaming lifecycle and across retry paths (including internal re-attempts).

Actual
- Non-streaming requests show near-100% attribution.
- Streaming requests show missing experiment_id/variant_id in:
  - OTel spans for a subset of requests.
  - Aggregated metrics (rollups) feeding Console results.
- Missing rate increases when a retry occurs (disconnect mid-stream or gateway retry).

Steps to Reproduce (internal)
1) Enable a route experiment on a chat route (A=baseline model, B=variant model).
2) Send streaming chat completions with a forced intermittent disconnect (tcp reset or client cancel) to trigger retry logic.
3) Compare:
   - Gateway/router logs: request_id -> selected variant.
   - OTel traces: check for experiment_id/variant_id attributes on root span and streaming child spans.
   - Metrics rollups: attributed vs unattributed counts for the same time window.

Initial Triage Notes (Support + Telemetry)
- Hypothesis A: experiment_id/variant_id set on initial request context but lost when stream is handed off to async writer / span context changes.
- Hypothesis B: retry code path creates a new request context without copying experiment assignment fields.
- Hypothesis C: sampling / cardinality protections dropping tags (less likely; customer observes missing even in traces, not just metrics).

What we checked
- Console Results API confirms high “attribution drop rate” for streaming routes only.
- Router debug endpoint (internal) for same stickiness key shows stable assignment (determinism OK).
- Correlated unattributed spans with “streaming retry” logs; strong correlation.

Workaround (shared with customer)
- Temporary: disable client-side retries on mid-stream disconnects OR retry with a new request_id but include an explicit, stable stickiness key header to reduce confusing partial aggregates.
- Not a true fix; attribution still missing on some internal retry flows.

Engineering Escalation
- Owner: Telemetry/Tracing (Caleb Johnson) with Router context plumbing review (Vikram Singh / Jordan Lee as needed).
- Requested: verify request context propagation across streaming + retry, ensure experiment attribution fields are attached to:
  - Root request span
  - Streaming chunk spans (if present)
  - Any retry attempt spans

Resolution
- Fix implemented in github:redwood/pr-28622-fix-attribution-missing-on-streaming-retries.
  - Summary (from PR): ensure experiment dimensions persist across retry paths for streaming requests; copy experiment attribution fields into retried request context and ensure span attributes set before stream writer takes over.
- Rollout:
  - Deployed to staging: 2025-03-03
  - Deployed to prod us-east: 2025-03-05
  - Deployed to prod eu-west: 2025-03-06

Validation
- Post-deploy: attribution drop rate on Acme AI route experiments reduced from ~18–25% (streaming) to <1%.
- Customer confirmed variant headers now present consistently on streaming responses; Console results updated within the expected aggregation window.

Follow-ups
- Add regression test for streaming + retry attribution in ENG-9031 style E2E suite (suggested to Console/Platform).
- Ensure docs call out that variant headers may be omitted on certain error responses, but attribution should still be present in telemetry.

---
Comments / Timeline

2025-02-28 09:12 PT — Rafael Mendes (Reporter)
Customer report from Acme AI (beta). Seeing ~20% unattributed traffic on experiment results, only when streaming enabled. Non-streaming is fine.

2025-02-28 10:03 PT — Caleb Johnson (Assignee)
Ack. Pulling traces + comparing streaming vs non-streaming. Suspect context not being copied to retry attempt spans.

2025-03-01 14:20 PT — Logan Wright
Confirmed correlation with retry path. When stream reconnect/retry occurs, the new attempt span lacks experiment_id/variant_id. Routing decision logs still show variant selection.

2025-03-03 11:47 PT — Caleb Johnson
Fix up in PR: github:redwood/pr-28622-fix-attribution-missing-on-streaming-retries. Adds explicit context copy + ensures span attributes set pre-stream handoff. Staging looks good.

2025-03-05 16:05 PT — Rafael Mendes
Prod us-east rollout completed. Watching attribution drop dashboard; trending down.

2025-03-06 09:30 PT — Rafael Mendes
Customer confirmed. Closing as Resolved; leaving internal follow-up to add E2E test coverage.

source.summary: AcmeBank (Private): audit log export has gaps during peak hours
source.description:
Issue summary:
AcmeBank compliance team observed missing audit log events in their Redwood Private deployment. They ingest our audit log stream into Splunk; some hours show fewer events than expected compared to their internal admin action counts.

Impact:
Low immediate app impact, but compliance reporting is unreliable; customer wants assurance and remediation plan.

Environment:
- Deployment: Redwood Private
- Region: eu-west
- Control plane: 1.14.3
- Sink: HTTP push to customer collector (then Splunk)

Notes:
Customer reports gaps correlate with peak inference traffic. No obvious 5xx errors from exporter.
comments: Aisha Rahman (Support) 2025-02-13: Triage notes: sounds similar to HelioPay ticket SUP-10432. Tagging SRE and Platform.
Ethan Park (SRE) 2025-02-13: Likely same root cause as IR-2025-017 (audit-event-router drop under backpressure). Workaround: increase router buffer + apply 1.14.4 hotfix when ready.
External (AcmeBank) 2025-02-17: Confirmed: gaps occur during 09:00-10:00 CET and 16:00-17:00 CET. Our audit review script shows 12 missing RBAC changes over 2 days.
Connor O'Brien (Eng Platform) 2025-02-18: Hotfix is safe for private tenants. Scheduling rollout with CS.

Aisha Rahman (Support) 2025-02-24: Customer confirmed post-hotfix: no new missing events observed for 72 hours. Closing; tracking longer-term via known-issue page + postmortem for compliance narrative.

source.summary: Orion Defense requesting offline installer bundle for closed network Private deployment
source.content:
Issue summary
Orion Defense cannot allow any outbound internet access from their Kubernetes cluster (closed enclave). They are requesting an offline installer bundle (images + charts) and a checklist documenting any outbound assumptions.

Impact
Blocks Private proof-of-concept until packaging is available for disconnected install.

Environment
On-prem Kubernetes, internal-only DNS/NTP, internal PKI. Public OCSP/CRL not permitted.

Customer request
- Provide container images in a format they can import into internal registry.
- Provide pinned digests + checksums.
- Provide upgrade cadence expectations (quarterly windows).

Comments
2025-02-14 Jordan Blake (reporter): Customer says "no outbound" and "no proxy". Need to make sure nothing phones home.
2025-02-15 Hiro Tanaka (assignee): We can generate a v0 bundle from the current private-installer release. Will include digest list + checksums.
2025-02-18 Priya Natarajan: Please validate install with egress blocked at firewall, not just DNS. Also include note about disabling telemetry.
2025-02-21 Hiro Tanaka: Delivered bundle via secure transfer. Orion confirmed they can import images to internal registry. Added checklist link and scheduled follow-up security review.

Resolution
Provided offline bundle + checklist. No code changes required for POC; documentation updates tracked separately.

Issue summary
Orion Defense cannot allow any outbound internet access from their Kubernetes cluster (closed enclave). They are requesting an offline installer bundle (images + charts) and a checklist documenting any outbound assumptions.

Impact
Blocks Private proof-of-concept until packaging is available for disconnected install.

Environment
On-prem Kubernetes, internal-only DNS/NTP, internal PKI. Public OCSP/CRL not permitted.

Customer request
- Provide container images in a format they can import into internal registry.
- Provide pinned digests + checksums.
- Provide upgrade cadence expectations (quarterly windows).
