source.summary: Tenant routed to secondary variant despite explicit model pin during orchestrator retry surge
source.description: Issue summary: One enterprise customer observed tenant traffic being routed to a secondary (fallback) variant even though their project had an explicit model version pin. Impact: customer-facing production traffic experienced degraded model behavior and increased cost due to falling back to a larger, higher-cost variant. This started during a rolling canary of a separate release when orchestrator retry activity spiked.
source.steps_to_reproduce: 1) Create a tenant-level model pin to redwood/rlhf-3b-v2 with strict affinity; 2) Initiate a rolling canary that toggles health checks for a different model in the same region to induce orchestrator retries; 3) Observe routing decisions in the control plane and edge traces for a burst window; 4) Repeat with increased orchestrator retry rate and note whether pinned affinity holds.
source.observed_behavior: During a control-plane retry surge the tenant's session affinity was dropped and requests were routed to the secondary variant for ~8 minutes. Edge traces show the pin still present in the tenant metadata but the orchestrator returned a fallback routing decision. Requests received mixed variant outputs and embeddings were inconsistent with pinned model behavior.
source.expected_behavior: Tenant-level explicit pins should be honored across transient orchestrator retries; traffic must remain bound to pinned model unless an explicit unpin or capacity-driven fallback policy is triggered by SLOs.
source.logs:
Selected control-plane events (timestamps UTC):
- 2025-02-13T14:21:03Z orchestrator: commit_attempt tenant=acme-42 commit_id=0x7af2 retry=1
- 2025-02-13T14:21:04Z orchestrator: commit_ack tenant=acme-42 commit_id=0x7af2 epoch=124
- 2025-02-13T14:21:05Z orchestrator: commit_attempt tenant=acme-42 commit_id=0x7af2 retry=3
- 2025-02-13T14:21:05Z routing: evaluated_policies tenant=acme-42 pin=redwood/rlhf-3b-v2 grace_window_reset=true decision=fallback->secondary
- 2025-02-13T14:28:12Z edge-proxy: request 200 route=secondary-variant
Relevant trace IDs: trace-4a7b2d, trace-4a7b91. Full logs attached in internal archive (link in comments).
source.investigation_notes: Initial triage by support: verified tenant pin exists and was set before the window. SRE pulled orchestrator commit logs and found that a retry sequence with duplicate commit_nonce values and a race in the grace-window update caused the grace_window_reset flag to be set erroneously. Hypothesis: orchestrator retry path treats a repeated commit attempt that includes a non-zero retry counter as a new commit for epoch reconciliation, which resets the pin's grace timer and allows fallback evaluation. Reproduced in staging by forcing commit_retry > 2 and toggling health probes. Engineering lead (J. Patel) identified a conditional in orchestrator/commit_manager that fails to short-circuit for duplicate commit_id when retry_count > 0 (see related PR).
source.mitigation_steps:
- Short term: Set customer routing policy to strict-pin on the tenant to stop further fallback decisions (applied at 2025-02-13T15:05Z).
- Throttle canary healthcheck toggles to reduce orchestrator retry storms while patch is prepared.
- Coordinate with Customer Success to notify Acme AI and confirm mitigation.
source.resolution_notes: Engineering pushed hotfix v2025.02.14-orch.1 to orchestrator in eu-west on 2025-02-14 04:30 UTC which adjusts commit_manager to ignore duplicate commit_id when retry_count > 0 and preserves existing grace_window unless an explicit unpin event occurs. Post-deploy monitoring shows pin stability restored; no additional fallback routing observed for the tenant in the 72-hour window after patch. A follow-up stabilization release scheduled to fully harden edge-proxy interaction.
comments: 2025-02-13T14:35Z - Support (Ava Chen): Acknowledged issue with customer, requested environment details and time window. Informed customer of P1 escalation.

2025-02-13T15:00Z - SRE (Luis Martinez): Confirmed pin exists at control plane and edge metadata; capturing control-plane logs and initiating deeper trace collection. Added temporary strict-pin policy for tenant.

2025-02-13T18:12Z - Eng (J. Patel): Reproduced in staging by simulating orchestrator retry storm. Found commit_manager race; drafting patch to preserve grace_window on duplicate commit_id. See PR draft in channel #sre-orchestrator.
2025-02-14T05:00Z - Eng (J. Patel): Hotfix deployed to eu-west. Monitoring shows routing decisions honour pins post-deploy. Will schedule canary of fix to other regions.

2025-02-14T09:30Z - Customer Success (Maria Gomez): Notified Acme AI that patch is live and asked for confirmation of observed behavior. Customer reported model outputs normalized and no further fallback traffic.
2025-02-15T10:45Z - Support (Ava Chen): Closing loop with customer; will follow up on postmortem and next steps for multi-region hardening.

source.summary: EU-pinned customer seeing cross-region (US) fallback attempt when EU capacity constrained
source.environment: prod (eu-west primary; observed fallback attempt to us-east)
source.description:
Issue summary:
Nordholm Payments reports that after enabling EU-only region pinning for their Hosted API project, they observed at least one request attempting to fall back to a US region when EU capacity was constrained. They require strict enforcement (no cross-region routing/fallback).

Impact:
- Compliance risk perception for customer (regulated payments).
- Customer paused POC traffic until confirmed fixed.

Customer expectation:
- Requests must be served only within the configured allowed regions (EU-only).
- If EU is unavailable/capacity constrained, the request must be blocked with an explicit residency violation error (not retried in US).

Environment:
- Prod
- Customer config: project pinned to EU-only; primary region: eu-west
- Observed behavior: smart router selected eu-west then attempted us-east fallback

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Configure project residency policy: allowed_regions=["eu-west"], primary_region="eu-west".
2) Send chat completion request during EU capacity pressure.
3) Observe request tracing showing attempted failover to us-east.

Evidence provided:
- Request IDs (customer): rwreq_01HTNQ8Z0YB3P0K4WJ5R4K0V8S, rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG, rwreq_01HTNQ9H2M6ZB5K2W7VQK7X2DP
- Customer noted response header "x-redwood-region: us-east" on one request.

Notes:
- Customer asked for written confirmation of behavior and whether any prompts could have been processed outside EU.
- We should treat this as a product bug until proven otherwise (routing should never attempt cross-region fallback when policy disallows it).
comments:
2025-02-10 09:14 (Hannah Schmitt - Support):
Thanks for flagging. We agree that for EU-only pinning, cross-region fallback should not occur. Can you share 2–3 request IDs and timestamps (UTC) so we can trace routing decisions end-to-end? Also confirm the project slug / org ID used for the traffic.

In parallel I’m pulling our router decision logs for your org to confirm whether this was a true cross-region execution vs. a header/reporting artifact.

2025-02-10 10:02 (Customer - Nordholm Payments):
Sharing request IDs and approximate times (UTC) as requested:
- rwreq_01HTNQ8Z0YB3P0K4WJ5R4K0V8S at ~08:41
- rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG at ~08:52
- rwreq_01HTNQ9H2M6ZB5K2W7VQK7X2DP at ~08:55
We verified our project is configured EU-only in the console. We can’t proceed if any processing can occur in the US.

We saw x-redwood-region: us-east on one response and a spike in latency.

2025-02-10 11:18 (Sean Gallagher - SRE):
Pulled router decision logs for rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG.

Key finding: router evaluated residency policy correctly (EU-only) but still attempted a fallback candidate in us-east after a capacity_exhausted signal from eu-west pool.

Redacted log snippet (prod smart-router):
2025-02-10T08:52:14.219Z level=INFO msg="route_decision" request_id=rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG org_id=org_7pHk... project_id=prj_2nQ... policy_id=rpol_9d3... allowed_regions=["eu-west"] primary_region="eu-west" selected_region="eu-west" fallback_attempted=true fallback_candidate="us-east" fallback_reason="capacity_exhausted" residency_enforcement_mode="warn" decision="ROUTE_PRIMARY_THEN_FALLBACK" router_version="1.18.3"

This should not happen when allowed_regions excludes us-east. Looks like our current enforcement mode for this org/project is still WARN (log-only) rather than BLOCK.

Action: coordinating with Routing (Mina/Ravi) to confirm whether WARN is expected for enterprise customers or if this is a misconfiguration/flag issue.

2025-02-10 11:37 (Hannah Schmitt - Support):
Acknowledged. We’re treating this as a bug/incorrect enforcement state. Immediate mitigation option: we can force strict enforcement for your org so any out-of-region fallback is blocked (and will return a deterministic residency error) while we roll out the permanent routing fix.

Sean—can we flip the org override today? Customer is paused pending confirmation.

2025-02-10 12:05 (Sean Gallagher - SRE):
Mitigation applied:
- Enabled org override flag: residency_enforcement_mode=BLOCK (org_7pHk...)
- Disabled cross-region failover for pinned workloads: cross_region_fallback_enabled=false (scoped to the project)

Expected behavior after flag: router will only select eu-west; if capacity is exhausted/unavailable it will return a policy-block style response (gateway should map to the standardized error once the gateway change is fully rolled out).

Requesting customer to re-test with a small volume and share new request IDs. Also adding a temporary dashboard watch for any routing attempts to us-east for this org.

2025-02-10 13:22 (Customer - Nordholm Payments):
We re-tested. Now we’re seeing failures instead of us-east. That’s acceptable as long as it is explicit/traceable.

One example:
- rwreq_01HTNR1G8E9J0YF4ZP6NQK4A1B

We’d like the failure to clearly state that it’s blocked by residency policy and not a generic 5xx.

2025-02-10 14:01 (Brian Murphy - API Error Contract):
Checked gateway behavior for the request ID provided. Current streaming path is still returning a generic upstream error in some cases depending on where the block triggers.

We have an in-flight standardization so policy blocks return a consistent payload + status code across endpoints (see ADR-022). For now, for this customer we can at least ensure the non-streaming endpoints return the residency violation error, and we can provide a supportable mapping for the streaming case until the gateway PR is fully rolled out.

I’ll sync with Amara on timing; related work is in ENG-4833.

2025-02-11 09:30 (Ravi Menon - Routing):
Root cause: smart-router fallback path did not consult residency constraints when enforcement_mode=WARN (log-only). In WARN, we were emitting the decision context but still allowing the normal fallback candidate selection. That is not acceptable for customers who have explicitly configured allowed_regions (even if we want a staged rollout).

Fix: update router to treat any explicit allowed_regions as a hard constraint for candidate selection (i.e., do not even consider out-of-set regions), independent of enforcement mode. Enforcement mode will control only whether we BLOCK vs return a best-effort in-region retry.

Fix is implemented in PR https://github.com/redwood/redwood/pull/21507 and will be rolled out progressively starting 2025-02-12.

2025-02-12 16:18 (Sean Gallagher - SRE):
PR-21507 deployed to prod (eu-west router fleet first, then global). Confirmed via canary logs that fallback_candidate selection now filters by allowed_regions.

Post-deploy verification (same org):
- Out-of-region candidates are no longer computed.
- When eu-west pool is exhausted, decision is now "BLOCK_RESIDENCY_OR_CAPACITY" and does not attempt us-east.

We can remove the temporary org override after 24h if customer prefers default behavior. Leaving it enabled for now to avoid surprises.

2025-02-13 10:44 (Hannah Schmitt - Support):
Update for Nordholm:
- We identified and fixed the routing bug that allowed a US fallback candidate to be considered despite EU-only configuration.
- The routing fix is now in production.
- Your org also has a temporary strict enforcement override enabled as an added safeguard.

Next step: please run your standard test suite again and confirm you no longer see x-redwood-region: us-east and that any failures are clearly attributable to capacity/residency constraints. If you share any new request IDs, we’ll validate end-to-end.

2025-02-14 08:20 (Customer - Nordholm Payments):
Confirmed. We no longer see any us-east region headers or traces. When EU is under pressure we get an explicit failure (we can work with that). Please keep the strict enforcement setting on for our org going forward.

Thanks for the quick turnaround.

2025-02-14 09:05 (Hannah Schmitt - Support):
Closing as resolved.

Summary:
- Root cause: routing fallback candidate selection did not enforce allowed_regions when enforcement_mode=WARN.
- Mitigation: enabled strict enforcement override (BLOCK) and disabled cross-region fallback for the project.
- Permanent fix: PR-21507 deployed; router now filters fallback candidates by allowed_regions regardless of enforcement mode.

Follow-ups:
- Ensure gateway returns standardized residency violation errors consistently for streaming + non-streaming (tracked in ENG-4833).
- Add an automated regression test covering “EU-only pinned + capacity exhausted => no cross-region attempt” (Routing/SRE to track under ENG-4851).
source.resolution: Resolved via routing fix (PR-21507) preventing out-of-allowed-region fallback candidate selection; customer additionally protected by strict enforcement feature flag (BLOCK) and cross-region fallback disabled at org/project scope. Customer confirmed no further US-region headers/traces.
attachments: Redacted router log snippet included in comments (request_id=rwreq_01HTNQ9B6K2C6X7Z7RZ0C9W3QG)
Customer-provided request IDs and timestamps (see comments)

source.summary: token accounting discrepancy between streaming usage and invoice export
source.description:
Issue summary:
Customer reports a token accounting discrepancy: the Usage page (streaming chat route) shows ~18% more billable tokens than what they compute from request/response logs. Their invoice export CSV matches the higher number.

Impact:
Customer is pausing rollout until they can reconcile token counts for February.

Steps to reproduce (from customer):
1) Call /v1/chat/completions with streaming enabled
2) Sum prompt_tokens + completion_tokens from the final chunk they receive
3) Compare to billable tokens shown in Console > Usage and invoice export

Notes:
- Customer claims some requests show completion_tokens=0 in their logs while they still received text
- They suspect retransmits / reconnects

Requested outcome:
Explain what counts as billable tokens for streaming and whether retries can double-count.
comments: Ava Chen (Support): Looping in runtime + billing. Customer explicitly says: 'token accounting discrepancy' and wants a definitive explanation. Any known behavior with streaming reconnect?

Dev Patel (Eng): Looking at metrics pipeline. Initial guess: client reconnect causes a second stream; we count both if we send tokens twice. Need to confirm whether they are reusing the same request id.

Priya Natarajan (Eng): Found duplicate request_ids from their integration. They retry on HTTP 499 but keep the same external correlation id, different Redwood request id. So their log reconciliation is missing retries.

Ava Chen (Support): Sharing guidance with customer: billable tokens are counted per successful token emitted; retries that receive tokens will be billed. Also sending link to SDK example for idempotency keys.
Dev Patel (Eng): Added console tooltip + docs patch queued: clarify streaming reconnect/retry semantics + recommend idempotency-key header to prevent double work.
source.resolution: Explained that the observed token accounting discrepancy was caused by client-side retry/reconnect behavior producing multiple billed streams; recommended idempotency-key and adjusted customer retry policy. Docs clarification filed and shipped.

source.summary: AuroraMed production surge: multipath fallback + autoscaler deadtime caused session resets and high-tail latency — customer restore plan & post-incident actions
source.description:
Issue summary: On 2024-11-15 03:12 UTC AuroraMed began seeing elevated tail latency and session resets for multi-turn chat flows. Impact: clients experienced truncated streaming responses, duplicate completions for retrying clients, and an increased 5xx rate (service errors) for approx. 43 minutes. Multiple integrations reported out-of-order tokens and session resumption failures.

Timeline (high level):
- 03:10 UTC: first anomaly flagged by synthetic monitors (latency > 99th pct).
- 03:12 UTC: AuroraMed engineering reported customer-facing errors via support channel and opened an escalation.
- 03:15–03:32 UTC: automatic fallback routing generated multipath requests to variant models; autoscaler entered a deadtime window when scaling signals peaked and could not spin new workers fast enough.
- 03:32 UTC: mitigation step applied (temporary routing throttle and manual scale-up of dedicated pool).
- 03:55 UTC: error rate returned to baseline; 04:05 UTC: streaming sessions stabilized.

Observed impact:
- ~1.8% of AuroraMed requests got 5xx responses during window, 3x normal long-tail latency, and an elevated token duplication rate for clients with aggressive retry logic.

Why this is customer-severe: AuroraMed uses real-time clinical chat for triage; truncated responses and retries produce operator workload and potential clinical friction. This ticket captures the incident and the plan to restore customer confidence and remediate root causes.
steps_to_reproduce: Send sustained multi-turn streaming traffic to AuroraMed tenant with session affinity enabled and model pinning to redwood-rlhf-13b.
Trigger a synthetic traffic spike (50% above baseline) with interleaved model variant routing enabled.
Observe autoscaler logs and serving-runtime worker creation times; watch for fallback routing to multiple model variants.
Confirm streaming token reassembly and session resume behavior under heavy fanout.
logs: Grafana: https://metrics.redwood.ai/d/auroramed/latency?from=1699998000000&to=1699999800000 (high 99th tail at 03:12 UTC)
Sentry traces: transaction=/v1/stream (increased errors with 'session-resume-failed' tag)
Autoscaler logs: worker_provision_delay average=22s during spike; binary deadtime window observed at 03:16–03:28 UTC
comments: Support (Marco Ruiz): Notified AuroraMed at 03:18 UTC with initial acknowledgment and synthetic evidence; escalated to SRE.
SRE (Lina Ortega): Observed autoscaler deadtime and multipath fallback amplification; advised manual scale-up and temporary routing throttle at 03:25 UTC.
Customer (AuroraMed Ops - Daniel Chen): Confirmed truncated replies and duplicate completion count ~0.9% of requests; asked for ETA to full restoration.
Engineering (Priya Natarajan): Deployed manual pool increase and pushed a rolling patch to alter fallback backoff window; mitigation validated at 03:52 UTC.
Support (Aisha Kaur): Sent stabilization note and follow-up commitment for post-incident review at 04:10 UTC.
CS (Marta Li): Opened credit request for affected billing window; finance approval pending.
investigation_notes: Hypothesis 1: Autoscaler algorithm incorrectly treated multiple concurrent fallback routes as independent demand signals, creating provisioning jitter and a deadtime where backpressure queued requests.
Hypothesis 2: Serving-runtime kernel selection for quantized variant caused slower worker spin-up (~20–30s) during cold starts; combined with routing fanout this amplified tail latency.
Telemetry shows increased KV cache misses during the window; cache rehydration added to latency.
Confirmed: fallback multiplier configured at 2x for this customer; when combined with client retry loops this generated >2.5x steady-state request fanout.
source.root_cause: Combined effect of aggressive multipath fallback configuration (2x fanout) and autoscaler deadtime during high concurrent cold starts; serving-runtime cold-start latency and KV cache misses amplified tail latency and triggered session resets.
source.resolution_summary: Mitigation applied: immediate manual scale-up of AuroraMed dedicated pool, temporary reduction of fallback multiplier from 2x to 1.1x, increased autoscaler sensitivity (reduced deadtime threshold) and patched serving-runtime to pre-warm quantized kernels for pinned models. Post-mitigation verification performed with synthetic traffic and customer confirmation. Customer services normalized by 03:55 UTC.
follow_up_actions: (P0) Short term: Deploy autoscaler deadtime fix to prod (rollback guard included) — rollout completed 2024-11-16.
(P1) Medium term: Add safe-guards to fallback routing to prevent simultaneous fanout above configured tenant cap — design doc due 2024-11-22.
(P1) Medium term: Implement model pre-warm heuristics for quantized variants when fallback is enabled — owner: serving-runtime team; target 2024-12-10.
(P2) Long term: Add customer-facing dashboard exposing fallback multiplier and estimated fanout to reduce surprises for enterprise tenants — product/console action item.
(P2) Customer ops follow-up: Provide AuroraMed a detailed runbook for client retry tuning and suggested backoff to reduce duplicate completions.
customer_communication: 2024-11-15 03:20 UTC - Acknowledgement: 'We are investigating, will follow up with mitigation steps within 30 mins.'
2024-11-15 04:15 UTC - Stabilization: 'Services restored; manual scale-up and routing throttle applied. We will share a root cause analysis and remediation timeline by 2024-11-20.'
2024-11-20 09:00 UTC - Post-incident report shared with AuroraMed (see Confluence link). Offered billing credit of one day proportional to affected request volume.
source.estimated_billing_impact: Estimated customer request credit: ~0.6% of monthly usage for affected day; finance to confirm exact credit. Credit request filed with billing ticket SUP-111200.
notes_for_sales_cs: Offer a waived onboarding fee for AuroraMed planned model add-ons as gesture of goodwill.
Schedule an architecture review with AuroraMed engineering to walk through fallback, retries, and autoscaler tuning.

title: AuroraMed production surge: multipath fallback + autoscaler deadtime caused session resets and high-tail latency — customer restore plan & post-incident actions
preview:
Issue summary: On 2024-11-15 03:12 UTC AuroraMed began seeing elevated tail latency and session resets for multi-turn chat flows. Impact: clients experienced truncated streaming responses, duplicate completions for retrying clients, and an increased 5xx rate (service errors) for approx. 43 minutes. Multiple integrations reported out-of-order tokens and session resumption failures.

Timeline (high level):
- 03:10 UTC: first anomaly flagged by synthetic monitors (latency > 99th pct).
- 03:12 UTC: AuroraMed engineering reported customer-facing errors via support channel and opened an escalation.
- 03:15–03:32 UTC: automatic fallback routing generated multipath requests to variant models; autoscaler entered a deadtime window when scaling signals peaked and could not spin new workers fast enough.
- 03:32 UTC: mitigation step applied (temporary routing throttle and manual scale-up of dedicated pool).
- 03:55 UTC: error rate returned to baseline; 04:05 UTC: streaming sessions stabilized.

Observed impact:
- ~1.8% of AuroraMed requests got 5xx responses during window, 3x normal long-tail latency, and an elevated token duplication rate for clients with aggressive retry logic.

Why this is customer-severe: AuroraMed uses real-time clinical chat for triage; truncated responses and retries produce operator workload and potential clinical friction. This ticket captures the incident and the plan to restore customer confidence and remediate root causes.
